questions.question: When the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation, it is called _______.
questions.question_raw: When the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation, it is called _______.
questions.explanation:
At equilibrium, the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation.
It may be represented by H2O (l) ⇌ H2O (vap)
The double half arrows indicate that the processes in both the directions are going on simultaneously. The mixture of reactants and products in the equilibrium state is called an equilibrium mixture.

questions.question:
In the dynamic equilibrium stage, one of the following events takes place?
Choose the right one:
questions.question_raw:
In the dynamic equilibrium stage, one of the following events takes place?
Choose the right one:

options.text: Rates of the forward reaction is faster.
The concentrations of the products are increased.
The concentrations of the reactants are decreased.
No change in the concentrations of either of the reactants or products.

questions.explanation: There is no change in the conc of reactant and product. At dynamic equilibrium, reactants are converted to products, and products are converted to reactants at an equal and constant rate. Reactions do not necessarily—and most often do not—end up with equal concentrations. Equilibrium is the state of equal, opposite rates, not equal concentrations.

questions.question: The rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmospheric pressure and 273 K. Both the processes occur simultaneously and at the same rate so that the amount of ice and water remains constant. This process is called _______.
questions.question_raw: The rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmospheric pressure and 273 K. Both the processes occur simultaneously and at the same rate so that the amount of ice and water remains constant. This process is called _______.
questions.explanation: Ice and water kept in a perfectly insulated thermos flask at 273K and the atmospheric pressure are in an equilibrium state and the system shows interesting characteristic features. The mass of ice and water do not change with time and the temperature remains constant. However, the equilibrium is not static. The intense activity can be noticed at the boundary between ice and water. Molecules from the liquid water collide against ice and adhere to it and some molecules of ice escape into the liquid phase. There is no change of mass of ice and water, as the rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmosphere pressure and 273K.

questions.question: During the process of transformation from liquid to vapour, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant. This is called ________.
questions.question_raw: During the process of transformation from liquid to vapour, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant. This is called ________.
questions.explanation:
The number of water molecules from the gaseous state into the liquid state also increases till the equilibrium is attained.
i.e.rate of evaporation = rate of condensation
H2O(l) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex}  H2O (vap)
At equilibrium, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant and is called the equilibrium vapour pressure of water increases with temperature.

questions.question: If we place solid iodine in a closed vessel after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour. When equilibrium is attained, the intensity of colour will be:
questions.question_raw: If we place solid iodine in a closed vessel after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour. When equilibrium is attained, the intensity of colour will be:
questions.explanation: We place solid iodine in a closed vessel, after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour and the intensity of colour increases with time. After a certain time, the intensity of colour becomes constant and at this stage, equilibrium is attained. Hence solid iodine sublimes to give iodine vapour and the iodine vapour condenses to give solid iodine. The equilibrium can be represented as I2 (solid) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} I2 (vapour).

questions.question: The boiling point of water at atmospheric pressure is ________.
questions.question_raw: The boiling point of water at atmospheric pressure is ________.
questions.explanation: Water and water vapour are in equilibrium position at atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar) and at 100°C in a closed vessel. The boiling point of water is 100°C at 1.013 bar pressure. For any pure liquid at one atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar), the temperature at which the liquid and vapours are at equilibrium is called the normal boiling point of the liquid. The boiling point of the liquid depends on the atmospheric pressure.

questions.question: The dynamic nature of chemical equilibrium can be demonstrated in the synthesis of ammonia by Haber's process. Choose the appropriate option given below:
questions.question_raw: The dynamic nature of chemical equilibrium can be demonstrated in the synthesis of ammonia by Haber's process. Choose the appropriate option given below:

options.text: by maintaining high temperature and low pressure

questions.explanation: The use of isotope (deuterium) in the formation of ammonia clearly indicates that chemical reactions reach a state of dynamic equilibrium in which the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal and there is no net change in composition.

questions.question: At a given temperature, equilibrium is possible only in a system which is a:
questions.question_raw: At a given temperature, equilibrium is possible only in a system which is a:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature for physical processes. When equilibrium is attained for a physical process, it is characterized by the constant value of one of its parameters at a given temperature.

questions.question: For liquid $ \rightleftharpoons $ vapour equilibrium, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.question_raw: For liquid {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} vapour equilibrium, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.explanation: The vapour pressure is constant at a given temperature for liquid {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} vapour equilibrium. The equilibrium vapour pressure of a compound at a given temperature is an intrinsic property of the compound, meaning it does not depend on variables like volume, the quantity of the compound, etc.

questions.question: For the dissolution of solids in liquids, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.question_raw: For the dissolution of solids in liquids, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.explanation: It is because at a particular temperature only a certain amount of solute can be dissolved. But it may be increased after increasing the temperature, so the solubility is constant at a given temperature.

questions.question: The concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = $ $\frac{{\left[ C \right]\left[ D \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]\left[ B \right]}}$ Where Kc is called as ________.
questions.question_raw: The concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, {tex}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = {/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left[ C \right]\left[ D \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]\left[ B \right]}}{/tex} Where Kc is called as ________.
questions.explanation:
Let us consider a general reversible reaction:
A + B {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} C + D
where A and B are the reactants, C and D are the products in the balanced chemical equation.
On the basis of experimental studies of many reversible reactions, opposed in 1864 that the concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, Kc = [C] [D] /[A] [B]
Where Kc is the equilibrium constant and the expression on the right side is called the equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question: Equilibrium constant for the reaction, 4NH3(g) + 5O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 4NO(g) + 6H2O (g) is written as:
questions.question_raw: Equilibrium constant for the reaction, 4NH3(g) + 5O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO(g) + 6H2O (g) is written as:
questions.explanation:
Kc is equilibrium constant {tex} = \frac{{{{\left[ {products} \right]}^{stoichiimetry}}}}{{{{[reactant]}^{stoichiimetry}}}}{/tex}    
{tex}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = {\text{ }}{\left[ {{\text{NO}}} \right]^{\text{4}}}{\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right]^{\text{6}}}/{\text{ }}{\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]^{\text{4}}}{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]^{\text{5}}}{/tex}

questions.question_raw: For reactions involving gases, however, it is usually more convenient to express the equilibrium constant in terms of
questions.explanation: Kp = {tex}\frac{\left(\mathbf{P}_{\text { products }}\right)^{\text { sttoichimery }}}{\left(\mathbf{P}_{\text { reactant }}\right)^{\text { stoichimetry }}}{/tex}

questions.question: For reactions in equilibrium involving gases, like N2(g) + 3H2 $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NH3(g), choose one of the following options.
questions.question_raw: For reactions in equilibrium involving gases, like N2(g) + 3H2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3(g), choose one of the following options.
questions.explanation:
In reaction, N2 (g) + 3H2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3 (g)
Here, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 2- (1+3) = 2 - 4 = -2
Therefore, Kp = Kc (RT)-2

questions.question: If the Kc is neither too small nor too large, one of the following will happen:
questions.question_raw: If the Kc is neither too small nor too large, one of the following will happen:

options.text: both reactants and products are at equilibrium

questions.explanation:
A High value of K is a high concentration of products. If Kc > 103, products predominate over reactants.

  • If Kc < 10–3, reactants predominate over products, i.e., if Kc is very small, the reaction proceeds rarely.
  • If Kc is in the range of 10– 3 to 103, appreciable concentrations of both reactants and products are present.

questions.question:
Find Kc for the following reaction, if CO2 pressure is in atmospheres:
CaCO3(s) $\rightarrow$ CaO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 2.1 $\times$ 10-4 (at 1000K).
questions.question_raw:
Find Kc for the following reaction, if CO2 pressure is in atmospheres:
CaCO3(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CaO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 (at 1000K).
questions.explanation:
Kp = Kc (RT)∆n
Here change in no. of moles i.e. ∆n = 1 because it is taken only for gaseous products and reactants by differing reactant from product.
{tex}2.1 \times {10^{ - 4}} = {K_c}{(0.0821 \times 1000)^1}{/tex}
{tex}{K_c} = \frac{{2.1 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{82.1}} = 2.57 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 2.6 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex}

questions.question: A particular ratio of product to reactant helps in predicting the direction in which a given reaction will proceed at any stage is called ________.
questions.question_raw: A particular ratio of product to reactant helps in predicting the direction in which a given reaction will proceed at any stage is called ________.
questions.explanation:
The reaction quotient Q. For a general reaction: aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD
Qc = [C]c [D]d / [A]a [B]b
Then, If Qc > Kc,
the reaction will proceed in the direction of reactants (reverse reaction).
If Qc < Kc, the reaction will proceed in the direction of the products (forward reaction).
If Qc = Kc, the reaction mixture is already at equilibrium.

questions.question: The value of Kc for a reaction is directly related to the thermodynamics of the reaction and in particular with:
questions.question_raw: The value of Kc for a reaction is directly related to the thermodynamics of the reaction and in particular with:
questions.explanation:
A mathematical expression of this thermodynamic view of equilibrium can be described by the following equation: {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 + RT ln Q
where G0 is standard Gibbs energy.
At equilibrium, when {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0 and Q = Kc,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 + RT ln K = 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 = – RT lnK (7.22)
lnK = – {tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 / RT
Taking antilog of both sides, we get,
K = e– {tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 / RT

questions.question: In order to decide what course the reaction adopts and make a qualitative prediction about the effect of a change in conditions on equilibrium we use:
questions.question_raw: In order to decide what course the reaction adopts and make a qualitative prediction about the effect of a change in conditions on equilibrium we use:
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle- It states that a change in any of the factors that determine the equilibrium conditions of a system will cause the system to change in such a manner so as to reduce or to counteract the effect of the change.

questions.question: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction for the conversion of reactants to products by:
questions.question_raw: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction for the conversion of reactants to products by:

options.text: lowering the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture
increasing the activation energy for the forward reaction
making available a new low energy pathway

questions.explanation: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction by making available a new low energy pathway for the conversion of reactants to products. It increases the rate of forward and reverse reactions that pass through the same transition state and does not affect equilibrium. Catalyst lowers the activation energy for the forward and reverse reactions by exactly the same amount. Catalyst does not affect the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture. It does not appear in the balanced chemical equation or in the equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question: A bare proton, H+ is very reactive and cannot exist freely in aqueous solutions. Thus, it bonds to the oxygen atom of a solvent water molecule to give:
questions.question_raw: A bare proton, H+ is very reactive and cannot exist freely in aqueous solutions. Thus, it bonds to the oxygen atom of a solvent water molecule to give:
questions.explanation:
Hydrogen ion by itself is a bare proton with a very small size (~10–15 m radius) and intense electric field, binds itself with the water molecule at one of the two available lone pairs on it giving H3O+. This species has been detected in many compounds (e.g., H3O+Cl – ) in the solid-state.
In aqueous solution, the hydronium ion is further hydrated to give species like {tex}{H_5}O_2^ + {/tex}.

questions.question: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali is called ________.
questions.explanation: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali are called Buffer Solutions. Buffer solutions of known pH can be prepared from the knowledge of pKa of the acid or pK b of base and by controlling the ratio of the salt and acid or salt and base. A mixture of acetic acid and sodium acetate acts as a buffer solution around pH 4.75 and a mixture of ammonium chloride and ammonium hydroxide acts as a buffer around pH 9.25.

questions.question: PCl5, PCl3 and Cl2 are at equilibrium at 500 K in a closed container and their concentrations are 0.8 $\times$ 10-3 mol L-1, 1.2 $\times$ 10-3 mol L-1 and 1.2 $\times$ 10-3 mol L-1 respectively. The value of Kc for the reaction PCl5(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) will be:
questions.question_raw: PCl5, PCl3 and Cl2 are at equilibrium at 500 K in a closed container and their concentrations are 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1, 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 and 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 respectively. The value of Kc for the reaction PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) will be:
questions.explanation: Kc =  {tex}\frac{{\left[ {PC{l_3}} \right]\left[ {C{l_2}} \right]}}{{\left[ {PC{l_5}} \right]}}{/tex} =  1.2 x 10-3 x 1.2 x 10-3 / 0.8 x 10-3 = 1.8 x 10-3 L mol-1

questions.question:
When hydrochloric acid is added to cobalt nitrate solution at room temperature, the following reaction takes place and the reaction mixture becomes blue. On cooling the mixture it becomes pink. On the basis of this information mark the correct answer.
$\mathop {{{\left[ {{\text{Co }}\left( {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right){\text{6}}} \right]}^{{\text{3}} + }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }(aq)}\limits_{pink}$ $ \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{{\left[ {{\text{CoC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right]}^{{\text{2}} - }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 6}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}(l)}\limits_{Blue} $

When hydrochloric acid is added to cobalt nitrate solution at room temperature, the following reaction takes place and the reaction mixture becomes blue. On cooling the mixture it becomes pink. On the basis of this information mark the correct answer.
{tex}\mathop {{{\left[ {{\text{Co }}\left( {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right){\text{6}}} \right]}^{{\text{3}} + }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }(aq)}\limits_{pink}{/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{{\left[ {{\text{CoC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right]}^{{\text{2}} - }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 6}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}(l)}\limits_{Blue} {/tex}

questions.question: The ionisation constant of an acid, Ka, is the measure of strength of an acid. The Ka values of acetic acid, hypochlorous acid and formic acid are 1.74 $\times$ 10-5, 3.0 $\times$ 10-8 and 1.8 $\times$ 10-4 respectively. Which of the following orders of pH of 0.1 mol dm-3 solutions of these acids is correct?
questions.question_raw: The ionisation constant of an acid, Ka, is the measure of strength of an acid. The Ka values of acetic acid, hypochlorous acid and formic acid are 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 and 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 respectively. Which of the following orders of pH of 0.1 mol dm-3 solutions of these acids is correct?

options.text: formic acid > acetic acid > hypochlorous acid
hypochlorous acid > acetic acid > formic acid
formic acid > hypochlorous acid > acetic acid
acetic acid > hypochlorous acid > formic acid

questions.explanation:
Here, Ka for Formic Acid (HCOOH) =  1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4  , Acetic Acid (CH3COOH) = 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 and  Hypochlorous Acid (HCIO) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–8
At a given temperature T, Ka is a measure of the strength of the acid HX i.e., larger the value of Ka , the stronger is the acid. Ka is a dimensionless quantity with the understanding that the standard state concentration of all species is 1M.
Ka = [H+][X-] / [HX]
{tex}{[{H_3}{O^ + }]\; = \;\sqrt {{K_a}.C} }{/tex}
{tex}{pH = \; - \log \;[{H_3}{O^ + }]}{/tex}
larger the value of Ka larger will be H3O+,hence lower will be pH

questions.question: What will be the value of pH of 00.1 mol dm-3 CH3COOH (Ka = 1.74 $\times$ 10-5)?

questions.explanation:
Acetic acid is a weak acid with Ka = 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and in this case cweak acid >>> K0,
that is the equation to use is: [H+] = (Ka.Cweakacid)1/2   = (1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01)1/2 = 4.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
pH = -log [H+] = - log (4.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) = - [log 4.3 + (-4)log 10] = -[0.633-4] = 3.367

questions.question:
The ionisation of hydrochloric in water is given below:
${\text{HCl }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }}\left( {\text{l}} \right)$ $ \rightleftharpoons $ $\begin{array}{l}H_3O^+\:+\;Cl^-\\\end{array}$
Label two conjugate acid-base pairs respectively in the ionization.
questions.question_raw:
The ionisation of hydrochloric in water is given below:
{tex}{\text{HCl }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }}\left( {\text{l}} \right){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}H_3O^+\:+\;Cl^-\\\end{array}{/tex}
Label two conjugate acid-base pairs respectively in the ionization.
questions.explanation:
A species formed by receiving a proton from a base is known as conjugate acid and Conjugate base is a species formed by the removal of proton from an acid.
In this case, Cl​​​​​-  is formed by donating a proton to water molecule hence it is a conjugate base while protonated water (H3O+)becomes conjugate acid.

questions.question: BF3 does not have a proton but still acts as an acid and reacts with NH3. Choose the correct option.
questions.question_raw: BF3 does not have a proton but still acts as an acid and reacts with NH3. Choose the correct option.

options.text: BF3 is a Brönsted acid and coordinate bond is formed.
BF3 is a Brönsted base and coordinate bond is formed.
BF3 acts as Lewis acid and coordinate bond is formed.
BF3 is a Lewis base and coordinate bond is formed.

questions.explanation:
BF3 is an electron-deficient compound. Hence, it acts as Lewis acid.
NH3 has a lone pair of electrons. Hence, it acts as a Lewis base.
A coordinate bond is formed between the two, as the nitrogen atom of ammonia acts as an electron donor, while B of BF3 acts as an electron acceptor.
H3N: {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} BF3

questions.question: 0.3g of Ca(OH)2 is dissolved in water to give 500 mL of solution. The pH of the solution is ________.
questions.question_raw: 0.3g of Ca(OH)2 is dissolved in water to give 500 mL of solution. The pH of the solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
Molarity={tex}\;\frac{mass\;of\;Ca(OH)_2\times1000}{Molar\;mass\;\times Volume}=\frac{0.3\times1000}{56\times500}={/tex} 0.0107M
The chemical reaction involved are, CaO + H2O = Ca(OH)2
Here, Ca(OH)2 = Ca2+ + 2OH-
Concentration of OH- = 2[OH-] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0107 = 0.0214M
Now,
Kw = [H3O+][OH-] {tex}{ \Rightarrow [{H_3}{O^ + }] = \frac{{{K_w}}}{{[O{H^ - }]}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{0.0214}}}{/tex} = 46.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14
Therefore,  pH = -log[H3O+] = - log [46.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14] = 12.33

questions.question: Calculate the hydrogen ion concentration in the human blood whose pH is 7.38.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the hydrogen ion concentration in the human blood whose pH is 7.38.
questions.explanation:
pH = -log[H+]
7.38 = -log[H+]
{tex}\log\lbrack H^+\rbrack\;=-7.38=\overset-8.62{/tex}
Taking antilog on both sides, we get [H+] = 4.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 M

questions.question: The ionization constant of acetic acid is $1.74 \times {10^{ - 5}}$. Calculate the degree of dissociation of acetic acid in its 0.05 M solution. Calculate the concentration of acetate ion in the solution and its pH.
questions.question_raw: The ionization constant of acetic acid is {tex}1.74 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}. Calculate the degree of dissociation of acetic acid in its 0.05 M solution. Calculate the concentration of acetate ion in the solution and its pH.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_a}C}{/tex}

Here, C=0.05M,

 {tex}K_a=1.74\times10^{-5}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = {\left\{ {\frac{{1.74 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{0.05}}} \right\}^{1/2}}{/tex} {tex}\alpha = {\{ 1.74 \times {10^{ - 5}}/0.05\} ^{1/2}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = [34.8 \times {10^{ - 5}}]{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = (3.48)1/2 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = 1.86 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
CH3COOH = CH3COO- + H+
Concentration of {tex}C{H_3}CO{O^ - } = C\alpha {/tex}
{tex} = 0.05 \times 1.86 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 0.093 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 0.00093M{/tex}
Since [CH3COO-] = [H+]
[H+] = 0.00093 = {tex}0.093 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
{tex}pH = - \log [{H^ + }] = - \log [0.093 \times {10^{ - 2}}]{/tex}
pH = -[log(0.093) + (-2)log10]
{tex}pH = 3.03{/tex}

questions.question: It has been found that the pH of a 0.01M solution of an organic acid is 4.15. Calculate the ionization constant of the acid, the concentration of the anion, and its pKa.
questions.question_raw: It has been found that the pH of a 0.01M solution of an organic acid is 4.15. Calculate the ionization constant of the acid,  the concentration of the anion, and its pKa.
questions.explanation:
pH = -log[H+]
4.15 = -log[H+]
[H+] = 7.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = [A-]
Ka = [H+][A-][HA]
Ka = 5.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
pKa = - log [5.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7] = 6.3001

questions.question: The Ksp for Cr(OH)3 is 1.6 $\times$ 10-30. The molar solubility of this compound in water is:
questions.question_raw: The Ksp for Cr(OH)3 is 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-30. The molar solubility of this compound in water is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\begin{array}{l}Cr(OH)_3=Cr^{3+}+3\;OH^-\\\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;s\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;3s\end{array}{/tex}
Ksp = (s)(3s)3 = 27s4
Now, 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-30 = 27s4
{tex}{ \Rightarrow {s^4} = \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 30}}}}{{27}}}{/tex}
{tex}{ \Rightarrow s = \sqrt[4]{{\frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 30}}}}{{27}}}}}{/tex}

questions.question: The pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12. The value of the solubility product (Ksp) of Ba(OH)2 is:
questions.question_raw: The pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12. The value of the solubility product (Ksp) of Ba(OH)2 is:
questions.explanation:
pH + pOH = 14
pOH = 14 - 12 = 2
pOH = -log[OH-]
OH- = 10 - pOH = 10 - 2 .......... (1)
At equilibrium, Ba(OH)2 = Ba+2 + 2OH-
let [OH-] = x , therefore, From above equation; 2[OH-] = 2x  = 10 - 2 (from equation 1)
Therefore {tex}x = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{2} = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
Ksp = [Ba+2] [OH-]2 = [0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10- 2[10-2]2 = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10- 6 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7

questions.question: The solubility of ${\text{C}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{3}}}{\left( {{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}$ in water is y moles/litre. Its solubility product is:
questions.question_raw: The solubility of {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{3}}}{\left( {{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{/tex} in water is y moles/litre. Its solubility product is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {C{a_3}}\limits_y {\left( {P{O_4}} \right)_2} = \mathop {3C{a^{2 + }}}\limits_{3y} + \mathop {2PO_4^{3 - }}\limits_{2y} {/tex}     
Solubility product = {tex}K_{sp}=\;\lbrack Ca^{{}_{2+}}\rbrack^3\lbrack PO_4^{3-}\rbrack^2\;=(3y)^3\;(2y)^2\;=108y^5{/tex}

questions.question: 0.023 g of sodium metal is reacted with 100 cm3 of water. The pH of the resulting solution is ______.
questions.question_raw: 0.023 g of sodium metal is reacted with 100 cm3 of water. The pH of the resulting solution is ______.
questions.explanation:
Moles of NaOH formed  = 0.023/23 =0.001
Concentration of NaOH = {tex}\frac{\left(0.001\right)\times\left(1000\right)}{\left(100\right)}=0.01{/tex}M
pOH=-log[OH-] = - log [ 0.01] = -log [10-2 ] = - (-2) log 10 = 2
We know that, pH + pOH =14
pH =14-2 = 12

questions.question: pH value of which one of the following is NOT equal to one?
questions.question_raw: pH value of which one of the following is NOT equal to one?
questions.explanation:
{tex}CH_3COOH\;+H_2O\rightleftharpoons\;CH_3COO^-+H_3O^+{/tex}
Since the dissociation constant of acetic acid is so small, we can assume that the concentration of acetic acid is still approximately 0.1 M even after dissociating.
Also [CH3COO-] = [H3O+]
Therefore,
{tex}K_a=\frac{\left[CH_3COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{l}K_a=\frac{\left[CH_3COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}=\frac{\left[H_3O^+\right]^2}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}\\\Rightarrow1.7\times10^{-5}\;=\frac{\left[H_3O^+\right]^2}{0.1}\\\Rightarrow\left[H_3O^+\right]=\sqrt{1.7\times10^{-6}}\\\Rightarrow\left[H_3O^+\right]=1.32\times10^{-3}\\now,\;pH=\;-\log\left[H_3O^+\right]=\;-\log1.32\times10^{-3}\;=2.88\end{array}{/tex}

questions.question: A vessel at 1000 K contains CO2 with a pressure of 0.5 atm. Some of the CO2 is converted into CO on the addition of graphite. If the total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm, the value of K is ________.
questions.question_raw: A vessel at 1000 K contains CO2 with a pressure of 0.5 atm. Some of the CO2 is converted into CO on the addition of graphite. If the total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm, the value of K is ________.
questions.explanation:
CO2 + C {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO
t = 0, 0.5, 0
t = t, 0.5-x, 2x
Ptotal = 0.5 - x + 2x = 0.8
i.e. x = 0.3 atm
i.e. {tex}K = \frac{{{{\left( {{P_{co}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{P_{co2}}}} = \frac{{0.6 \times 0.6}}{{0.2}} = 1.8atm{/tex}

questions.question: Conjugate acid of a weak base is always stronger. What will be the decreasing order of basic strength of the following conjugate bases? OH-, RO-, CH3COO-, Cl-
questions.question_raw: Conjugate acid of a weak base is always stronger. What will be the decreasing order of basic strength of the following conjugate bases? OH-, RO-, CH3COO-, Cl-
questions.explanation:
conjugate acids of given bases are H2O, ROH, CH3COO-, Cl-
their acidic strength in the order
Cl- > CH3COO- > H2O > ROH
basic strength in the order {tex}{\text{R}}{{\text{O}}^ - }{/tex} > {tex}{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }{/tex} > {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CO}}{{\text{O}}^ - }{/tex} > {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }{/tex}

questions.question: pH of a solution of a strong acid is 5.0. What will be the pH of the solution obtained after diluting the given solution a 100 times?
questions.question_raw: pH of a solution of a strong acid is 5.0. What will be the pH of the solution obtained after diluting the given solution a 100 times?
questions.explanation:
pH=5 means the solution is acidic. On dilution, it will remain acidic. However, acidic character decreases so much that it is almost neutral (pH=5 means [H+] = 10-5M). After dilution, [H+] = {tex}\frac{10^{-5}}{100}=10^{-7}M{/tex} ; [H+] from H2O cannot be neglected.
Hence total [H+] = 10-7 + 10-7 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH=-log(2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 ) = 7-0.3010=6.699

questions.question:
Following data is given for the reaction: ${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ CaO }}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$
${\Delta _f}HV|CaO(s)] = - - 635.1\;kJ1mo{l^{ - 1}}$
${\Delta _f}HV[C{O_2}(g)] = - - 393.5\;kJ\;mo{l^{ - 1}}$
${\Delta _f}HV[CaC{O_3}(s)] = - - 1206.9\;kJmo{l^{ - 1}}$
Predict the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant of the above
Reaction (Hint: Apply Le-Chatelier’s principle)
questions.question_raw:
Following data is given for the reaction: {tex}{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ CaO }}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV|CaO(s)] = - - 635.1\;kJ1mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV[C{O_2}(g)] = - - 393.5\;kJ\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV[CaC{O_3}(s)] = - - 1206.9\;kJmo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Predict the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant of the above
Reaction (Hint: Apply Le-Chatelier’s principle)

options.text: increase in temperature reaction will stop.
increase in temperature reaction will proceed in reverse direction
increase in temperature reaction will proceed in forward direction
increase in temperature reaction will attain equilibrium

questions.explanation:
{tex}{\Delta _{rxn}}H = (\Delta {H_{product}} - \Delta {H_{reac\tan t}}){/tex}
= -393.5 - 635.1 + 1206.9 = +ve
This is endothermic reaction, which is favoured at high temperature.

questions.question: Does the number of moles of reaction products increase, decrease or remain same when each of the following equilibria is subjected to a decrease in pressure by increasing the volume? ${\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{5}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$ $ \rightleftharpoons $ ${\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$
questions.question_raw: Does the number of moles of reaction products increase, decrease or remain same when each of the following equilibria is subjected to a decrease in pressure by increasing the volume? {tex}{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{5}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} {tex}{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}
questions.explanation:
1. Pressure will increase in the forwarded reaction and the number of moles of the products increase.
  2. Pressure will increase in the backward reaction and the number of moles of the products decrease.
  3. The change in pressure will have no effect on the equilibrium constant and there will be no change in the no. of moles.

questions.question: One of the reaction that takes place in producing steel from iron ore is the reduction of iron (II) oxide by carbon monoxide to give iron metal and CO2. FeO(s) + CO (g) $\rightarrow$ Fe (s) + CO2 (g) Kp = 0.265 at 1050 K. What are the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO2 at 1050 K if the initial partial pressures are: Pco = 1.4 atm and $P_{CO_2}$ = 0.80 atm?
questions.question_raw: One of the reaction that takes place in producing steel from iron ore is the reduction of iron (II) oxide by carbon monoxide to give iron metal and CO2. FeO(s) + CO (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe (s) + CO2 (g) Kp = 0.265 at 1050 K. What are the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO2 at 1050 K if the initial partial pressures are: Pco = 1.4 atm and {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 0.80 atm?
questions.explanation:
For the given reaction; {tex}\mathop {FeO + CO(g)}\limits_{1.4atm} = \mathop {Fe(s) + C{O_2}(g)}\limits_{0.80atm} {/tex}
{tex}Q_p=\frac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{CO}}=\frac{0.80}{1.4}=\;0.571{/tex}
It is given that Kp = 0.265
Since QP > Kp
The reaction will proceed in the backward reaction.
Therefore, we can say that the pressure of CO will increase while the pressure of CO2 will decrease.
Now, let the increase in pressure of CO = decrease in pressure of CO2 be P.
Then we can write
{tex}{{K_p} = \frac{{{P_{C{O_2}}}}}{{{P_{CO}}}} = \frac{{0.80 - P}}{{1.4 + P}}}{/tex}
{tex}{ \Rightarrow 0.265 = \frac{{0.80 - P}}{{1.4 + P}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.265 (1.4 + P) = 0.80 - P
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.371 + 0.265P = 0.80 - P
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1.265P = 0.429 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 0.339atm
Therefore, equilibrium partial pressure of CO2 = 0.80 - 0.339= 0.461 atm
Also, equilibrium partial pressure  of CO = 1.4 + 0.339 = 1.739 atm

questions.question: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 $\times$ 10-4. Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. The concentrations of all species present i.e. H3O+,F- and HF in the solution, and its pH respectively are.
questions.question_raw: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. The concentrations of all species present i.e. H3O+,F- and HF in the solution, and its pH respectively are.
questions.explanation:
HF {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + F-
Initial Concentration 1 0 0
Equilibrium concentration C(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) C {tex}\alpha{/tex} C {tex}\alpha{/tex}
Now, {tex}{{K_a}\; = \frac{{\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]\left[ {{F^ - }} \right]}}{{\left[ {HF} \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {\;C\alpha } \right]\left[ {\;C\alpha } \right]}}{{\left[ {C(1 - \alpha )\;} \right]}} = \frac{{\;C{\alpha ^2}}}{{(1 - \alpha )\;}} \approx C{\alpha ^2}}{/tex}  (Since {tex}\alpha{/tex} is less (1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) can be written as 1
{tex}{ \Rightarrow \alpha = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_a}\;}}{C}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3.2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{0.02}}} = 12.65 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}{/tex}
[H3O+] = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} =0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1265 = 2.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
[F-]  = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1265 = 2.53 {tex}\times{/tex}10-3
[HF]= C(1-{tex}\alpha{/tex}) = 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} (1-0.1265) = 17.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
Now,
pH= - log [H3O+] = - log (2.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = 3 - 0.4031 = 2.5969

questions.question: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of 0.002 M KOH solution.
questions.question_raw: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of 0.002 M KOH solution.
questions.explanation:
KOH {tex}\to{/tex} K+ + OH- (As KOH undergoes complete ionization)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [OH-] = [KOH] = 0.02
Now, Kw = [H+] [OH-]{tex}{ \Rightarrow \left[ {{H^ + }} \right] = \frac{{\;{K_w}\;}}{{\left[ {O{H^ - }} \right]}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{0.02}} = \;5 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH of KOH = - log [5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12] = 12 - log 5 = 12 - 0.699 {tex}\approx{/tex} 11.31

{tex}CaC{O_3}(s) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g){/tex}

{tex}{K_c} = \frac{{\left[ {{C_a}O(s)} \right]\left[ {C{O_2}(g)} \right]}}{{\left[ {{C_a}C{o_3}(s)} \right]}}{/tex}, Where [CaO(s)] = 1, [CaCO3(s) = 1
Therefore, Kc = [CO2(g)]

questions.question: Hydrogen molecule (H2) can be dissociated into hydrogen atoms (H). Which one of the following changes will not increase the number of atoms present at equilibrium?
questions.question_raw: Hydrogen molecule (H2) can be dissociated into hydrogen atoms (H). Which one of the following changes will not increase the number of atoms present at equilibrium?

options.text: increasing the volume of the container

questions.explanation:
The equilibrium reaction for dissociation of H2 into H atoms is as follows:
H2 {tex}⇌{/tex}H+H.
Since a number of atoms on the reactant side and product side are the same, therefore, change in pressure has no effect on the position of equilibrium.

questions.question:
What is the expression for Kc for the reaction?
 $N_2O(g)\;+\;\frac12O_2(g)\:\rightleftharpoons\;2NO(g)$
questions.question_raw:
What is the expression for Kc for the reaction?
 {tex}N_2O(g)\;+\;\frac12O_2(g)\:\rightleftharpoons\;2NO(g){/tex}
questions.explanation:
{tex}{{N}_{2}}O(g)\ +\ \frac{1}{2}{{O}_{2}}(g)\;\rightleftharpoons \ 2NO(g){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2{{N}_{2}}O(g)\ +\ {{O}_{2}}(g)\;\rightleftharpoons \ 4NO(g){/tex}

questions.question: A catalyst will increase the rate of a chemical reaction by:
questions.question_raw: A catalyst will increase the rate of a chemical reaction by:

options.text: shifting the equilibrium to the right
shifting the equilibrium to the left

questions.explanation:
Catalysts increase the rate of the reaction without being used up. They do this by lowering the activation energy needed. With a catalyst, more collisions result in a reaction, so the rate of reaction increases.
"A catalyst provides an alternative route for the reaction with lower activation energy."

questions.question_raw:
In a closed system;
{tex}A(s)\:\rightleftharpoons2B(g)+3C(g){/tex}
If the partial pressure of C is doubled, then the partial pressure of B will be:
questions.explanation:
For the closed system A(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2B(g)+3C(g)
Kp = ( PB )2(PC)3.......(1)
Thus, Kp is constant, if we double the partial pressure of C, P'C =2PC
Kp= (P'B)2(P'C)3 = (P'B)2(2PC)3 = 8(P'B)2(PC)3 .....(2)
From (1) and( 2), we get
8( P'B)2 = (PB)2
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {{{{P}'}}_{B}} \right)=\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\left( {{P}_{B}} \right){/tex}

questions.question: What effect does a catalyst have on the equilibrium position of a reaction?
questions.question_raw: What effect does a catalyst have on the equilibrium position of a reaction?

options.text: a catalyst favours the formation of reactants
a catalyst favours the formation of products
a catalyst may favour reactants or product formation, depending upon the direction in which the reaction is written
a catalyst does not change the equilibrium position of a reaction

questions.explanation: Catalyst provides a low activation energy path to complete the reaction. This affects reactions in forward and backward direction equally. Thus, Catalysts have no effect on the position of equilibrium.

questions.question: A chemist dissolves an excess of BaSO4 in pure water at 25°C if its $K_{sp}=1\times10^{-10}$. what is the concentration of barium in the water ?
questions.question_raw: A chemist dissolves an excess of BaSO4 in pure water at 25°C if its {tex}K_{sp}=1\times10^{-10}{/tex}. what is the concentration of barium in the water ?
questions.explanation:
BaSO4 → Ba2+ + SO42-
Ksp =  [Ba+2] [SO4-2]
Ksp = x2= 10-10
x2 = 10-10
x= 10 -5

questions.question: If in a mixture where Q = k is combined, then what happens?
questions.question_raw: If in a mixture where Q = k is combined, then what happens?

options.text: nothing appears to happen, but forward and reverse are continuing at the same rate

questions.explanation:
Q=K
The reaction is already at equilibrium. The concentrations won't change since the rates of the forward and backward reactions are equal.

questions.question: The incorrect match in the following is
questions.question_raw: The incorrect match in the following is
questions.explanation:
The incorrect match is {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° < 0, K < 1
For an ideal gas {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° = - RT ln K.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In {tex}K=-\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{R T}{/tex} and {tex}K=e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T}{/tex}
The above equation is helpful in predicting the spontaneity of the reaction. e.g.

  1. If {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}<0,-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T{/tex} = + ve and {tex}e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T}{/tex} > 1 and hence, K > 1. It means that the reaction occurs spontaneously in the forward direction or products predominate over reactants.
  2. If {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}>0 ;-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T{/tex} = - ve and {tex}e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T}{/tex} <1 and hence, K < 1. It means that the reaction is non-spontaneous in the forward direction (i.e. product side) but spontaneous in reverse direction (i.e. reactants predominate over products or the reaction occurs rarely).
  3. When K = 1, then {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° - 0. This situation generally occurs at equilibrium

questions.question: In which one of the following equilibria, Kp $\ne$ KC?
questions.question_raw: In which one of the following equilibria, Kp {tex}\ne{/tex} KC?
questions.explanation:
Consider the following equilibria reactions

  1. {tex}2 \mathrm{C}(s)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=n_{\text {product }}-n_{\text {reactant }}=2-(1)=1{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g} \neq 0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{So}, K_{p} \neq K_{c}{/tex}
  2. {tex}2 \mathrm{HI}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{I}_{2}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=n_{\text {product }}-n_{\text {reactant }}=2-2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=0 \Rightarrow \text { So, } K_{p}=K_{c}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{SO}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}(g)+\mathrm{SO}_{3}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=n_{\text {product }}-n_{\text {reactant }}=2-2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=0 \Rightarrow \text { So, } K_{p}=K_{c}{/tex}
  4. {tex}2 \mathrm{NO}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=n_{\text {product }}-n_{\text {reactant }}=2-2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{So}, K_{p}=K_{c}{/tex}

questions.question: For the reaction, 2SO2 (g) + O2(g) $\rightarrow$2SO3 (g), $\Delta$H = - 57.2 kJ mol-1 and Kc = 1.7 $\times$ 1016. Which of the following statement is incorrect?
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, 2SO2 (g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}2SO3 (g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = - 57.2 kJ mol-1 and Kc = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1016. Which of the following statement is incorrect?

options.text: The equilibrium constant decreases as the temperature increases.
The addition of inert gas at constant volume will not affect the equilibrium constant.
The equilibrium constant is large suggestive of reaction going to completion and so no catalyst is required.
The equilibrium will shift in a forward direction as the pressure increases.

questions.explanation:
The explanation of the given statements are as follows:

  1. For the given equilibrium, AH is negative, so the equilibrium constant will decrease with increase in temperature and the equilibrium will shift in the backward direction.
  2. When inert gas is added at constant volume and constant temperature, an equilibrium remains undisturbed. 
  3. For the equilibrium,
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 2 - (2 + 1) = -1, i.e. (-ve)
    So, increase in pressure will shift the equilibrium in the forward direction.
  4. The reaction takes place in the presence of a catalyst which is V2O5(s) in the contact process or NO(g) in the chamber process.

questions.question:
For the following reactions, equilibrium constants are given : S(s) + O2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ SO2(g); K1 =1052
2S(s) + 3O2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2SO3(g); K2 =10129. The equilibrium constant for the reaction, 2SO(s) + O2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2SO3(g) is
questions.question_raw:
For the following reactions, equilibrium constants are given : S(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} SO2(g); K1 =1052
2S(s) + 3O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2SO3(g); K2 =10129. The equilibrium constant for the reaction, 2SO(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2SO3(g) is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{S}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}, K_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{SO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{S}+\mathrm{O}_{2}, K_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{K_{1}}{/tex}
or, {tex}2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{S}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2}, K_{1}^{\prime \prime}=\left(K_{1}^{\prime}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{K_{1}^{2}}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{S}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}, K_{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
Now, [(i) + (ii)] gives
{tex}2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}, K_{3}{/tex}
The value of equilibrium constant,
{tex}K_{3}=K_{2} \times K_{1}^{\prime \prime}=K_{2} \times \frac{1}{K_{1}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=10^{129} \times \frac{1}{\left(10^{52}\right)^{2}}=10^{129-104}=10^{25}{/tex}

questions.question: In a chemical reaction, A + 2B $\stackrel{K}{\rightleftharpoons} $ 2C + D, the initial concentration of B was 1.5 times of the concentration of A, but the equilibrium concentrations of A and B were found to be equal. The equilibrium constant (K) for the aforesaid chemical reaction is
questions.question_raw: In a chemical reaction, A + 2B {tex}\stackrel{K}{\rightleftharpoons} {/tex} 2C + D, the initial concentration of B was 1.5 times of the concentration of A, but the equilibrium concentrations of A and B were found to be equal. The equilibrium constant (K) for the aforesaid chemical reaction is
questions.explanation:
For the given chemical reaction,
 [x = degree of dissociation]
Given, at equilibrium.
[A] = [B]
a0 - x = 1.5a0 - 2x
x = 0.5a0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [A] = a0 - x = a0 - 0.5a0 = 0.5a0
[B] = 1.5a0 - 2x = 1.5a0 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5a0 = 0.5a0
[C] - 2x = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5a0= a0
[D] = x = 0.5a0
Now, {tex}K=\frac{[C]^{2}[D]}{[A][B]^{2}}{/tex}
Now, substituting the values in above equation, we get
{tex}K=\frac{\left(a_{0}\right)^{2} \times\left(0.5 a_{0}\right)}{\left(0.5 a_{0}\right) \times\left(0.5 a_{0}\right)}=4{/tex}

questions.question:
Two solids dissociate as follows:
$A(s) \rightleftharpoons B(g)+\mathrm{C}(g) ; K_{p_{1}}=x \ \mathrm{atm}^{2}$
$D(s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}(g)+E(g) ; K_{p_{2}}=y \ \mathrm{atm}^{2}$
The total pressure when both the solids dissociate simultaneously is
questions.question_raw:
Two solids dissociate as follows:
{tex}A(s) \rightleftharpoons B(g)+\mathrm{C}(g) ; K_{p_{1}}=x \ \mathrm{atm}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}D(s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}(g)+E(g) ; K_{p_{2}}=y \ \mathrm{atm}^{2}{/tex}
The total pressure when both the solids dissociate simultaneously is
questions.explanation:
The equilibrium reaction for the dissociation of two solids is given as:
{tex}2(\sqrt{x+y}) \text { atm }{/tex}
{tex}\underset{Atequilibrium}\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;A(s)\rightleftharpoons\underset{P_1}{B(g)}+\underset{P_1+P_2}{C(g)}{/tex}
{tex} \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ K_{p_{1}}=x=p_{B} \cdot p_{C}=p_{1}\left(p_{1}+p_{2}\right) \\ Similarly, D(s) \rightleftharpoons C(g)+E(g){/tex} ...(i)
At equilibrium {tex}p_{1}+p_{2} \cdot p_{2}{/tex}
{tex}K_{p_{2}}=y= p_C \cdot p_E |=\left(p_{1}+p_{2}\right) p_{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
On adding Eq. (i) and (ii), we get.
KP1 + Kp2 = x + y = p1 (p1 + P2) + p2(p1 + p2)
= (p1 + p2)2
or {tex}\sqrt{x+y}=p_{1}+p_{2}{/tex} ...(iii)
Now, total pressure is given as
PT = PB + PC + PE
= P1 + (P1 + P2)+ P2
= 2(p1 + p2)
On substituting the value of p1 + p2 from Eq. (iii) to Eq. (iv), we get
{tex}p_{T}=2 \sqrt{x+y}{/tex}

questions.question:
Consider the reaction,
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g)$
The equilibrium constant of the above reaction is Kp. If pure ammonia is left to dissociate, the partial pressure of ammonia at equilibrium is given by (Assume that $p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}<<p_{\text {total }}$ at equilibrium)
questions.question_raw:
Consider the reaction,
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g){/tex}
The equilibrium constant of the above reaction is Kp. If pure ammonia is left to dissociate, the partial pressure of ammonia at equilibrium is given by (Assume that {tex}p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}<<p_{\text {total }}{/tex} at equilibrium)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g){/tex}
At equilibrium: pNi = P, pHl = 3P, 2NH 3<g) Pnh 3 = 2-P
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p(total) = {tex}p_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+p_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}+p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}\simeq p_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+p_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} p(total) >> {tex}p_{NH_3}{/tex}]
= p + 3p = 4p
Now, {tex}K_{p}=\frac{p^{2} \mathrm{NH}_{3}}{p_{\mathrm{N}_{2}} \times p_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}^{3}}=\frac{p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{2}}{p \times(3 p)^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{2}}{27 \times p^{4}}=\frac{p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{2}}{27 \times\left(\frac{P}{4}\right)^{4}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} p = 4p]
{tex}K_{p}=\frac{p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{2} \times 4^{4}}{3^{2} \times 3 \times P^{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{2}=\frac{3^{2} \times 3 \times P^{4} \times K_{p}}{4^{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad p_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}=\frac{3 \times 3^{1 / 2} \times P^{2} \times K_{p}^{1 / 2}}{4^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3^{3 / 2} K_{p}^{1 / 2} P^{2}}{16}{/tex}

questions.question: 5.1 g NH4SH is introduced in 3.0 L evacuated flask at 327°C. 30% of the solid NH4SH decomposed to NH3 and H2S as gases. The K of the reaction at 327° C is (R = 0.082 atm mol-1 K-1 , molar mass of S = 32g mol-1, molar mass of N = 14 g mol-1)
questions.question_raw: 5.1 g NH4SH is introduced in 3.0 L evacuated flask at 327°C. 30% of the solid NH4SH decomposed to NH3 and H2S as gases. The K of the reaction at 327° C is (R = 0.082 atm mol-1 K-1 , molar mass of S = 32g mol-1, molar mass of N = 14 g mol-1)
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of NH4SH = 18 + 33 = 51 g mol-1
Number of moles of NH4SH introduced in the vessel {tex}=\frac{\text { Weight }}{\text { Molar mass }}=\frac{5 \cdot 1}{51}=0.1 \mathrm{mol}{/tex}

{tex}K_{C}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{HS}(s)\right]}=\frac{0.01 \times 0.01}{1}=10^{-4}\left(\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K_{p}=K_{C}(R T)^{\Delta_{n_g}}{/tex}
[where, {tex}\Delta n_{g}=\Sigma n_{\text {product }}-\Sigma n_{\text {reactant }}{/tex}] = 2 - 0 = 2
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{p}=K_{C}(R T)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=10^{-4} \times[0.082 \times(273+327)]^{2} \mathrm{atm}^{2}{/tex}
= 0.242 atm2

questions.question:
The values of $ \frac{K_{p}}{K_{C}}$ for the following reactions at 300 K are, respectively (At 300 K, RT= 24.62dm3 atm mol-1)
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(g)$
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)$
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g)$
questions.question_raw:
The values of {tex} \frac{K_{p}}{K_{C}}{/tex} for the following reactions at 300 K are, respectively (At 300 K, RT= 24.62dm3 atm mol-1)
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(g){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g){/tex}

options.text: 1, 24.62 dm3 atm mol-1, 606.0 dm6 atm2 mol2
24.62 dm3 atm mol-1, 606.0 dm6 atm-2 mol2, 1.65 $\times$ 10-3 dm-6 atm-2 mol2
1, 4.1 $\times$ 102 dm-3 atm-1 mol, 606 dm6 atm2 mol-2
1, 24.62 dm3 atm mol-1, 1.65 $\times$ 10-3 dm-6 atm-2 mol2

questions.explanation:
We know that, the relationship between K and K of a chemical equilibrium state (reaction) is
{tex}K_{p}=K_{C}(R T)^{\Delta n_{g}} \Rightarrow \frac{K_{p}}{K_{C}}=(R T)^{\Delta n_{g}}{/tex}
where, {tex}\Delta n_{g}=\Sigma n_{\text {products }}-\Sigma n_{\text {Reactants }}{/tex}

  1. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (RT)2 - (1 + 1) = (RT)0 = 1
  2. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (RT)2 - 1= RT = 24.62dm3 atm mol-1
  3. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (RT)2 - (3 + 1) = (RT)-2
    {tex}=\frac{1}{\left(24.62 \mathrm{dm}^{3} \mathrm{atm} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
    = 1.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 dm-6 atm-2 mol2

questions.question:
Consider the following reversible chemical reactions,
$A_{2}(g)+B_{2}(g) \stackrel{K_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 A B(g)$ ...(i)
$6 A B(g) \stackrel{K_{2}}{\rightleftharpoons} 3 A_{2}(g)+3 B_{2}(g)$ ...(ii)
The relation between K1 and K2 is
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following reversible chemical reactions,
{tex}A_{2}(g)+B_{2}(g) \stackrel{K_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 A B(g){/tex} ...(i)
{tex}6 A B(g) \stackrel{K_{2}}{\rightleftharpoons} 3 A_{2}(g)+3 B_{2}(g){/tex} ...(ii)
The relation between K1 and K2 is
questions.explanation:
1. {tex}A_{2}(g)+B_{2}(g) \stackrel{K_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 A B(g){/tex} ; {tex}K_{1}=\frac{[A B]^{2}}{\left[A_{2}\right]\left[B_{2}\right]}{/tex}
  2. {tex}6 A B(g) \stackrel{K_{2}}{\rightleftharpoons} 3 A_{2}(g)+3 B_{2}(g){/tex} ; {tex}K_{2}=\frac{\left[A_{2}\right]^{3}\left[B_{2}\right]^{3}}{[A B]^6}=\frac{1}{\left(\frac{[A B]^{2}}{\left[A_{2}\right]\left[B_{2}\right]}\right)^{3}}=\frac{1}{K_{1}^{3}}{/tex},
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K2 = K1-3

questions.question: An aqueous solution contains 0.10 M H2S and 0.20 M HCl. If the equilibrium constants for the formation of H S- from H2S is 1.0 $\times$ 10-7 and that of S2- from HS- ions is 1.2 $\times$ 10-13 then the concentration of S2- ions in aqueous solution is:
questions.question_raw: An aqueous solution contains 0.10 M H2S and 0.20 M HCl. If the equilibrium constants for the formation of H S- from H2S is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 and that of S2- from HS- ions is 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 then the concentration of S2- ions in aqueous solution is:
questions.explanation:
Given [H2S] = 0.10 M
[HCI] = 0.20 M So, [H+] = 0.02 M
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HS^-}, K_{1}=1.0 \times 10^{-7}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{HS} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{S}^{2-}, K_{2}=1.2 \times 10^{-13}{/tex}
It means for,
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{S}^{2-}{/tex}
{tex}K=K_{1} \times K_{2}=10 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-13}{/tex}
{tex}=1.2 \times 10^{-20}{/tex}
Now, [S2-] = {tex}\frac{K \times\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} [according to the final equation]
{tex}=\frac{1.2 \times 10^{-20} \times 0.1 \mathrm{M}}{(0.2 \mathrm{M})^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.2 \times 10^{-20} \times 1 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{M}}{4 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}}{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20M

questions.question: The equilibrium constant at 298 K for a reaction, $A+B \rightleftharpoons C+D$ is 100. If the initial concentrations of all the four species were 1 M each, then equilibrium concentration of D (in mol L-1) will be
questions.question_raw: The equilibrium constant at 298 K for a reaction, {tex}A+B \rightleftharpoons C+D{/tex} is 100. If the initial concentrations of all the four species were 1 M each, then equilibrium concentration of D (in mol L-1) will be
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{[C][D]}{[A][B]}=\frac{(1+x)(1+x)}{(1-x)(1-x)}=\frac{(1+x)^{2}}{(1-x)^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}100=\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)^{2} \text { or } 10=\frac{1+x}{1-x}{/tex}
or 10 -10x = 1 + x
10 - 1 = x + 10x
9 = 11x
{tex}x=\frac{9}{11}=0.818{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [D] = 1 + x = 1 + 0.818 = 1.818

questions.question: The standard Gibbs energy change at 300 K for the reaction, $2 A \rightleftharpoons B+C$ is 2494. 2 J. At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is $[A]=\frac{1}{2},[B]=2 \text { and }[C]=\frac{1}{2}$. The reaction proceeds in the (R = 8.314 JK/mol, e = 2.718)
questions.question_raw: The standard Gibbs energy change at 300 K for the reaction, {tex}2 A \rightleftharpoons B+C{/tex} is 2494. 2 J. At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is {tex}[A]=\frac{1}{2},[B]=2 \text { and }[C]=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}. The reaction proceeds in the (R = 8.314 JK/mol, e = 2.718)

options.text: forward direction because Q > K c

questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=2494.2 \mathrm{J}{/tex}
{tex}Q=\frac{[B][C]}{[A]^{2}}=\frac{2 \times \frac{1}{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=4{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} we know, {tex}\Delta G=\Delta G^{\circ}+R T{/tex} In Q
= 2494.2 + 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex}300 In 4
= 28747.27 J(+ve value)
Also, we have {tex}\Delta G=R T \ln \frac{Q}{K}{/tex}
If {tex}\Delta G{/tex} is positive, Q > K
Therefore, reaction shifts in reverse direction.

questions.question: For the reaction, SO2(g) + $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons$ SO3(g) if Kp = Kc(RT)x where, the symbols have usual meaning, then the value of x is (assuming ideality)
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, SO2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons{/tex} SO3(g) if Kp = Kc(RT)x where, the symbols have usual meaning, then the value of x is (assuming ideality)
questions.explanation:
For the given reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = nP - nR
where, nP = number of moles of products
nR = number of moles of reactants {tex}K_{p}=K_{c}(R T)^{\Delta n_{g}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta n_{g}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The species present in solution when CO2 is dissolved in water are
questions.question_raw: The species present in solution when CO2 is dissolved in water are
questions.explanation:
When CO2 is dissolved in water, following equilibria are established:
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}
Therefore, in solution, all of the above mentioned species exist.

questions.question:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ .Which is a correct statement if N2 is added at equilibrium condition?
 The equilibrium will shift to forward direction because according to Ilnd law of thermodynamics, the entropy must increase in the direction of spontaneous reaction.
 The condition for equilibrium is G(N2) + 3G(H2) = 2G(NH3) where, G is Gibbs free energy per mole of the gaseous species measured at that partial pressure. The condition of equilibrium is unaffected by the use of catalyst, which increases the rate of both the forward and backward reactions to the same extent.
 The catalyst will increase the rate of forward reaction by $\alpha$ and that of backward reaction by $\beta$.
 Catalyst will not alter the rate of either of the reaction.
questions.question_raw:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex} .Which is a correct statement if N2 is added at equilibrium condition?

  1. The equilibrium will shift to forward direction because according to Ilnd law of thermodynamics, the entropy must increase in the direction of spontaneous reaction.
  2. The condition for equilibrium is G(N2) + 3G(H2) = 2G(NH3) where, G is Gibbs free energy per mole of the gaseous species measured at that partial pressure. The condition of equilibrium is unaffected by the use of catalyst, which increases the rate of both the forward and backward reactions to the same extent.
  3. The catalyst will increase the rate of forward reaction by {tex}\alpha{/tex} and that of backward reaction by {tex}\beta{/tex}.
  4. Catalyst will not alter the rate of either of the reaction.
questions.explanation:
At equilibrium, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0
G(reactants) = G(products)
G(N2) + 3G(H2) = 2G(NH3)
A catalyst does not affect either equilibrium composition or equilibrium constant, it just increases rate of both forward and backward reaction but by the same factor.

questions.question: Consider the following equilibrium in a closed container $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)$. At a fixed temperature, the volume of the reaction container is halved. For this change, which of the following statements hold true regarding the equilibrium constant (Kp) and degree of dissociation ($\alpha$)?
questions.question_raw: Consider the following equilibrium in a closed container {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g){/tex}.  At a fixed temperature, the volume of the reaction container is halved. For this change, which of the following statements hold true regarding the equilibrium constant (Kp) and degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex})?

options.text: Kp does not change but $\alpha$ changes
Kp changes but $\alpha$ does not change

questions.explanation:
At constant temperature Kp or Kc remains constant. For the equilibria:
N2O4(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g)
{tex}\triangle n{/tex} = 1
Since, temperature is constant so Kc or Kp will remain constant. Further, since volume is halved, the pressure will be doubled so {tex}\alpha{/tex} will decrease so as to maintain the constancy of Kc or Kp.
From Le-Chatelier's principle reaction will move backwards.

questions.question: At constant temperature, the equilibrium constant (Kp) for the decomposition reaction, $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, is expressed by $K_{p}=\frac{4 x^{2} p}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}$, where, p = pressure, x = extent of decomposition. questions.question_raw: At constant temperature, the equilibrium constant (Kp) for the decomposition reaction, {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}{/tex}, is expressed by {tex}K_{p}=\frac{4 x^{2} p}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}, where, p = pressure, x = extent of decomposition. Which one of the following statement is true?

options.text: Kp increases with increase of x
Kp remains constant with change in p and x
Kp increases with decrease of x
Kp increases with increase of p

questions.explanation: {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}{/tex}, {tex}K_{p}=\frac{4 x^{2} p}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}, Kp is function of temperature only, does not change with either p or x

questions.question: When two reactants, A and B are mixed to give products, C and D, the reaction quotient, (Q) at the initial stages of the reaction:
questions.question_raw: When two reactants, A and B are mixed to give products, C and D, the reaction quotient, (Q) at the initial stages of the reaction:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left.A+B \rightleftharpoons C+D, Q=\frac{[C][D]}{[A][B]}\right]{/tex}
As time passes, amount of products ‘C’ and 'D’ increases, hence Q increases.

questions.question: For the reversible reaction, $\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g)$ at 500° C, the value of Kp is 1.44 $\times$10-5 when partial pressure is measured in atmosphere. The corresponding value of Kc with concentration in mol/L is

The corresponding value of Kc with concentration in mol/L is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g){/tex} {tex}\Delta n=-2{/tex}
{tex}K_{p}=K_{c}(R T)^{\Delta n}{/tex}
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{K_{p}}{(R T)^{\Delta n}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.44 \times 10^{-5}}{(0.082 \times 773)^{-2}}{/tex}

questions.question:
For the reaction,
 $\mathrm{CO}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2}(g)$ at a given temperature, the equilibrium amount of CO2 (g) can be increased by

 {tex}\mathrm{CO}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2}(g){/tex} at a given temperature, the equilibrium amount of CO2 (g) can be increased by

options.text: Decreasing the volume of the container

CO(g) + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CO2(g) + H2(g)
KC = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CO}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]}{[\mathrm{CO}]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex}
A catalyst simply helps in attaining the equilibrium earlier. Addition of inert gas has no effect on a reaction because in it, {tex}\Delta n{/tex} = 0.

On increasing the amount of CO, KC should decrease but it is constant at constant temperature, so for maintaining the constant value of KC, the amount of CO2 increases.
