questions.question: The molar solubitlity of Cd(OH)2 is 1.84 $\times$ 10-5 m in water. The expected solubility of Cd(OH)2 in a buffer solution of pH = 12 is
questions.question_raw: The molar solubitlity of Cd(OH)2 is 1.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 m in water. The expected solubility of Cd(OH)2 in a buffer solution of pH = 12 is
questions.explanation:
Solubility of Cd(OH)2 (s) = 1.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
Given, pH = 12 [for Cd(OH)2 in buffer solution]
So, pOH = 2 ({tex}\because{/tex} pH + pOH = pAw)
12 + pOH = 14
pOH = 14 - 12 = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 10-2 in buffer solution.
For reaction {tex}\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cd}_{s}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=(S)(2 S)^{2}=4 S^{3}=4\left(1.84 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{3}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=24.9 \times 10^{-15}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\right]=\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\right]=\frac{24.9 \times 10^{-15}}{\left(10^{-2}\right)^{2}}=24.9 \times 10^{-15} \times 10^{+4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ 24.9 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{M}{/tex}
[Cd2+] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2.49 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M
The expected solubility of Cd(OH)2 in a buffer solution of pH = 12 is 2.49 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M.

questions.question: What is the molar solubility of Al(OH)3 in 0.2 M NaOH solution? Given the, solubility product of Al(OH)3 = 2.4 $\times$ 10-24
questions.question_raw: What is the molar solubility of Al(OH)3 in 0.2 M NaOH solution? Given the, solubility product of Al(OH)3 = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24
questions.explanation:
{tex}A B \rightleftharpoons A^{+}+B^{-}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[A^{+}\right]\left[B^{-}\right]{/tex}

{tex}\mathrm{NaOH}\longrightarrow\underset{0.2}{\mathrm{Na}^+}+\underset{0.2}{\mathrm{OH}^-}{/tex}
Ksp of Al (OH)3 = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 (Given)
Ksp =[AI3+][OH-]3
2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 = [S'][3S + 0.2]3 [{tex}\because{/tex} 0.2 >> S]
2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 = [S] [0.008]
[S] = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22

questions.question: The pH of a 0.02 M NH4Cl solution will be [Given Kb(NH4OH) = 10-5 and log2 = 0.301]
questions.question_raw: The pH of a 0.02 M NH4Cl solution will be [Given Kb(NH4OH) = 10-5 and log2 = 0.301]
questions.explanation:
Given, Kb(NH4OH) = 10-5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pKb = - logKb = - log(10-5) = 5
C = concentration of salt solution = 0.02 M
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M
Now, pH = {tex}7-\frac{1}{2}\left(p K_{b}+\log C\right){/tex}
On substituting the given values in above equation, we get
{tex}=7-\frac{1}{2}\left[5+\log \left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=7-\frac{1}{2}[5+\log 2-2]{/tex}
{tex}=7-\frac{1}{2}[5+0.301-2]{/tex} = 7 - 1.65 = 5.35

questions.question:
Consider the following statements.
 The pH of a mixture containing 400 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 and 400 mL of 0.1 M NaOH will be approximately 1.3.
 Ionic product of water is temperature-dependent.
 A monobasic acid with Ka = 10-5 has a pH = 5. The degree of dissociation of this acid is 50%.
 The Le-Chatelier’s principle is not applicable to the common ion effect.
 The correct statements are
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following statements.

  1. The pH of a mixture containing 400 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 and 400 mL of 0.1 M NaOH will be approximately 1.3.
  2. Ionic product of water is temperature-dependent.
  3. A monobasic acid with Ka = 10-5 has a pH = 5. The degree of dissociation of this acid is 50%.
  4. The Le-Chatelier’s principle is not applicable to the common ion effect.
    The correct statements are
questions.explanation:
The explanation of given statements are as follows:
In statement (I), millimoles of H+ = 400 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 80
Millimoles of OH- = 400 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 40 (Limiting reagent)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Millimoles of H+ left = 80 - 40 = 40
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{40}{400+400}=\frac{40}{800} M=\frac{1}{20} M{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left(\frac{1}{20}\right){/tex}
= -logl + log2 + log10
= -0 + 0.301 + 1
= 1.30
In a statement (II), the ionic product of H2O is temperature-dependent.
Kw = [H+][OH-] {tex}\approx{/tex} 10-14(mol/L)2 at 25°C
With the increase in temperature, dissociation of H2O units into H+ and OH- ions will also increase. As a result, the value of the ionic product, [H+] {tex}\times{/tex} [OH-] will be increased, e.g.

Temperature Kw(mol/L2)
5°C 0.186 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14
25°C 1.008 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14
45°C 4.074 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14

Hence, the option (b) is correct.
In the statement (III), for a weak monobasic acid HA
{tex}\mathbf{H} A \rightleftharpoons \mathbf{H}^{\oplus}+A^{\ominus}{/tex}
(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) CM {tex}\alpha{/tex}CM {tex}\alpha{/tex}CM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH of the solution is 5, i.e.
[H+] = 10-5 M = {tex}\alpha{/tex}C
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K_{a}=\frac{\alpha C \times \alpha C}{(1-\alpha) C}=\frac{10^{-5} \times \alpha}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 10^{-5}=\frac{10^{-5} \times \alpha}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=0.5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha{/tex}% = 50
In the statement (IV), Le-Chatelier’s principle is applicable to the common ion effect. Because, in the presence of common ion (given) by a strong electrolyte (say, Na+ {tex}\overline A{/tex}), the product of the concentration terms in RHS increases. For the weaker electrolyte, HA (say) the equilibrium shifts to the LHS,
{tex}\mathbf{H} A \rightleftharpoons \mathbf{H}^{\oplus}+A^{\ominus}{/tex}.
As a result dissociation of IL4 gets suppressed. Hence, the option is incorrect.

questions.question: If solubility product of Zr3(PO4)4 is denoted by Ksp and its molar solubility is denoted by S , then which of the following relation between S and Ksp is correct?
questions.question_raw: If solubility product of Zr3(PO4)4 is denoted by Ksp and its molar solubility is denoted by S , then which of the following relation between S and Ksp is correct?
questions.explanation:
For, Zr3(PO4)4,
{tex}{\mathrm{Zr}}_3{\left({\mathrm{PO}}_4\right)}_4(s) \rightleftharpoons \underset{3SM}{3\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}}(aq)+\underset{4SM}{4\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}(aq)}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}\right]^{3}\left[\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}\right]^{4}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{ap}}=(3 S)^{3}(4 S)^{4}=6912 \ S^{7} \text { or } S=\left(\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{6912}\right)^{\frac{1}{7}}{/tex}
Thus, the relation between molar solubility(5) and solubility product (Ksp) will be
{tex}S=\left(\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{6912}\right)^{1 / 7}{/tex}

questions.question: If Ksp of Ag2CO3 is 8 $\times$ 10-12, the molar solubility of Ag2CO3 in 0.1 M AgNO3 is
questions.question_raw: If Ksp of Ag2CO3 is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12, the molar solubility of Ag2CO3 in 0.1 M AgNO3 is
questions.explanation:
Let the solubility of Ag2CO3 is S. Now, 0.1 M of AgNO3 is added to this solution after which let the solubility of Ag2CO3
becomes S'.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ag+ ] = S + 0.1 and [{tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}] = S'
Ksp = (S + 0.1)2 (S') ...(i)
Given, Ksp = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
{tex}\because{/tex} Ksp is very small, we neglect S' against S in Eq. (i)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp  = (0.1)2 S'
or 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 = 0.01 S'
or S' =  8 {tex}\times{/tex}​​​​​​​ 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex}​​​​​​​ 10 -10 M
Thus, molar solubility of Ag2CO3 in 0.1 M
AgNO3 is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 M

questions.question: 20 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 solution is added to 30 mL of 0.2 M NH4OH solution. The pH of the resultant mixture is [pK b of NH4OH = 4.7]
questions.question_raw: 20 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 solution is added to 30 mL of 0.2 M NH4OH solution. The pH of the resultant mixture is [pK b of NH4OH = 4.7]
questions.explanation:
The reaction takes place when H2SO4 is added to NH4OH is as follows:


So, the resulting solution is a basic buffer
[NH4OH+ (NH4)2S04]
According to the Henderson’s equation,
{tex}\mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{p} K_{b}+\log \frac{\left[\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}=4.7+\log \frac{2}{2}{/tex}
= 4.7 + 0.3010
= 5.001 {tex}\approx{/tex} 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 5 = 9

questions.question: An aqueous solution contains an unknown concentration of Ba2+. When 50 mL of a 1 M solution of Na2SO4 is added, BaSO4 just begins to precipitate. The final volume is 500 mL. The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1 $\times$ 10-10. What is the original concentration of Ba2+?
questions.question_raw: An aqueous solution contains an unknown concentration of Ba2+. When 50 mL of a 1 M solution of Na2SO4 is added, BaSO4 just begins to precipitate. The final volume is 500 mL. The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10. What is the original concentration of Ba2+?
questions.explanation:
Its given that the final volume is 500 mL and this final volume was arrived when 50 mL of 1 M a2SO4 was added to unknown Ba2+ solution.
So, we can interpret the volume of unknown Ba2+ solution as 450 mL i.e
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {450mL}\limits_{B{a^{2 + }}} }\limits_{Solution} + \mathop {\mathop {50mL}\limits_{N{a_2}S{O_4}} }\limits_{Solution} \to \mathop {\mathop {500mL}\limits_{BaS{O_4}\;} }\limits_{Solution} {/tex}
From this we can calculate the concentration of S04“ ion in the
solution via
M1V1 = M2V2
{tex}1 \times 50=M_{2} \times 500{/tex}
(as 1M Na2SO4 is taken into consideration)
{tex}M_{2}=\frac{1}{10}=0.1 \mathrm{M}{/tex}
Now for just precipitation,
Ionic product = Solubility product (Ksp)
i.e. {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]=K_{\mathrm{sp}} \text { of } \mathrm{BaSO}_{4}{/tex}
Given Ksp of BaSO4 {tex}=1 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
So, {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right][0.1]=1 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
or {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right]=1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{M}{/tex}
Remember This is the concentration of Ba2+ ions in final solution. Hence, for calculating the Ba2+] in original solution.
we have to use
M1V1 = M2V2
as {tex}M_{1} \times 450=10^{-9} \times 500{/tex}
So, {tex}M_{1}=1.1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{M}{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following are Lewis acids?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following are Lewis acids?
questions.explanation:
The compound given are
PH3 - Octet complete although P has vacant 3d-orbital but does not have the tendency to accept lone pair in it. Hence, it cannot be considered as Lewis acid.
BCl3 -Incomplete octet with following orbital picture.

Hence, vacant p-orbital of B can accept one lone pair thus it can be considered as Lewis acid.
AlCl3 - Similar condition is visible in AlCl3 as well i.e.
Al (Valence orbital only) =

Hence this compound can also be considered as Lewis acid.
SiCl4 - Although this compound does not have incomplete octet but it shows the tendency to accept lone pair of electrons in its vacant d-orbital. This tendency of SiCl4 is visible in following reaction.

Thus, as the condition of a proper Lewis acid is more well defined in BCl3 and AlCl3.

questions.question: Which of the following salts is the most basic in aqueous solution?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following salts is the most basic in aqueous solution?
questions.explanation:
Among the given salts FeCl3 is acidic in nature i.e., have an acidic solution as it is the salt of a weak base and strong acid.
Al(CN)3 and Pb(CH3COO)2 are the salts of a weak acid and weak base.
CH3COOK is the salt of a strong base and weak acid.
Hence, the solution of CH3COOK will be most basic because of the following reaction.
{tex}CH_3COOK\;+\;H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons\underset{(Weak\;acid)}{CH_3COOH\;}+\underset{(Strong\;base)}{KOH}{/tex}

questions.question: pKa of a weak acid (HA) and pKb of a weak base (BOH) are 3.2 and 3.4, respectively. The pH of their salt (AB) solution is
questions.question_raw: pKa of a weak acid (HA) and pKb of a weak base (BOH) are 3.2 and 3.4, respectively. The pH of their salt (AB) solution is
questions.explanation:
For a salt of weak acid and weak base,
pH = {tex}7+\frac{1}{2} p K_{a}-\frac{1}{2} p K_{b}{/tex}
Given, pKa (HA) = 3.2, pKa (BOH) = 3.4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = {tex}7+\frac{1}{2}(3.2)-\frac{1}{2}(3.4){/tex}
= 7 + 1.6 - 1.7 = 6.9

questions.question: How many litres of water must be added to 1 L of an aqueous solution of HCl with a pH of 1 to create an aqueous solution with pH of 2?
questions.question_raw: How many litres of water must be added to 1 L of an aqueous solution of HCl with a pH of 1 to create an aqueous solution with pH of 2?
questions.explanation:
pH = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-1 = 0.1 M
pH = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-2 = 0.01 M
For dilution of HCl, M1V1 = M2V2
0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} V2
V2 = 10 L
Volume of water to be added = 10 -1 = 9 L

questions.question: The solubility product constant (Ksp) of salts of types MX, MX2 and M3X at temperature T are 4.0 $\times$ 10-8, 3.2 $\times$ 10-14 and 2.7 $\times$ 10-15, respectively. Solubilities (mol dm-3) of the salts at temperature T are in the order
questions.question_raw: The solubility product constant (Ksp) of salts of types MX, MX2 and M3X at temperature T are 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8, 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 and 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15, respectively. Solubilities (mol dm-3) of the salts at temperature T are in the order
questions.explanation:
MX {tex}\longrightarrow{ }_s^{M_s^{+}}+\underset{s}{X^{-}}{/tex} (Where s is the solubility)
Then Ksp = s2 or s = {tex}\sqrt{K_{s p}}{/tex}
Similarly for MX2 {tex}\longrightarrow M_s^{2+}+\underset{2 s}{2 X^{-}}{/tex}
Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} (2s)2 = 4s3 or s = {tex}\left[\frac{K_{s p}}{4}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
and for M3X {tex}\longrightarrow \underset{3 s}{3 M^{+}+\underset{s}{X^{-3}}}{/tex}
Ksp = (3s)3 {tex}\times{/tex} s = 27s4 or s = {tex}\left[\frac{K_{s p}}{27}\right]^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
From the given values of Ksp for MX, MX2 and M3X, we can find the solubilities of those salts at temperature, T.
Solubility of MX = {tex}\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
Solubility of MX2 = {tex}\left[\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-14}}{4}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{32}{4} \times 10^{-15}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
= {tex}\left[8 \times 10^{-15}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} or 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
Solubility of M3X = {tex}\left[\frac{2.7 \times 10^{-15}}{27}\right]^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\left[10^{-16}\right]^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} or 10-4
Thus, the solubilities are in the order MX > M3X > MX2 i. e. the correct answer is (d).

questions.question: 2.5 mL of $\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{M}$ weak monoacidic base (Kb = 1 $\times$ 10-12 at 25°C) is titrated with $\frac{2}{15} \mathrm{M}$ HCl in water at 25°C. The concentration of H+ at equivalence point is (Kw = 1 $\times$ 10-14 at 25°C)
questions.question_raw: 2.5 mL of {tex}\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{M}{/tex} weak monoacidic base (Kb = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 at 25°C) is titrated with {tex}\frac{2}{15} \mathrm{M}{/tex} HCl in water at 25°C. The concentration of H+ at equivalence point is (Kw = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 at 25°C)
questions.explanation:
Let the weak manoacidic base be BOH, then the reaction that occurs during titration is BOH + HCl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} BCl + H2O
Equilibrium: {tex}\underset{C(1-h)}{B^{+}}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \underset{C . h}{B O H}+\underset{C . h}{\mathrm{H}^{+}}{/tex}
Using the normality equation, {tex}\begin{aligned} & N_1 V_1= \\ & \text { (acid) } \end{aligned}{/tex}{tex}\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{N}_2 V_2 \\ & \text { (base) } \end{aligned}{/tex}
Substituting various given values, we get
{tex}\frac{2}{15} \times V_1=2.5 \times \frac{2}{5}{/tex} r V1 = {tex}2.5 \times \frac{2}{5} \times \frac{15}{2}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 705 mL
Then the concentration of BCl in resulting solution is given by
[BCl] = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{15} \times 2.5}{10}=\frac{2}{10}{/tex} or 0.1 M
Since Kh = {tex}\frac{K_w}{K_b}{/tex} {tex}\therefore K_h=\frac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{1 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 10-2
Thus Kh = {tex}\frac{0.1 h^2}{(1-h)}{/tex} or 10-2 = {tex}\frac{0.1 h^2}{(1-h)}{/tex}
or 10-2 - 10-2 h = 0.1 h2 or 0.1 h2 + 10-2 h - 10-2 = 0
(Solving this quadratic equation for h, we get)
h = {tex}\frac{-10^{-2} \pm \sqrt{\left(10^{-2}\right)^2+4 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2}}}{2 \times 0.1}{/tex}
{tex}\left[x=\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4 a c}}{2 a}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-10^{-2} \pm \sqrt{10^{-4}+4 \times 10^{-3}}}{2 \times 0.1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-0.01 \pm \sqrt{.0001+0.004}}{0.2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-0.01 \pm \sqrt{0.0041}}{0.2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-0.01 \pm 0.64}{0.2}=\frac{0.54}{0.2}{/tex} [Neglecting the negative term]
= 0.27
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = C.h = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.27 = 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M

questions.question: The pH of 0.1 M solution of the following salts increases in the order
questions.question_raw: The pH of 0.1 M solution of the following salts increases in the order
questions.explanation:
The characteristics of the given solutions are:
NaCl - neutral solution
NH4Cl - slightly acidic due to the following reaction
{tex}\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{H}^{+}{/tex}
NaCN - slightly alkaline due to the following reaction
CN- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HCN + OH-
HCl - highly acidic
The pH of the solution will follow the order highly acidic < slightly acidic < neutral < slightly alkaline i.e.
HCl < NH4Cl < NaCl < NaCN

questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest acid?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the strongest acid?
questions.explanation:
1. Higher the electronegatively of central atom, higher will be the acidic strength.
  2. In case of same atom, higher the value of oxidation state of the metal, higher will be its acidic strength.
    The electronegativity of Cl > S.
    Oxidation no. of Cl in ClO3(OH) = + 7
    Oxidation no. of Cl in ClO2(OH) = + 5
    Oxidation no. of S in SO(OH)2 = + 4
    Oxidation no. of S in SO2(OH)2 = + 6
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} ClO3(OH) is the strongest acid.

Solubility product < Ionic product

Ionic product = [Ag+][Cl] = {tex}\frac{10^{-4}}{2} \times \frac{10^{-4}}{2}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
which is greater than Ksp (1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10).

Ionised in the small intestine and almost unionised in the stomach
Completely ionised in the small intestine and in the stomach

Given, KspCaF2 = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
CaF2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ca2+ + 2F-
Ionic product of CaF2 = [Ca2+] [F-]2

  2. I.P. of CaF2 = (10-2) {tex}\times{/tex} (10-3)2 = 10-8

questions.question:
For the reaction, 1g mole of $CaC{O_3}$ is enclosed in 5L container
$CaC{O_3}\left( s \right)\; \to CaO\left( s \right) + C{O_2}\left( g \right)$
${K_p}$ = 1.16 at 1073 K then per cent dissociation of $CaC{O_3}$ is
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction, 1g mole of {tex}CaC{O_3}{/tex} is enclosed in 5L container
{tex}CaC{O_3}\left( s \right)\; \to CaO\left( s \right) + C{O_2}\left( g \right){/tex}
{tex}{K_p}{/tex} = 1.16 at 1073 K then per cent dissociation of {tex}CaC{O_3}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{C(1-\alpha)}{\underset C{CaCO_3(s)}}\;\leftrightarrow\underset{C\alpha}{\underset0{\;CaO(s)\;}}+\underset{C\alpha}{\underset0{\;CO_2(g)}}{/tex}
{tex}K_p\;=\;{K_c(RT)}^{\bigtriangleup n}{/tex}
For the reaction, Δn = 1
So, Kp = Kc RT
{tex}or, K_c=\frac{K_p}{ RT}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.16}{0.0821×1073}{/tex}
=0.0132
For the reaction, 
{tex}K_c = \frac{[CaO][CO_2]}{[CaCO_3]}{/tex}
Also, since CaCO3 and CaO are solids
So, [CaCO3] = [CaO] = 1
or, Kc= [CO2]
or, Cα=Kc
or, {tex}α=\frac{K_c}{C}{/tex}
Here, {tex}C=\frac{Mass}{Volume}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
So, {tex}α=\frac{0.0132×5}{1}=0.0658{/tex}
or, % α = 0.0658 ×100 = 6.58%

According to Guldberg and Waage, the rate at which substance reacts is directly proportional to its active mass (i.e. molar concentration).
{tex}aA\;+\;bB\Leftrightarrow\;xX\;+\;yY{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a reversible reaction?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is a characteristic of a reversible reaction?

options.text: It can be influenced by a catalyst
Number of moles of reactants and products are equal
It proceeds only in forward direction

questions.explanation:
A characteristic feature of reversible reactions is that they never proceed to completion.
Reversible reactions always attain equilibrium and never go for completion. Reversible reactions, however, a static equilibrium is not being established. In most of the reactions when carried out in closed vessels do not go to completion, under a given set of conditions of temperature and pressure. In fact in all such cases, in the initial state, only the reactants are present but as the reaction proceeds, the concentration of reactants decreases and that of products increases. Finally, a stage is reached when no further change in the concentration of the reactants and products is observed.

questions.question: The strongest Bronsted base among the following anions is
questions.question_raw: The strongest Bronsted base among the following anions is
questions.explanation: Bronsted base is the substance that can accept H+ ions. With the increase in the number of oxygen atoms in the given conjugate bases, the delocalization of the {tex}\pi{/tex} bond becomes more extended. This results in a decrease in electron density. Consequently, proton attraction and basicity also decrease in the order ClO−>ClO2−​>ClO3−​>ClO4−​. Hence, the strongest Bronsted base is ClO−.

questions.question: Ostwald’s dilution law is applicable to only
questions.question_raw: Ostwald’s dilution law is applicable to only
questions.explanation:
Ostwald’s dilution law is the application of the law of mass action to weak electrolytes in solution.
Ostwald's dilution law is only applicable to weak electrolytes because in strong electrolytes the dissociation is almost complete and the extent of dissociation is almost equal to 1, hence Ka​ goes to infinity.
In weak electrolytes, it is finite as the extent of dissociation is small.

questions.question: The pH value of human blood is about
questions.question_raw: The pH value of human blood is about
questions.explanation: The pH scale ranges from 0 (strongly acidic) to 14 (strongly basic or alkaline). A pH of 7.0, in the middle of this scale, is neutral. Blood is normally slightly basic, with a normal pH range of about 7.35 to 7.45. Usually, the body maintains the pH of blood close to 7.40. to 7.5.

questions.question: Which of the following compounds will undergo hydrolysis?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following compounds will undergo hydrolysis?
questions.explanation:
The salt of strong acid and strong bases like NaCl, KCl, and NaNO3 does not undergo hydrolysis.
In this case, neither the cations nor the anion undergoes hydrolysis. Therefore, the solution remains neutral.
In the aqueous solution of NaCl, KCl, and NaNO3  its ions Na+, K+, NO3, and Cl- ions have no tendency to react with H+ or OH- ions of water.
This is because the possible products of such reaction are NaOH, KOH, and HCl, which are they completely dissociated. As a result, there is no change in the concentration of H+ and OH- ions and hence the solution continues to remain neutral.
Only NH4Cl undergoes hydrolysis.

Concentration = Moles/liter
= 60/Molar mass
Given active mass = 2
So, 60/Molar mass = 2
=> Molar mass = 30

{tex}NaCl{O_2} \rightarrow Na^+ \ + ClO_2^-{/tex}
{tex}NaClO \rightarrow Na^+ \ + ClO^-{/tex}
In aqueous solution, these compounds decompose into ions. ClO-4 will most easily abstract protons from water molecules. So it will be most acidic.

questions.question: A solution with pH = 2 is more acidic than one with a pH = 6, by a factor
questions.question_raw: A solution with pH = 2 is more acidic than one with a pH = 6, by a factor
questions.explanation:
pH = -log [H+]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 = -log  [H+], and 6 = -log  [H+]2
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{log[H^+]_1}{log[H^+]_2} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-6}} = 10^{-4} = 10000{/tex}
Therefore, acidity is more at pH = 2 than pH =6 by an order of 10,000.

questions.question: 2 moles of $PC{l_5}$ were introduced in a 2 litre flask and heated at 625 K to establish equilibrium, when 60% $PC{l_5}$ dissociated into $PC{l_5}$ and $C{l_2}$. ${K_c}$(equilibrium constant) will be

[∵ Initial concentration of {tex}PCl_5​=\frac{2mole​}{2L}=1M/L{/tex}]

{tex}​\underset{\;At\;Equilibrium}{\underset{Initial}{}}​\underset{0.4\;mole}{\underset{1\;mole}{2SO_{3(g)}}}\;\Leftrightarrow\underset{0.6mole}{\underset{0\;mole}{2SO_{2(g)}}}\;+\underset{0.3\;mole}{\;\underset{0\;mole}{O_{2(g)}}}{/tex}

questions.question: The concentration of H+ ions in a solution of 1 M NaOH is
questions.question_raw: The concentration of H+ ions in a solution of 1 M NaOH is
questions.explanation:
Since NaOH is a strong base, .1 mol of it will dissociate into 1 mol Na+ and OH-.
By definition [H+]× [OH-] =Kw where Kw, the ionization constant of water, is 10-14. This is why the pH scale is 0–14 and also why looking at the exponent H+ concentration will generally tell you approximately the pH.
So if the concentration of the base (OH-) is 1M, then we can say
Ka= {tex}{10^{ - 14}}\,M{/tex}

The pH at room temperature is ~7 meaning there are about 10-7 moles of H+ per liter of water at room temperature. As the temperature increases, the ability of water to ionize in this way increases and so the concentration of H+ in solution will increase (and hence the pH will drop).

questions.question:
Of the following which change will shift the reaction towards the product?
${I_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons 2I\left( g \right),{\text{ }}\Delta {H_r}^\circ \left( {298{\text{ }}K} \right) = + 150{\text{ }}kJ$
questions.question_raw:
Of the following which change will shift the reaction towards the product?
{tex}{I_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons 2I\left( g \right),{\text{ }}\Delta {H_r}^\circ \left( {298{\text{ }}K} \right) =  + 150{\text{ }}kJ{/tex}
questions.explanation:
The reaction is {tex}I_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 I^{-}(g), \Delta H_{r}^{\circ}(298 K)=+150 k J{/tex}
According to Le-Chatelier's principle, the conditions favouring the formation of I are given below:

  1. The high concentration of I2​(g).
  2. High temperature (reaction is endothermic so, on increasing the temperature equilibrium it will shift in the direction where heat is absorbed).
  3. Low pressure (moles of products are more than moles of reactant. Therefore, if pressure is increased, equilibrium will shift in the direction where moles are decreasing).

questions.question: When 10 ml of 0.1 M acetic acid $\left( {p{K_a} = 5.0} \right)$ is titrated against 10 ml of 0.1 M ammonia solution$\left( {p{K_b} = 5.0} \right)$, the equivalence point occurs at pH

questions.question: 40 ml of 0.1 M ammonia solution is mixed with 20 ml of 0.1 M HCl. What is the pH of the mixture? ($p{K_b}$ of ammonia solution is 4.74)
questions.question_raw: 40 ml of 0.1 M ammonia solution is mixed with 20 ml of 0.1 M HCl. What is the pH of the mixture? ({tex}p{K_b}{/tex} of ammonia solution is 4.74)
questions.explanation:
40mL of 0.1 M ammonia solution = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1
= 4 milli-equivalent ammonia solution
20mL of 0.1 M HCl = 20 {tex}\times{/tex}0.1
= 2 milli-equivalent HCl
{tex}\underset{\underset{Milli-eq.\;after\;reaction}{Initial\;milli-eq}.}\;\;\underset{4-2=2}{\underset4{{\mathrm{NH}}_4\mathrm{OH}}}+\underset0{\underset2{\mathrm{HCl}}}\rightarrow\underset2{\underset0{{\mathrm{NH}}_4\mathrm{Cl}}}+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{pK}_{b}+\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]}=4.74+\log \frac{2}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=4.74+\log 1=4.74{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-4.74=9.26{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=4.75{/tex}

For the chemical equilibrium,
$CaC{O_3}(g) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g),$
$\Delta {H_r}^\circ $ can be determined from which one of the following plots?
questions.question_raw:
For the chemical equilibrium,
{tex}CaC{O_3}(g) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g),{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {H_r}^\circ {/tex} can be determined from which one of the following plots?
questions.explanation:
For the reaction, {tex}\operatorname{CacO} 3(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO} 2(\mathrm{g}){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{Kp}=P_{C O_{2}} \text { and } \mathrm{KC}=\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]{/tex} ([CaCO3] = 1 and [CaO] = 1 for solids]
According to Arrhenius equation we have  
{tex}K=A e^{-\Delta H^{\circ} r / R T}{/tex}
Taking logarithm, we have   
{tex}\log K_{p}=\log A-\frac{\Delta H_{r}^{o}}{R T(2.303)}{/tex}
 This is an equation of a straight line. When log Kp is plotted against 1 / T. we get a straight line.

The intercept of this line {tex}=\log A, \text { slope }=? \Delta H^{\circ} \mathrm{r} / 2.303 \mathrm{R}{/tex}
Knowing the value of slope from the plot and universal gas constant R, ∆Hr° can be calculated.
(Equation of straight line : Y = m.x + C). Here, 
{tex}\underset Y{\log K_p}=-\underset m{\frac{\Delta H_r^o}{2.303R}\;}\underset X{\left(\frac1T\right)}+\underset C{\log A}{/tex}

questions.question: The pH of neutral water at 25oC is 7.0. As the temperature increases, ionisation of water increases, however, the concentration of H+ ions and OH- ions are equal. What will be the pH of pure water at 60oC?
questions.question_raw: The pH of neutral water at 25oC is 7.0. As the temperature increases, ionisation of water increases, however, the concentration of H+ ions and OH- ions are equal. What will be the pH of pure water at 60oC?
questions.explanation:
At 25oC Kw = 10-14. It increases on heating i.e., Kw > 10-14 at 60oC
But [H+] = [OH-]
Thus [H+]2 > 10-14
or [H+]2 > 10-7 or pH < 7

questions.question_raw: The ionisation constant of an acid, Ka, is the measure of strength of an acid. The Ka values of acetic acid, hypochlorous acid and formic acid are 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 and 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 respectively. Which of the following orders of pH of 0.1 mol dm-3 solutions of these acids is correct?

options.text: Hypochlorous acid > formic acid > acetic acid
Acetic acid > hypochlorous acid > formic acid
Formic acid > acetic acid > hypochlorous acid
Formic acid > hypochlorous acid > acetic acid

questions.explanation:
Higher is Ka, more will be [H+] for weak acid
[H+] = c{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}c \sqrt{\frac{K a}{c}}=\sqrt{K a \cdot c}{/tex} lower will be pH.

questions.question:
Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions:
H2S $ \rightleftharpoons $ H+ + HS-
HS- $ \rightleftharpoons $ H+ + S2-
H2S $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2H+ + S2-
The correct relationship between Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 is:
questions.question_raw:
Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions:
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + HS-
HS- {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + S2-
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2H+ + S2-
The correct relationship between Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 is:
questions.explanation:
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + HS-; Ka1 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{HS}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{/tex}
HS- {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + S2-; Ka2 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HS}]^{-}}{/tex}
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2H+ + S2-; Ka3 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ka3 = Ka1 {tex}\times{/tex} Ka2

questions.question: The percentage error in hydronium ion concentration made by neglecting the ionisation of water in 1.0 $\times$ 10-6 M NaOH is:
questions.question_raw: The percentage error in hydronium ion concentration made by neglecting the ionisation of water in 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 M NaOH is:
questions.explanation:
Neglecting ionisation of water
[H+] = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
Also if ionisation of H2O is not neglected then
H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + OH-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kw = {tex}\overset{a}{10^{-14}}=\overset{(10^{-6}+a)}a\times(a + 10^{-6}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 9.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} % error = {tex}\frac{10 \times 10^{-9}-99 \times 10^{-9}}{9.9 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 1%

questions.question: PCl5, PCl3 and Cl2 are at equilibrium at 500 K in a closed container and their concentrations are 0.8 $\times$ 10-3 mol L-1, 1.2 $\times$ 10-3 mol L-1 and 1.2 $\times$ 10-3 mol L-1 respectively. The value of Kc for the reaction PCl5(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) will be:
questions.question_raw: PCl5, PCl3 and Cl2 are at equilibrium at 500 K in a closed container and their concentrations are 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1, 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 and 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 respectively. The value of Kc for the reaction PCl5(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) will be:
questions.explanation:
At eq. {tex}\mathop {{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_5}(g)}\limits_{0.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_3}}\limits_{1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} + \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}}\limits_{1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} {/tex}
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is incorrect?
 In equilibrium mixture of ice and water kept in perfectly insulated flask mass of ice and water does not change with time.
 The intensity of red colour increases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron(III) nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.
 On the addition of catalyst, the equilibrium constant value is not affected.
 The equilibrium constant for a reaction with negative $\Delta$H value decreases as the temperature increases
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements is incorrect?

  1. In equilibrium mixture of ice and water kept in perfectly insulated flask mass of ice and water does not change with time.
  2. The intensity of red colour increases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron(III) nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.
  3. On the addition of catalyst, the equilibrium constant value is not affected.
  4. The equilibrium constant for a reaction with negative {tex}\Delta{/tex}H value decreases as the temperature increases
questions.explanation:
{tex}F{e^{ + 3}} + SCN^- \rightleftharpoons {[Fe\mathop {(CNS)}\limits_{red} ]^2}^ + {/tex}
Addition of oxalic acid shows redox change with Fe3+ to convert them into Fe2+ and thus backward reaction predominates and thus the intensity of red colour decreases.

questions.question:
When hydrochloric acid is added to cobalt nitrate solution at room temperature, the following reaction takes place and the reaction mixture becomes blue. On cooling the mixture it becomes pink. On the basis of this information mark the correct answer.
${[Co{(\mathop {{H_2}O}\limits_{(pink)} )_6}]^{2 + }}(aq) + 4C{l^ - }(aq)$$ \rightleftharpoons {[\mathop {CoC{l_4}}\limits_{(blue)} ]^{2 - }}(aq) + 6{H_2}O(l)$
questions.question_raw:
When hydrochloric acid is added to cobalt nitrate solution at room temperature, the following reaction takes place and the reaction mixture becomes blue. On cooling the mixture it becomes pink. On the basis of this information mark the correct answer.
{tex}{[Co{(\mathop {{H_2}O}\limits_{(pink)} )_6}]^{2 + }}(aq) + 4C{l^ - }(aq){/tex}{tex} \rightleftharpoons {[\mathop {CoC{l_4}}\limits_{(blue)} ]^{2 - }}(aq) + 6{H_2}O(l){/tex}

options.text: The sign of $\Delta$H cannot be predicted on the basis of this information

questions.explanation:
{tex}{[Co{(\mathop {{H_2}O}\limits_{(pink)} )_6}]^{3 + }}(aq.) + 4C{l^ - }(aq.){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {[\mathop {CoC{l_4}}\limits_{(blue)} ]^{2 - }}(aq.) + 6{H_2}O{/tex}
On cooling mixture becomes pink means backward reaction predominates i.e., {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is -ve for backward reaction. Thus {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is +ve for the forward reaction.

questions.question: An acid type indicator, HIn differs in colour from its conjugate base (In-). The human, eye is sensitive to colour differences only when the ratio [In-]/[HIn] is greater than 10 or smaller than 0.1. What should be the minimum change in the pH of the solution to observe a complete colour change (Ka = 1.0 $\times$ 10-5):
questions.question_raw: An acid type indicator, HIn differs in colour from its conjugate base (In-). The human, eye is sensitive to colour differences only when the ratio [In-]/[HIn] is greater than 10 or smaller than 0.1. What should be the minimum change in the pH of the solution to observe a complete colour change (Ka = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5):
questions.explanation:
The two conditions when colour of indicator will be visible are derived by
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HIn}]}{/tex}

  1. pH = 5 + log 10 = 6
  2. pH = 5 + log 0.1 = 4

Thus, minimum change in pH i.e., {tex}\Delta{/tex}pH = (6 - 5) or (5 - 4) = 1

{tex}\underset{Colour A}HIn \rightleftharpoons H^{+}+\underset{ColourB}In^{-}{/tex}
KIn = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{Hln}]}{/tex}
The indicator HIn will show colour change at mid-point that is the point at which [In-] = [HIn]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} KIn = [H+] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: The pH of a saturated solution of Mg(OH)2 (Ksp of Mg (OH)2 = 8.9 $\times$ 10-12) is:
questions.question_raw: The pH of a saturated solution of Mg(OH)2 (Ksp of Mg (OH)2 = 8.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12) is:

Let solubility of Mg(OH)2 be S mol litre-1
{tex}Mg(OH)_2\rightleftharpoons \underset{s}mg^{2+} = \underset{2s}2OH^-{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Mg2+] [OH-]2 = Ksp

questions.question: The pH of a solution obtained by mixing 10 mL of 0.1 M HCl and 40 mL of 0.2 M H2SO4 is:
questions.question_raw: The pH of a solution obtained by mixing 10 mL of 0.1 M HCl and 40 mL of 0.2 M H2SO4 is:
questions.explanation:
Milli equivalent of H+ from HCl = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 1
Milli equivalent of H+ from H2SO4 = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 16
Total meq. of H+ in solution = 1 + 16 =17
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}\frac{17}{50}{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 {tex}\left(\because\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{\mathrm{Meq} .}{V_{\mathrm{in} \mathrm{mL}}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = -log [H+] = -log 0.34
pH = 0.4685

questions.question: What is the pH of solution after mixing 100 mL 0.1 M CH3COOH and 100 mL 0.04 M CH3COONa Ka for CH3COOH = 1.8 $\times$ 10-5
questions.question_raw: What is the pH of solution after mixing 100 mL 0.1 M CH3COOH and 100 mL 0.04 M CH3COONa Ka for CH3COOH = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
questions.explanation:
For acidic buffer mixture
pH = -log Ka + {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
[CH3COOH] = {tex}\frac{100 \times 0.1}{200}{/tex} = 0.05, [CH3COO-] = {tex}\frac{100 \times 0.4}{200}{/tex} = 0.2
pH = -log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{02}{0.05}{/tex}
pH = 4.7447 + log {tex}\frac{0.2}{0.05}{/tex}
= 4.7447 + 0.6020 = 5.3467

B+ + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} BOH + H+

questions.question: The pH of pure water at 25oC and 35oC are 7 and 6 respectively. The heat of formation of water from H+ and OH- are:
questions.question_raw: The pH of pure water at 25oC and 35oC are 7 and 6 respectively. The heat of formation of water from H+ and OH- are:
questions.explanation:
At 250C; [H+] = 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kw = 10-14
At 35oC; [H+] = 10-6
Kw = 10-12
Now using
2.303 log {tex}\frac{K_{w_{2}}}{K_{w_{1}}}=\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} \times T_{2}}\right]{/tex}
2.303 log {tex}\frac{10^{-12}}{10^{-14}}=\frac{\Delta H}{2}\left[\frac{10}{298 \times 308}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 84551.4 cal/mol
= 84.551 kcal/mol
Thus H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + OH-; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 84.551 kcal/mol
{tex}\therefore{/tex} H+ + OH- {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2O; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -84.551 kcal/mol

questions.question: The concentration of fluoroacetic acid (Ka of acid = 2.6 $\times$ 10-5) which is required to get [H+] = 1.50 $\times$ 10-3 M is:
questions.question_raw: The concentration of fluoroacetic acid (Ka of acid = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) which is required to get [H+] = 1.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M is:
questions.explanation:
Given, [H+] = {tex}C \alpha{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol litre-1
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Ka = {tex}\frac{(C \alpha)(C \alpha)}{C(1-\alpha)}=\frac{C \alpha \cdot \alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = {tex}\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.634
Now, {tex}C \alpha{/tex} = 1.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{1.50 \times 10^{-3}}{0.634}{/tex} = 2.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
Note: Since Ka is of the order of 10-3 M and thus it is not advisable to use Ka = {tex}C \alpha^{2}{/tex}. Because (1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) is not equal to 1 since {tex}\alpha{/tex} is not small.

questions.question: What is the pH of the solution on mixing 100 mL of 0.1 M NH4OH and 100 mL of 0.1 M (NH4)3 PO4? Kb for NH4OH = 1.8 $\times$ 10-5
questions.question_raw: What is the pH of the solution on mixing 100 mL of 0.1 M NH4OH and 100 mL of 0.1 M (NH4)3 PO4? Kb for NH4OH = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
questions.explanation:
For basic buffer mixture
pOH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate acid }]}{[\text { Base }]}{/tex}
[base] = [NH4OH] = {tex}\frac{100 \times 0.1}{200}{/tex} = 0.05
[conjugate acid] = {tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]=\frac{100 \times 0.1 \times 3}{200}{/tex} = 0.15
(NH4)3 PO4 gives three ions of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} (conjugate acid)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{0.15}{0.05} {/tex}
= 4.7447 + 0.4771 = 5.2218
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - 5.2218 = 8.7782

questions.question:
Given, HF + H2O $\stackrel{K_{a}}{\longrightarrow}$ H3O+ + F-
F- + H2O $\stackrel{K_{b}}{\longrightarrow}$ HF + OH-
Which relation is correct?

Given, HF + H2O {tex}\stackrel{K_{a}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} H3O+ + F-

questions.explanation:

and Kb = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HF}]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{F}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex}

questions.question: The concentration of hydroxyl ion in a solution left after mixing 100 mL of 0.1 M MgCl2 and 100 mL of 0.2 M NaOH (Ksp Mg(OH)2 = 1.2 $\times$ 10-11) is:

questions.explanation:
Thus, 10 m mole of Mg(OH)2 are formed. The ionic product of [Mg2+] [OH-]2 is therefore = {tex}\left[\frac{10}{200}\right] \times\left[\frac{20}{200}\right]^{2}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 which is more than Ksp of Mg (OH)2 and thus solution will lead to precipitation of Mg (OH)2
Also, Ksp = 4S3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{1.2 \times 10^{-11}}{4}}{/tex} = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 2S = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4

questions.question: A weak acid HA after treatment with 12 mL of 0.1 M strong base BOH has a pH of 5. At the end point, the volume of same base required is 26.6 mL. Ka of acid is:
questions.question_raw: A weak acid HA after treatment with 12 mL of 0.1 M strong base BOH has a pH of 5. At the end point, the volume of same base required is 26.6 mL. Ka of acid is:
questions.explanation:
For neutralisation:
Total meq. of acid = meq. of base
= 26.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 2.66
Now for partial neutralisation of acid

The resultant mixture acts as a buffer and [HA] and [BA] or [conjugate base B+] may be placed in terms of Meq. since volume of mixture is constant.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
or 5 = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[1.2]}{[1.46]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ka = 8.219 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6

questions.question:
At 500 K, equilibrium constant, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}$, for the following reaction is 5:
$\frac{1}{2}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) + \frac{1}{2}{\text{ }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \rightleftharpoons $ HI (g)
What would be the equilibrium constant Kc for the reaction $2HI(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + {I_2}(g)$
questions.question_raw:
At 500 K, equilibrium constant, {tex}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}{/tex}, for the following reaction is 5:
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) + \frac{1}{2}{\text{ }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \rightleftharpoons {/tex} HI (g)
What would be the equilibrium constant Kc for the reaction {tex}2HI(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + {I_2}(g){/tex}
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HI}(g) ; K_{1}=5=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{I}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}(g) ; K_{2}=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{II}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}=K_{1}^{2}=25{/tex}
{tex}2 \mathrm{HI}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \mathrm{I}_{2}(g) ; K_{3}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}=\frac{1}{25}{/tex} = 0.04

questions.question: Which of the following options will be correct for the stage of half completion of the reaction A $ \rightleftharpoons $ B?

A {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} B

questions.question: pH for the solution of salt undergoing anionic hydrolysis (say CH3COONa) is given by:
questions.question_raw: pH for the solution of salt undergoing anionic hydrolysis (say CH3COONa) is given by:
questions.explanation:
CH3COO- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COOH + OH-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = C {tex}\cdot{/tex} h = {tex}C \sqrt{\frac{K_{H}}{C}}=\sqrt{K_{H} \cdot C}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a}}} C{/tex}
or - log OH = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [log Kw + log C - log Ka]
or pOH = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [pKw - log C - pKa]
Now pH + pOH = pKw
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = {tex}\therefore{/tex} [pKw + log C + pKa]

questions.question: Which statement is/are correct?
questions.question_raw: Which statement is/are correct?

options.text: All cations are acids and all anions are bases
All Bronsted bases are also Lewis bases
All Bronsted acids are not Lewis acids

questions.explanation: Bronsted bases are proton acceptor and each Lewis base contain one or more electron pair and thus accept a proton. On the other hand, Bronsted acids are proton donors and may or may not be capable of accepting lone pairs of electrons, e.g., H2SO4 is Bronsted acid but not Lewis acid; BF3 is Lewis acid but not Bronsted acid. Also, each cation is acid and each anion is base.

questions.question: The reaction, A + 2B $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2C +D was studied using an initial concentration of B which was 1.5 times that of A. But the equilibrium concentration of A and C were found to be equal. Then the Kc for the equilibrium is:
questions.question_raw: The reaction, A + 2B {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2C +D was studied using an initial concentration of B which was 1.5 times that of A. But the equilibrium concentration of A and C were found to be equal. Then the Kc for the equilibrium is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop A\limits_a }\limits_{(a - x)} + \mathop {\mathop {2B}\limits_{\frac{3}{2}a} }\limits_{\left( {\frac{3}{2}a - 2x} \right)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2C}\limits_0 }\limits_{2x} + \mathop {\mathop D\limits_0 }\limits_x {/tex}
Given, a - x = 2x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = \frac {a}{3}{/tex}
Now, {tex}K_{c}=\frac{[C]^{2}[D]}{[A][B]^{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{2 a}{3}\right)^{2} \times \frac{a}{3}}{\left(a-\frac{a}{3}\right)\left(\frac{3 a}{2}-\frac{2 a}{3}\right)^{2}}{/tex} =  0.32

questions.question: 50 mL of 2 N acetic acid mixed with 10 mL of 1 N sodium acetate solution will have an approximate pH of (Ka = 10-5):

meq. of acetic acid = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 100
meq. of CH3COONa = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 10
pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
or pH = -log 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{10}{100}{/tex} = 4

questions.question: If $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s)$ + 2H2O(v) Kp = 1086 $\times$ 10-4 atm2 at 25oC. The efflorescent nature of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ can be noticed when vapour pressure of H2O in atmosphere is:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s){/tex} + 2H2O(v) Kp = 1086 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 atm2 at 25oC. The efflorescent nature of {tex}\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} can be noticed when vapour pressure of H2O in atmosphere is:
questions.explanation:
An efflorescent salt is one that loses H2O to the atmosphere.
For the reaction,
{tex}\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(s){/tex} + 2H2O(v)
{tex}K_{p}=\left(P_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\prime}\right)^{2}=1.086 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ P_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\prime}=1.042 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~atm}{/tex} = 7.92 mm
{tex}\because{/tex} If {tex}P_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}^{\prime}{/tex} at 25oC, < 7.92 mm only then, the reaction will proceed in forward direction.

questions.question: One litre of water contains 10-7 mole H+ ions. The degree of ionisation of water is:
questions.question_raw: One litre of water contains 10-7 mole H+ ions. The degree of ionisation of water is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\text { number of mole dissociated }}{\text { total mole present }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{10^{-7}}{1000 / 18}{/tex} = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 % (Total mole of H2O in 1 litre = {tex}\frac{1000}{18}{/tex})

questions.question: The solubility of PbCl2 in water is 0.01 M at 25oC. Its maximum concentration in 0.1 M NaCl will be:

Ksb PbCl2 = 4s3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.01)3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
In NaCl solution for PbCl2;
Ksp = [Pb2+] [Cl-]2
or 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 = [Pb2+] [0.1]2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Pb2+] = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M

questions.question: Sulphide ions in alkaline solution react with solid sulphur to form polyvalent sulphide ions. The equilibrium constant for the formation of $\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}$ from S and S2- ions are 1.7 and 5.3 respectively. Equilibrium constant for the formation of $\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}$ from $\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}$ and S is:
questions.question_raw: Sulphide ions in alkaline solution react with solid sulphur to form polyvalent sulphide ions. The equilibrium constant for the formation of {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} from S and S2- ions are 1.7 and 5.3 respectively. Equilibrium constant for the formation of {tex}\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} from {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} and S is:
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\mathrm{S}(s)+\mathrm{S}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}, {tex}K_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=1.7{/tex}
{tex}2 \mathrm{S}(s)+\mathrm{S}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}, \quad K_{\mathrm{C}_{2}}=5.3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}=1.7{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}K_{\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}=5.3{/tex} ...(ii)
Now for {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}+\mathrm{S}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} By eqs. (2) and (1), {tex}\frac{K_{\mathrm{C}_{2}}}{K_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}\right]}=K_{\mathrm{C}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} KC = 3.11

questions.question: One mole of nitrogen is mixed with 3 mole of hydrogen in a closed 3 litre vessel. 20% of nitrogen is converted into NH3. Then Kc for the $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{3} $ is:
questions.question_raw: One mole of nitrogen is mixed with 3 mole of hydrogen in a closed 3 litre vessel. 20% of nitrogen is converted into NH3. Then Kc for the {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{3} {/tex} is:

{tex}{\mathop {\frac{1}{2}{{\text{N}}_2}}\limits_{\left( {1 - \frac{{20}}{{100}}} \right)} + \mathop {\frac{3}{2}{{\text{H}}_2}}\limits_{\left( {3 - \frac{{3 \times 20}}{{100}}} \right)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{\left( {\frac{{20 \times 2}}{{100}}} \right){\text{mole}}\;{\text{at}}\;{\text{eq}}{\text{.}}} }{/tex}
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{[20 \times 2 /(100 \times 3)]}{\left[\frac{80}{100 \times 3}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\frac{240}{100 \times 3}\right]^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = 0.36 L mol-1

questions.question:
Slaked lime, Ca(OH)2 is used extensively in sewage treatment. What is the maximum pH that can be established in Ca(OH)2(aq):
Ca(OH)2 (s) $\rightleftharpoons$ Ca(aq) + 2OH- (aq), Ksp = 5.5 $\times$ 10-6
questions.question_raw:
Slaked lime, Ca(OH)2 is used extensively in sewage treatment. What is the maximum pH that can be established in Ca(OH)2(aq):
Ca(OH)2 (s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ca(aq) + 2OH- (aq), Ksp = 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6

{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{5.5 \times 10^{-6}}{4}}{/tex} = 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 2S = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 1.6528

questions.question: The solubility products of MA, MB, MC and MD are 1.8 $\times$ 10-10, 4 $\times$ 10-3, 4 $\times$ 10-8 and 6 $\times$ 10-5 respectively. If a 0.01 M solution of MX is added dropwise to a mixture containing A-, B-, C- and D- ions, then the one to be precipitated first will be:
questions.question_raw: The solubility products of MA, MB, MC and MD are 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 and 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 respectively. If a 0.01 M solution of MX is added dropwise to a mixture containing A-, B-, C- and D- ions, then the one to be precipitated first will be:
questions.explanation:
Ksp = [M+] [anion]; [A-] = {tex}\frac{10^{-10}}{0.01}{/tex}; [B-] = {tex}\frac{10^{-3}}{0.01}{/tex}; [C-] = {tex}\frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{0.01}{/tex}; [D-] = {tex}\frac{6 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01}{/tex}
Thus [A-] is smallest and the product of ionic concentration will exceed its Ksp in MA to show precipitation first.

questions.question:
The equilibrium constants for the reactions are:
H3PO4 $\overset{K_1}\rightleftharpoons$ H+ + H2P$\text O_4^{-}$; K1
H2PO4 $\overset{K_2}\rightleftharpoons$ H+ + HP$\text O_4^{2-}$; K2
HPO4 $\overset{K_3}\rightleftharpoons$ H+ + P$\text O_4^{3-}$; K3
The equilibrium constant for
$\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}$ will be:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constants for the reactions are:
H3PO4 {tex}\overset{K_1}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + H2P{tex}\text O_4^{-}{/tex}; K1
H2PO4  {tex}\overset{K_2}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + HP{tex}\text O_4^{2-}{/tex}; K2
HPO4  {tex}\overset{K_3}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + P{tex}\text O_4^{3-}{/tex}; K3
The equilibrium constant for
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}{/tex} will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{1}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{2}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{3}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}\right]}{/tex}
Multiplying these, three, K {tex}=K_{1} \times K_{2} \times K_{3}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{3}\left[\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}\right]}{/tex}.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 1

questions.question: At temperature, T, a compound AB2(g) dissociates according to the reaction 2AB2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2AB(g) + B2 (g) with a degree of dissociation x which is small compared with unity. The expression for Kp in terms of x and the total pressure, P is:
questions.question_raw: At temperature, T, a compound AB2(g) dissociates according to the reaction 2AB2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2AB(g) + B2 (g) with a degree of dissociation x which is small compared with unity. The expression for Kp in terms of x and the total pressure, P is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {2A{B_2}}\limits_1 }\limits_{1 - x} (g) \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2AB(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_x + \mathop {\mathop {{B_2}(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_{\frac{x}{2}} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{p}=\frac{x^{2} \cdot x}{2(1-x)^{2}}\left[\frac{P}{1+\frac{x}{2}}\right]^{1}{/tex}
x being small
{tex}\therefore \quad 1-x=1{/tex} and {tex}1+\frac{x}{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{p}=\frac{x^{3} \cdot P}{2}{/tex}

questions.question:
One mole of SO3 in one litre vessel attains equilibrium at T K:
2SO3(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2SO2(g) + O2(g)
The equilibrium mixture required 0.2 mole KMnO4 in acid medium. What is Kc for:
$\mathrm{SO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
questions.question_raw:
One mole of SO3 in one litre vessel attains equilibrium at T K:
2SO3(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2SO2(g) + O2(g)
The equilibrium mixture required 0.2 mole KMnO4 in acid medium. What is Kc for:
{tex}\mathrm{SO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
Eq. of SO2 formed = eq. of KMnO4 used
{tex}=0.2 \times 5=1.0^{-}{/tex} (Mn7+ + 5e + Mn2+)
mole of SO2 formed {tex}=\frac{1.0}{2}=0.5{/tex} {tex}\left(\mathrm{S}^{4+} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}^{6+}+2 e\right){/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(g) & \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+ \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.25 \end{array}{/tex}
{tex}K_{c_{1}}=\frac{(0.5)^{2} \times 0.25}{(0.5)^{2}}=0.25{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K_{c_{2}} \text { for } \mathrm{SO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}K_{c_{2}}=\sqrt{K_{c_{1}}}=\sqrt{0.25}{/tex} = 0.5

questions.question: A mixture of nitrogen and hydrogen in the ratio of 1 : 3 reach equilibrium with ammonia when 50% of the mixture has reacted. If the total pressure is P, the partial pressure of ammonia in the equilibrium mixture was:

questions.question: What weight of solid ammonium carbamate (NH2COONH4) when vaporised at 200°C will have a volume of 8.96 litre at 1.0 atm pressure. Assume that the solid completely decomposes into CO2 and NH3 at 200°C and 1.0 atm?
questions.question_raw: What weight of solid ammonium carbamate (NH2COONH4) when vaporised at 200°C will have a volume of 8.96 litre at 1.0 atm pressure. Assume that the solid completely decomposes into CO2 and NH3 at 200°C and 1.0 atm?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Before\;dissociation} }\limits_{After\;dissociation} \mathop {\mathop {N{H_2}COON{H_4}}\limits_a }\limits_0 \to \mathop {\mathop {2N{H_3}}\limits_0 }\limits_{2a} + \mathop {\mathop {C{O_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_a {/tex}
Total mole after decomposition = 3a
Thus PV = nRT
{tex}\therefore \ 1 \times 8.96=3 a \times 0.0821 \times 473{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 0.077
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of NH2COONH4 = 0.077 {tex}\times{/tex} 78 = 6.0 g

questions.question:
The equilibrium constant for a reversible chemical reaction varies with T as,
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \begin{gathered} {K_p}(at{m^{ - 2}}) \hfill \\ T(K) \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} {10^{ - 2}} \hfill \\ 400 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} {10^{ - 3}} \hfill \\ 450 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array}$
From this, it may be deduced that:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constant for a reversible chemical reaction varies with T as,
{tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \begin{gathered} {K_p}(at{m^{ - 2}}) \hfill \\ T(K) \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} {10^{ - 2}} \hfill \\ 400 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} {10^{ - 3}} \hfill \\ 450 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array}{/tex}
From this, it may be deduced that:

options.text: The value of Kp increases with increase in temperature
The reaction proceeds ten times faster at 450 K than 400 K
There are more molecules on the right hand side of the chemical equation than on the left
The forward reaction gives out heat

questions.explanation:
{tex}2.303 \log \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}=\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex}
and K1 > K2 and T2 > T1, then {tex}\Delta H=-\mathrm{ve}{/tex}

questions.question: A certain weak acid has a dissociation constant 1.0 $\times$ 10-4. The equilibrium constant for its reaction with a strong base is:
questions.question_raw: A certain weak acid has a dissociation constant 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. The equilibrium constant for its reaction with a strong base is:
questions.explanation:
HA {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + A-
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, HA + B+ + OH- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} B+ + A- + H2O
Keq. = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{/tex} ...(ii)
By (i) and (ii),
{tex}\frac{K_{e q}}{K_{a}}=\frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}=\frac{1}{K_{w}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Keq. = {tex}\frac{K_{a}}{K_{w}}=\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-14}}{/tex} = 1010

questions.question: The degree of dissociation of PCI5 ($\alpha$) obeying the equilibrium: PCl5 $ \rightleftharpoons $ PCl3 + Cl2 is related to the pressure at equilibrium by:
questions.question_raw: The degree of dissociation of PCI5 ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) obeying the equilibrium: PCl5 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl3 + Cl2 is related to the pressure at equilibrium by:

{tex}\mathop {\mathop {PC{l_5}}\limits_1 }\limits_{1 - \alpha } \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {PC{l_3}}\limits_0 }\limits_\alpha + \mathop {\mathop {C{l_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_\alpha {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{p}=\frac{\alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}\left(\frac{P}{1+\alpha}\right)^{1}{/tex}
or {tex}K_{p}=\frac{\alpha^{2} P}{1-\alpha^{2}} \text { or } \alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_{p}}{P}}{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\alpha^{2}=1\right){/tex}

questions.question: For the reaction, A+B $ \rightleftharpoons $ 3C at 25°C, a 3 litre vessel contains 1, 2, 4 moles of A, B and C respectively. If Kc for the reaction is 10, the reaction will proceed in:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, A+B {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 3C at 25°C, a 3 litre vessel contains 1, 2, 4 moles of A, B and C respectively. If Kc for the reaction is 10, the reaction will proceed in:
questions.explanation:
Quotient Q {tex}=\frac{[C]^{3}}{[A][B]}=\frac{4^{3} \times 3 \times 3}{3^{3} \times 1 \times 2}{/tex} = 10.66
{tex}\left[[C]=\frac{4}{3} ;[A]=\frac{1}{3},[B]=\frac{2}{3}\right]{/tex}
Since Kc = 10, Thus Q must decrease to attain value of Kc and therefore [C] must decrease or [A] or [B] must increase i.e., backward direction.

questions.question: A buffer solution is prepared by mixing 10 mL of 1.0 M acetic and 20 mL of 0.5 M sodium acetate and then diluted to 100 mL with distilled water. If the pKa of CH3COOH is 4.76, what is the pH of the buffer solution prepared?

questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{\text { [Conjugate base }}{\text { [Acid] }}{/tex}
[Conjugate base] = {tex}\frac{20 \times 0.5}{30}{/tex}; [Acid] = {tex}\frac{10 \times 1}{30}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 4.76 + log {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} = 4.76

{tex}K_{p}=K_{c}(R T)^{\Delta n}{/tex},
{tex}\because \Delta n=-1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K_{p}=26 \times(0.0821 \times 523)^{-1}{/tex} = 0.61 atm-1

questions.question: The value of log10Ko for a reaction is A $ \rightleftharpoons $ B: (Given $\Delta_{r} \mathrm{H}_{298}$ = -54.07 kJ mol-1, $\Delta_{r} \mathrm{S}_{298 \mathrm{~K}}^{0}=10 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1, 2.303 $\times$ 8.314 $\times$ 298 = 5705):
questions.question_raw: The value of log10Ko for a reaction is A {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} B: (Given {tex}\Delta_{r} \mathrm{H}_{298}{/tex} = -54.07 kJ mol-1, {tex}\Delta_{r} \mathrm{S}_{298 \mathrm{~K}}^{0}=10 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex} and R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1, 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 = 5705):
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=-54.07 \times 10^{3}-298 \times 10{/tex} = -57050
Now {tex}-\Delta G^{\circ}=2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log K^{\circ}{/tex}
57050 {tex}=2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \log K^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ \log K^{\circ}=\frac{57050}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}=\frac{57050}{5705}{/tex} = 10

questions.question:
Three reactions involving H2$\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ are given below:
 H3PO4 + H2O $\rightarrow$ H3O+ + H2$\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$
 H2$\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ + H2O $\rightarrow$ $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}$ + H3O+
 H2$\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ + OH- $\rightarrow$ H3PO4 + O2-
In which of the above does H2$\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ act as an acid?
questions.question_raw:
Three reactions involving H2{tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} are given below:

  1. H3PO4 + H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H3O+ + H2{tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}
  2. H2{tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + H3O+
  3. H2{tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + OH- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H3PO4 + O2-

questions.explanation: H2{tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} donates H+ to H2O.

questions.question:
For the reaction in equilibrium,
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)$
the concentrations, of N2O4 and NO2 at equilibrium are $4.8 \times 10^{-2}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ respectively. The value of Kc for this reaction is:
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction in equilibrium,
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g){/tex}
the concentrations, of N2O4 and NO2 at equilibrium are {tex}4.8 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} and {tex}1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}{/tex} respectively. The value of Kc for this reaction is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right]}=\frac{\left[1.2 \times 10^{-2}\right]^{2}}{4.8 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1

questions.question: The solubility product of silver bromide is 5.0 $\times$ 10-13. The quantity of potassium bromide (molar mass taken as 120 g mol-1) to be added to 1 litre of 0.05 M solution of silver nitrate to start the precipitation of AgBr is:
questions.question_raw: The solubility product of silver bromide is 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13. The quantity of potassium bromide (molar mass taken as 120 g mol-1) to be added to 1 litre of 0.05 M solution of silver nitrate to start the precipitation of AgBr is:
questions.explanation:
[Ag+] [Br-] = Ksp AgBr
[0.05] [Br-] = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Br-] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 = [KBr]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mole of KBr = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11
Weigth of KBr = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 {tex}\times{/tex} 120
= 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 g

questions.question: The pH of a 0.1 molar solution of the acid HQ is 3. The value of the ionization constant, Ka of this acid is:
questions.question_raw: The pH of a 0.1 molar solution of the acid HQ is 3. The value of the ionization constant, Ka of this acid is:
questions.explanation:
0.1 M weak acid (HQ) has pH = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}C \alpha{/tex} = 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 10-2 ({tex}\because{/tex} C = 10-1)
Now for weak acid,
Ka = {tex}C \alpha^{2}=C \alpha \cdot \alpha{/tex} = 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = 10-5

questions.question:
The equilibrium constant (Kc) for the reaction,
N2(g) + O2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO(g)
at room temperature T is 4 $\times$ 10-4. The value of Kc for $\mathrm{NO}(g) \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g)$ at the same T is:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constant (Kc) for the reaction,
N2(g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO(g)
at room temperature T is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. The value of Kc for {tex}\mathrm{NO}(g) \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g){/tex} at the same T is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{c_{1}}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}=4 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}K_{c_{2}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}{[\mathrm{NO}]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{c_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{K_{c_{1}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-4}}}{/tex} = 50

questions.question: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4 is 1.1 $\times$ 10-12 at 298 K. The solubility (in mol/L) of Ag2CrO4 in a 0.1 M AgNO3 solution is:

questions.explanation:
Ionic product of Ag2CrO4 = [Ag+]2{tex}\left[\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{-2}\right]{/tex}
= (2s + 0.1)2 {tex}\times{/tex} s = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
neglecting s as compared to 0.1
s = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10

questions.question: The initial rate of hydrolysis of methyl acetate (1 M) by a weak acid (HA, 1M) is 1/100th of that of a strong acid (HX, 1M), at 25oC. The Ka of HA is:
questions.question_raw: The initial rate of hydrolysis of methyl acetate (1 M) by a weak acid (HA, 1M) is 1/100th of that of a strong acid (HX, 1M), at 25oC. The Ka of HA is:
questions.explanation:
Rate = K[Ester] [H+]
For strong acid [H+] = 1 M
For weak acid [H+] = {tex}\sqrt{K_{\mathrm{a}} \cdot C}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\text { Rate }_{1}}{\text { Rate }_{2}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{1} \text { for strong acid }}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{2} \text { for weak acid }}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{1 / 100}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{K_{a} \times 1}}{/tex} (C = 1 M for weak acid)
Ka = 10-4 M

questions.question: For the reaction: 2NO2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO (g) + O2(g); Kc = 1.8 $\times$ 10-6 at 184oC and R = 0.083 litre atm K-1 mol-1. When Kp and Kc are compared at 184°C, it is found that:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction: 2NO2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO (g) + O2(g); Kc = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 at 184oC and R = 0.083 litre atm K-1 mol-1. When Kp and Kc are compared at 184°C, it is found that:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta n{/tex} = 2 + 1 - 2 = +1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kp = Kc(RT)1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kp > Kc

 ZnO + Na2O $\rightarrow$ Na2ZnO2

  1. ZnO + Na2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na2ZnO2
  2. ZnO + CO2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnCO3

questions.question: A definite amount of solid NH4HS is placed in a flask already containing NH3 gas at certain temperature and 0.50 atm pressure. NH4HS decomposes to give NH3 and H2S and total equilibrium pressure in flask is 0.84 atm. The equilibrium constant for the reaction is:
questions.question_raw: A definite amount of solid NH4HS is placed in a flask already containing NH3 gas at certain temperature and 0.50 atm pressure. NH4HS decomposes to give NH3 and H2S and total equilibrium pressure in flask is 0.84 atm. The equilibrium constant for the reaction is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\begin{array}{cccc} & \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{HS}(s) & \rightleftharpoons & \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(g) \\ \mathrm{at} & t=0 & & 0.5 & 0 \\ \mathrm{at} & t=\mathrm{eq} . & & 0.5+P & P \end{array}{/tex}
PT = 0.5 + P + P = 0.84
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P = 0.17 atm
Kp = {tex}P_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}} \times P_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}{/tex} = (0.5 + 0.17) {tex}\times{/tex} 0.17
= 0.11 atm

questions.question: pKa of a weak acid (HA) and pKb of a weak base (BOH) are 3.2 and 3.4, respectively. The pH of their salt (AB) solution is:
questions.question_raw: pKa of a weak acid (HA) and pKb of a weak base (BOH) are 3.2 and 3.4, respectively. The pH of their salt (AB) solution is:
questions.explanation:
pH = 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (pKa - pKb)
= 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (3.2 - 3.4)
= 6.9

questions.question:
Given:
$\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} ; $ $K_{1}^{\circ}=1.4 \times 10^{-4}$
$\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} ; $ $K_{2}^{\circ}=4.3 \times 10^{-4}$
The instability constant of the complex Ag $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}^{+}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw:
Given:
{tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} ; {/tex} {tex}K_{1}^{\circ}=1.4 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} ; {/tex} {tex}K_{2}^{\circ}=4.3 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
The instability constant of the complex Ag {tex}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}^{+}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Ko for
{tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex}
{tex}K^{\circ}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}}{/tex}
Also, {tex}K_{1}^{\circ}=1.4 \times 10^{-4}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+2}}{/tex}
and {tex}K_{2}^{\circ}=4.3 \times 10^{-4}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K^{0}=K_{1}^{o} \times K_{2}^{o}=1.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.3 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}=6.02 \times 10^{-8}{/tex}

questions.question:
The equilibrium constant for the reaction,
$\mathrm{SO}_{3}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) K_{c}=4.9 \times 10^{-2}$
The Kc for the reaction: 2SO2(g) + O2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2SO3(g) will be:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constant for the reaction,
{tex}\mathrm{SO}_{3}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) K_{c}=4.9 \times 10^{-2}{/tex}
The Kc for the reaction: 2SO2(g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2SO3(g) will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{c_{1}}=4.9 \times 10^{-2}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{c_{2}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K_{c_{2}}=\left[\frac{1}{K_{c_{1}}}\right]^{2}=\left[\frac{1}{4.9 \times 10^{-2}}\right]^{2}{/tex} = 416.5

questions.question: The activation energies of forward and backward reaction: A2 + B2 $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2AB are 180 kJ mol-1 and 200 kJ mol-1 respectively. The presence of a catalyst lowers the activation energy of both (forward and backward) reactions by 100 kJ mol-1. The enthalpy change of the reaction in the presence of catalyst will be (in kJ mol-1)
questions.question_raw: The activation energies of forward and backward reaction: A2 + B2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2AB are 180 kJ mol-1 and 200 kJ mol-1 respectively. The presence of a catalyst lowers the activation energy of both (forward and backward) reactions by 100 kJ mol-1. The enthalpy change of the reaction in the presence of catalyst will be (in kJ mol-1)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta H=E_{a_{(f)}}-E_{a_{(b)}}=180-200{/tex}
= -20 kJ mol-1 (in absence of catalyst)
{tex}\therefore \Delta H=E_{a_{(f)}}-E_{a_{(b)}}{/tex}
= [180 - 100] - [200 - 100]
= -20 kJ mol-1 (in presence of catalyst)
Catalyst's have no influence on {tex}\Delta{/tex}H

HCl + CH3COOH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Cl- + CH3COO{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}{/tex}

questions.question: The equilibrium constants $K_{P_{1}}$ and $K_{P_{2}}$ for the reactions X $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2Y and Z $ \rightleftharpoons $ P + Q, respectively are in the ratio of 1 : 9. If the degree of dissociation of X and Z be equal then the ratio of total pressure at these equilibria is:
questions.question_raw: The equilibrium constants {tex}K_{P_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}K_{P_{2}}{/tex} for the reactions X {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2Y and Z {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} P + Q, respectively are in the ratio of 1 : 9. If the degree of dissociation of X and Z be equal then the ratio of total pressure at these equilibria is:
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}\frac{K_{P_{1}}}{K_{P_{2}}}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha_{x}=\alpha_{z}{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\mathop X\limits_1 }\limits_{1 - \alpha } \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2Y}\limits_0 }\limits_{2\alpha } {/tex} {tex}\therefore K_{P_{1}}=\frac{\left(2 \alpha_{x}\right)^{2}}{\left(1-\alpha_{x}\right)} \times\left[\frac{P_{1}}{1+\alpha_{x}}\right]^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\mathop Z\limits_1 }\limits_{1 - \alpha } \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {P + Q}\limits_0 }\limits_{2\alpha } {/tex} {tex}\therefore K_{P_{2}}=\frac{\left(\alpha_{z}\right)^{2}}{\left(1-\alpha_{z}\right)} \times\left[\frac{P_{2}}{1+\alpha_{z}}\right]^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\frac{K_{P_{1}}}{K_{P_{2}}} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{9} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{36}{/tex}

questions.question: The degree of dissociation of water at 25oC is 1.9 $\times$ 10-7 % and density is 1.0 g cm-3. The equilibrium constant for water is:
questions.question_raw: The degree of dissociation of water at 25oC is 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 % and density is 1.0 g cm-3. The equilibrium constant for water is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{e q}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex}
for {tex}\underset{1-\alpha}{H_2O}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset\alpha{H^+}\;+\;\underset\alpha{OH^-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}C \alpha{/tex} and {tex}C_{\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{2}} \mathrm{O}}=\frac{1000}{18}{/tex} = 55.6
{tex}K_{e q}=\frac{C \alpha \cdot C \alpha}{C(1-\alpha)}{/tex} (1 - {tex}\alpha \simeq{/tex} 1)
{tex}\therefore K_{e q .}=\frac{\left(55.6 \times 1.9 \times 10^{-9}\right)^{2}}{55.6}{/tex} = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16
