questions.question:
For the following three reactions a, b and c equilibrium constants are given:
 CO(g) + H2O(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ CO2 (g) + H2(g); K1
 CH4 (g) + H2O(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ CO(g) + 3H2 (g); K2
 CH4(g) + 2H2O(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ CO2 (g) + 4H2 (g); K3
Which of the following relations is correct?
questions.question_raw:
For the following three reactions a, b and c equilibrium constants are given:

  1. CO(g) + H2O(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO2 (g) + H2(g); K1
  2. CH4 (g) + H2O(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO(g) + 3H2 (g); K2
  3. CH4(g) + 2H2O(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO2 (g) + 4H2 (g); K3

Which of the following relations is correct?
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{1}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{[\mathrm{CO}]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex} ...(1)
{tex}K_{2}=\frac{[\mathrm{CO}]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex} ...(2)
{tex}K_{3}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{4}}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex} ...(3)
By eqs. (1), (2) and (3)
{tex}K_{3}=K_{1} \times K_{2}{/tex}

questions.question: A vessel at 1000 K contains CO2 with a pressure of 0.5 atm. Some of the CO2 is converted into CO on the addition of graphite. If the total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm, the value of K is:
questions.question_raw: A vessel at 1000 K contains CO2 with a pressure of 0.5 atm. Some of the CO2 is converted into CO on the addition of graphite. If the total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm, the value of K is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{O_2}}\limits_{0.5\;atm} }\limits_{0.5 - p} + C(s) \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2CO(g)}\limits_{} }\limits_{2p} {/tex}
Total pressure = 0.5 - P + 2P = 0.8
P = 0.3
{tex}K_{p}=\frac{P_{\mathrm{CO}}^{2}}{P_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}=\frac{(2 P)^{2}}{(0.5-P)}=\frac{(0.6)^{2}}{(0.5-0.3)}{/tex}
Kp = 1.8

questions.question: The dissociation constant of benzoic acid at 25oC is 1.0 $\times$ 10-4. The pH of a 0.01M solution of its sodium salt is:
questions.question_raw: The dissociation constant of benzoic acid at 25oC is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. The pH of a 0.01M solution of its sodium salt is:
questions.explanation:
C6H5COO- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C6H5COOH + OH-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = CH = {tex}\mathrm{C} \sqrt{\frac{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{\mathrm{C}}}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w} \mathrm{C}}{K_{a}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14} \times 0.01}{1 \times 10^{-4}}}{/tex} = 10-6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-8 or pH = 8

questions.question: The equilibrium constant at 298 K for a reaction A + B $ \rightleftharpoons $ C + D is 100. If the initial concentration of all the four species were 1 M each, then equilibrium concentration of D (in mol L-1) will be:
questions.question_raw: The equilibrium constant at 298 K for a reaction A + B {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} C + D is 100. If the initial concentration of all the four species were 1 M each, then equilibrium concentration of D (in mol L-1) will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Concentration\;at\;t = 0} }\limits_{Concentration\,\;at\;eq.} \mathop {\mathop A\limits_1 }\limits_{1 - x} + \mathop {\mathop B\limits_1 }\limits_{1 - x} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop C\limits_1 }\limits_{1 + x} + \mathop {\mathop D\limits_1 }\limits_{1 + x} {/tex}
{tex}\because \quad K_{c}=\frac{[C][D]}{[A][B]}=\frac{(1+x)^{2}}{(1-x)^{2}}=100{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1+x}{1-x}=10{/tex}
or 1 + x = 10 - 10x
{tex}\therefore \quad x=\frac{9}{11}{/tex}
Now [D] = 1 + x {tex}=1+\frac{9}{11}{/tex} = 1.818

questions.question_raw:
An alkali is titrated against acid with methyl orange as indicator, which of the following is a correct combination?

  Base Acid Endpoint
(a) Weak Strong Yellow to pinkish-red
(b) Strong Strong Pink to colourless
(c) Weak Strong Colourless to pink
(d) Strong Strong Pinkish red to yellow
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{Weak\;base}{NH_4OH}\;+\underset{Strong\;acid}{\;HCl\;}\rightarrow NH_4Cl\;+\;H_2O{/tex}
At the end point, pH will be less than 7 due to hydrolysis of NH4Cl
{tex}NH_4^+{/tex} + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NH4OH + H+
Working range of methyl orange (MeOH):
In acid medium : Pink red
In alkaline medium : Yellow
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Methyl orange will change colour from yellow to pinkish red at the end point.

questions.question:
The following reaction is performed at 298 K.
2NO (g) + O2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO2(g)
The standard free energy of formation of NO(g) is 86.6kJ/mol at 298 K. What is the standard free energy of formation of NO2(g) at 298 K? (Kp = 1.6 $\times$ 1012)
questions.question_raw:
The following reaction is performed at 298 K.
2NO (g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2(g)
The standard free energy of formation of NO(g) is 86.6kJ/mol at 298 K. What is the standard free energy of formation of NO2(g) at 298 K? (Kp = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G_{f}^{\circ}=2 \times G^{\circ} \mathrm{NO}_{2}-\left[2 \times G_{\mathrm{NO}}^{\circ}+G_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=2 G_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}^{\circ}-2 G_{\mathrm{NO}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\left[G_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\circ}=0\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad G_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2} \Delta G_{f}^{\circ}+G_{\mathrm{NO}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[-R T \ln K_{p}\right]+G_{\mathrm{NO}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[-R \times 298 \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right)\right]+86600{/tex}
{tex}=0.5\left[-R \times 298 \ln 1.6 \times 10^{12}+2 \times 86600\right]{/tex}

questions.question: An aqueous solution contains an unknown concentration of Ba2+. When 50 mL of a 1 solution of Na2SO4 is added, BaSO4 just begins to precipitate. The final volume is 500mL. The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1 $\times$ 10-10. What is the original concentration of Ba2+?
questions.question_raw: An aqueous solution contains an unknown concentration of Ba2+. When 50 mL of a 1 solution of Na2SO4 is added, BaSO4 just begins to precipitate. The final volume is 500mL. The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10. What is the original concentration of Ba2+?
questions.explanation:
Ba2+ + {tex}\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}\rightleftharpoons B\underset{ppt}{aS}O_{4(s)}{/tex}
Final conc. of {tex}\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]=\frac{\text { milli mole }}{\text { Total volume }}=\frac{1 \times 50}{500}{/tex},= 0.1 M
Final cone, of [Ba2+] when BaSO4 start precipitating
Ksp = [Ba2+] {tex}\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex}
10-10 = [Ba2+] (0.1 M)
[Ba2+] = 10-9 M in 500 mL
Initial cone. [Ba2+]:
Initial volume = Final volume - Volume of Na2SO4 added
= 500 - 50 = 450 mL
Initial milli mole = Final milli mole
450 {tex}\times{/tex} M = 10-9 {tex}\times{/tex} 500
{tex}\therefore{/tex} M = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 M

questions.question:
The standard Gibbs energy change at 300 K for the reaction 2A $ \rightleftharpoons $ B+ C is 2494.2 J. At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is [A] $=\frac{1}{2}$, [B] = 2 and [C] $=\frac{1}{2}$. The reaction proceeds in the:
[R = 8.314 J/K/mol]
questions.question_raw:
The standard Gibbs energy change at 300 K for the reaction 2A {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} B+ C is 2494.2 J. At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is [A] {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, [B] = 2 and [C] {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}. The reaction proceeds in the:
[R = 8.314 J/K/mol]
questions.explanation:
{tex}Q=\frac{[B][C]}{[A]^{2}}=\frac{2 \times \frac{1}{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=4{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -2.303 RT log KC
2494.2 {tex}=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \log K_{c}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{c}=0.36{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Q > Kc thus backward reaction

questions.question: An aqueous solution contains 0.10 M H2S and 0.20 M HCl. If the equilibrium constant for the formation of HS- from H2S is 1.0 $\times$ 10-7 and that of S2- from HS- ions is 1.2 $\times$ 10-13 then the concentration of S2- ions in aqueous solution is:
questions.question_raw: An aqueous solution contains 0.10 M H2S and 0.20 M HCl. If the equilibrium constant for the formation of HS- from H2S is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 and that of S2- from HS- ions is 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 then the concentration of S2- ions in aqueous solution is:
questions.explanation:
H2S {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + HS-; {tex}\mathrm{Ka}_{1}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{II}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{HS}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{/tex}
HS- {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + S2-; {tex}K a_{2}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HS}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} K1 {tex}\times{/tex} K2 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{/tex}
or [S2-] = {tex}\frac{K_{1} \times K_{2} \times\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-13} \times 0.1}{(0.2)^{2}}{/tex} =  3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20
[H+] are provided by strong acid as dissociation of weak acid H2S is suppressed in presence of strong acid HCl.

questions.question:
K1 and K2 are equilibrium constant for reactions (i) and (ii)
$\mathrm{N}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{(g)}$ ...(i)
$\mathrm{NO}_{(g)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2(g)}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)}$ ...(ii)
questions.question_raw:
K1 and K2 are equilibrium constant for reactions (i) and (ii)
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{(g)}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\mathrm{NO}_{(g)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2(g)}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)}{/tex} ...(ii)
questions.explanation:
N2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g) K1
Reverse the reaction
2NO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} N2(g) + O2(g), {tex}\frac {1}{K_1}{/tex}
Divide the above equation by 2
NO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} N2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\left(\frac {1 }{ K_1}\right)^\frac {1 }{ 2}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{1}{ K _1}\right)^\frac {1}{2}= K _2\left(\frac{1}{ K _1}\right)^\frac {1}{ 2}= K _2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow K _1=\left[\frac{1}{ K _2}\right]^2{/tex}

questions.question: 1.1 mole of A are mixed with 2.2 mole of B and the mixture is then kept in one litre flask till the equilibrium is attained A + 2B $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2C + D. At the equilibrium 0.2 mole of C are formed. The equilibrium constant of the reaction is:
questions.question_raw: 1.1 mole of A are mixed with 2.2 mole of B and the mixture is then kept in one litre flask till the equilibrium is attained A + 2B {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2C + D. At the equilibrium 0.2 mole of C are formed. The equilibrium constant of the reaction is:

{tex}\mathop {\mathop A\limits_a }\limits_{(a - x)} + \mathop {\mathop {2B}\limits_b }\limits_{(b - 2x)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2C}\limits_0 }\limits_{2x} + \mathop {\mathop D\limits_0 }\limits_x {/tex}

x = 0.1, b = 2.2
{tex}\therefore K_{c}=\frac{(2 x)^{2} \cdot x}{(a-x)(b-2 x)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(2 \times 0.1)^{2} \times 0.1}{(1.1-0.1)(2.2-0.2)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.001

questions.question: Reaction BaO2 (s) BaO(s) + O2(g); $\Delta H$ = +ve. In equilibrium condition, pressure of O2 depends on:
questions.question_raw: Reaction BaO2 (s) BaO(s) + O2(g); {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = +ve. In equilibrium condition, pressure of O2 depends on:

options.text: increase mass of BaO2 and BaO both

questions.explanation:
{tex}BaO _2( s ) \rightleftharpoons BaO ( s )+ O _2( g ){/tex}
At equilibrium, {tex}K =\frac{[ BaO ]\left[ O _2\right]}{\left[ BaO _2\right]}{/tex}
BaO2 and BaO are in solid state. Thus, their concentration is constant and does not change. Hence, equilibrium constant is independent of these two concentration terms.
K = [O2]
Thus, increased mass of BaO2 or BaO does not have any effect on equilibrium state of the reaction. Now, as the reaction is endothermic (as {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0) then increase in temperature will favour the forward direction of equilibrium and hence more O2 is formed and higher will be the pressure of O2,

questions.question:
The equilibrium constants for following reactions are given
N2 + 3H2 $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NH3, K1
N2 + O2 $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO, K2
H2 + $\frac 12$O2 $ \rightleftharpoons $ H2O, K3
The equilibrium constant (K) of the reaction, 2NH3 + $\frac{5}{2} O _2 \rightleftharpoons$ 2NO + 3H2O will be:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constants for following reactions are given
N2 + 3H2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3, K1
N2 + O2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO, K2
H2 + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}O2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H2O, K3
The equilibrium constant (K) of the reaction, 2NH3 + {tex}\frac{5}{2} O _2 \rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO + 3H2O will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}
{tex}K=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^\frac {3 }{ 2}}=\frac{K_{2} \times K_{3}^{3}}{K_{1}}{/tex}

questions.question:
In the two gaseous reactions (i) and (ii) at 250oC
 $\mathrm{NO}_{(g)}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}_{2(g)} , K_{1}$
 2NO2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO(g) + O2(g), K2
questions.question_raw:
In the two gaseous reactions (i) and (ii) at 250oC

  1. {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{(g)}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}_{2(g)} , K_{1}{/tex}
  2. 2NO2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO(g) + O2(g), K2
questions.explanation:
{tex}NO _{(g)}+\frac{1}{2} O _{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons NO _{2(g)} K _1{/tex}
Reverse the above equation
{tex}NO _{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons NO _{(g)}+\frac{1}{2} O _{2(g)} \frac {1 }{ K _1}{/tex}
Multiply the above equation by 2
2NO2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g) + O2(g) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{ K _1}\right)^2= K _2{/tex}

{tex}C\underset{c(1-\alpha)}{H_3COO}H\;\rightleftharpoons\;C\underset{c\alpha}{H_3CO}O^-\underset{3.4\times10^{-4}}{\;+\underset{c\alpha}{\;H^+}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} c{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\text { c. } \sqrt{\frac{K_{a}}{c}}{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
or {tex}\sqrt{K_{a} \cdot c}{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
or c = {tex}\frac{\left(3.4 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{2}}{1.7 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 6.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question:
For the reaction,
CH4(g) + 2O2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ CO2(g) + 2H2O(l); $\Delta_{r} H$ = -170.8 KJ mol-1
Which of the following statements is not true?
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction,
CH4(g) + 2O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O(l); {tex}\Delta_{r} H{/tex} = -170.8 KJ mol-1
Which of the following statements is not true?

options.text: At equilibrium, the concentrations of CO2(g) and H2O(l) are not equal
Addition of CH4(g) or O2(g) at equilibrium will cause a shift to the right
The equilibrium constant for the reaction is given by: $K_{p}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{2}}$

questions.explanation:
The products CO2 and H2O have different stoichiometric coefficients and they are at different physical states thus, they have different concentration at equilibrium.
For the given reaction, equilibrium constant
{tex}K _{ C }=\frac{\left[ CO _2\right]}{\left[ CH _4\right]\left[ O _2\right]^2}{/tex}
Now, as we know, Kp = Kc {tex}\times{/tex} (RT)
{tex}\Delta n_g =n_{\text {products }}-n_{\text {reactants }}{/tex}
{n = number of gaseous moles}
= 1 - (1 + 2)
= -2
{tex}K _{ P } = K _{ C }( RT )^{-2} \frac{\left[ CO _2\right]}{\left[ CH _4\right]\left[ O _2\right]} \times \frac{1}{( RT )^2}{/tex}
Thus, statement (b) is not true.
On addition of CH4 or O2, i.e., reactants will increase the concentration of reactants and according to Le-Chatelier's principle the equilibrium shifts to the right.
As change in enthalpy of the reactions is negative thus, the reaction is exothermic. Hence, the statements (a), (c) and (d) are true while (b) is not true.

questions.question: The rapid change of pH near the stoichiometric point of an acid-base titration is the basic of indicator detection. pH of the solution is related to the ratio of the concentration of conjugate acid [HIn] and base [In-]:
questions.question_raw: The rapid change of pH near the stoichiometric point of an acid-base titration is the basic of indicator detection. pH of the solution is related to the ratio of the concentration of conjugate acid [HIn] and base [In-]:

options.text: $\stackrel{4}{ C } H \equiv \stackrel{3}{ C }-\stackrel{2}{ C } H =\stackrel{1}{ C } H _2$

questions.explanation:
The equilibrium reaction for acid indicators is as follows:
{tex}H _{ In } \rightleftharpoons H ^{+}+ In ^{-}{/tex}
{tex}K _{ In }=\frac{\left[ H ^{+}\right]\left[ In ^{-}\right]}{\left[ H _{\text {In }}\right]}{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow\left[ H ^{+}\right]=\frac{ K _{\text {In }}[ HIn ]}{\left[ In ^{-}\right]}{/tex}
Take -log on both sides
{tex}-\log \left[ H ^{+}\right]=-\log \left(\frac{ K _{\text {In }}[ HIn ]}{\left[ In ^{-}\right]}\right) {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow pH =-\log K _{\text {In }}+\log \frac{\left[ In ^{-}\right]}{[ HIn ]}{/tex}
{tex}pH =p K _{\text {In }}+\log \frac{\left[\text { In }^{-}\right]}{[ HIn ]} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{\left[\text { In }^{-}\right]}{[ HIn ]}=p H -p K _{\text {In }}{/tex}

questions.question:
The values of Kp1 and Kp2 for the reactions
$X \rightleftharpoons Y+Z$ ...(i)
and $A \rightleftharpoons 2 B$ ...(ii)
are in the ratio 9 : 1. If degree of dissociation of X and A be equal, then total pressure at equilibrium (i) and (ii) are in the ratio:
questions.question_raw:
The values of Kp1 and Kp2 for the reactions
{tex}X \rightleftharpoons Y+Z{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}A \rightleftharpoons 2 B{/tex} ...(ii)
are in the ratio 9 : 1. If degree of dissociation of X and A be equal, then total pressure at equilibrium (i) and (ii) are in the ratio:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop X\limits_{1 - \alpha } }\limits_{} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop Y\limits_\alpha }\limits_{} + \mathop {\mathop Z\limits_\alpha ;}\limits_{} {/tex} {tex}K_{c_{1}}=\frac{[Y][Z]}{[X]}=\frac{\alpha^{2}}{(1-\alpha)} \cdot\left[\frac{P_{1}}{1+\alpha}\right]^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\mathop A\limits_{1 - \alpha } }\limits_{} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2B}\limits_\alpha ;}\limits_{} {/tex} {tex}K_{c_{2}}=\frac{[B]^{2}}{[A]}=\frac{4 \alpha^{2}}{(1-\alpha)} \cdot\left[\frac{P_{2}}{1+\alpha}\right]^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{c_{1}}}{K_{c_{2}}}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\alpha^{2} \times(1-\alpha)}{(1-\alpha) \times 4 \alpha^{2}} \times \frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\frac{36}{1}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M

questions.question:
The reaction,
2A(g) + B(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 3C (g) + D(g)
is begun with the concentration of A and B both at an initial value of 1.00M. When equilibrium is reached, the concentration of D is measured and found to be 0.25. The value for the equilibrium constant for this reaction is given by the expression:

2A(g) + B(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 3C (g) + D(g)
is begun with the concentration of A and B both at an initial value of 1.00M. When equilibrium is reached, the concentration of D is measured and found to be 0.25. The value for the equilibrium constant for this reaction is given by the expression:

{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Initial} }\limits_{At\;Eq.} \mathop {\mathop {2A(g)}\limits_{1.0} }\limits_{0.5} + \mathop {\mathop {2B(g)}\limits_{1.0} }\limits_{0.75} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {3C(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_{0.75} + \mathop {\mathop {D(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_{0.25} {/tex}
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{(0.25) \times(0.75)^{3}}{(0.5)^{2} \times(0.75)}{/tex}

questions.question: The hydrogen ion concentration of a 10-8 M HCl aqueous solution at 298 K (Kw = 10-14) is:
questions.question_raw: The hydrogen ion concentration of a 10-8 M HCl aqueous solution at 298 K (Kw = 10-14) is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}HCl\;\rightarrow\underset{10^{-8}}{H^+}\;+\;Cl^-{/tex}
{tex}H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons\underset{10^{-8}+a}{H^+\;}+\underset a{\;OH^-\;}{/tex}The dissociation of H2O is suppressed due to common ion effect
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kw = [H+] [OH-]
or 10-14 = (10-8 + a) {tex}\times{/tex} a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 0.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H-] = 10-8 + 0.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 = 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M

questions.question: Calculate the pOH of a solution at 25oC that contains 1 $\times$ 10-10 M of hydronium ions?
questions.question_raw: Calculate the pOH of a solution at 25oC that contains 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M of hydronium ions?
questions.explanation:
pH = -log [H+] = -log (10-10) = 10
At 25oC, pH + pOH = 14
10 + pOH = 14
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pOH = 14 - 10 = 4

questions.question: Equal volumes of three acid solutions of pH 3, 4 and 5 are mixed in a vessel. What will be the H+ ion concentration in the mixture?
questions.question_raw: Equal volumes of three acid solutions of pH 3, 4 and 5 are mixed in a vessel. What will be the H+ ion concentration in the mixture?
questions.explanation:
The problem is valid only if strong acids are mixed. After mixing equal volumes of three acids, total volume = 3 V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] after mixing = {tex}\frac{10^{-3} \times V}{3 V}+\frac{10^{-4} \times V}{3 V}+\frac{10^{-5} \times V}{3 V}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.11 \times 10^{-3}}{3}{/tex} = 3.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M

questions.question:
The value of $\Delta H$ for the reaction
X2(g) + 4Y2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2XY4(g) is less than zero
Formation of XY4(g) will be favoured at:
questions.question_raw:
The value of {tex}\Delta H{/tex} for the reaction
X2(g) + 4Y2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2XY4(g) is less than zero
Formation of XY4(g) will be favoured at:
questions.explanation: As {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0 i.e., the given reaction is exothermic. According to Le-Chatelier principle, for exothermic reaction, forward reaction is favoured when temperature becomes low. Also, there are 5 gaseous moles on reactant side and 2 gaseous moles on products side. So, forward reaction is favoured when pressure of the reaction mixture becomes high. The reason is that at high pressure reaction tends to more in direction where there is lessee number of gaseous moles.

questions.explanation: KH = {tex}\frac{K_{w}}{K_{b}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{1.77 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 5.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10

questions.question:
Given that the equilibrium constant for the reaction
2SO2(g) + O2( g ) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2SO3(g)
has a value of 278 at a particular temperature. What is the value of the equilibrium constant for the following reaction at the same temperature?
$\mathrm{SO}_{3}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g)$
questions.question_raw:
Given that the equilibrium constant for the reaction
2SO2(g) + O2( g ) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2SO3(g)
has a value of 278 at a particular temperature. What is the value of the equilibrium constant for the following reaction at the same temperature?
{tex}\mathrm{SO}_{3}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g){/tex}
questions.explanation:
2SO2 + O2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2SO3; K = 278
{tex}\mathrm{SO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} ; \quad K^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{K}}{/tex}
{tex}K^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{K}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{278}}=6 \times 10^{-2}{/tex}

questions.question: The dissociation constant for acetic acid and HCN at 25oC are 1.5 $\times$ 10-5 and 4.5 $\times$ 10-10 respectively. The equilibrium constant for the equilibrium, CN- + CH3COOH $\rightleftharpoons$ HCN + CH3COO- would be:
questions.question_raw: The dissociation constant for acetic acid and HCN at 25oC are 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 respectively. The equilibrium constant for the equilibrium, CN- + CH3COOH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HCN + CH3COO- would be:
questions.explanation:
CH3COOH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COO- + H+
Ka1 = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex} ...(i)
HCN {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + CN-
Ka2 = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HCN}]}{/tex} ...(ii)
By (i)/(ii)
K = {tex}\frac{K_{a_{1}}}{K_{a_{2}}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HCN}]\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{4.5 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 104

questions.question: The equivalent conductance of $\frac{M}{32}$ solution of a weak monobasic acid is 8.0 mhos cm2 eq-1 and at infinite dilution is 400 mhos cm2 eq-1. The dissociation constant of this acid is:
questions.question_raw: The equivalent conductance of {tex}\frac{M}{32}{/tex} solution of a weak monobasic acid is 8.0 mhos cm2 eq-1 and at infinite dilution is 400 mhos cm2 eq-1. The dissociation constant of this acid is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{v}}{\Lambda_{\infty}}=\frac{8}{400}{/tex} = 0.02
Now Ka = {tex}c \alpha^{2}=\frac{1}{32} \times{/tex} (0.02)2
= 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question:
Given the reaction between 2 gases represented by A2 and B2 to give the compound AB (g),
A2(g) + B2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2AB(g)
At equilibrium, the concentration
of $A_{2}=3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$
of $B_{2}=4.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$
of AB $=2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$
If the reaction takes place in a sealed vessel at 527°C, then the value of Kc will be:
questions.question_raw:
Given the reaction between 2 gases represented by A2 and B2 to give the compound AB (g),
A2(g) + B2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2AB(g)
At equilibrium, the concentration
of {tex}A_{2}=3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}{/tex}
of {tex}B_{2}=4.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}{/tex}
of AB {tex}=2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}{/tex}
If the reaction takes place in a sealed vessel at 527°C, then the value of Kc will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{[A B]^{2}}{\left[A_{2}\right]\left[B_{2}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left(2.8 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{3 \times 10^{-3} \times 42 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.62

questions.question: If pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12, the value of its Ksp is:
questions.question_raw: If pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12, the value of its Ksp is:
questions.explanation:
pH = 12
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 2
or [OH]- = 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ba2+] = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{2}{/tex}
Ksp Ba(OH)2 = [Ba2+] [OH-]2 = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{2} \times{/tex} (10-2)2
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M3

questions.question: What is [H+] in mol/L of a solution that is 0.20 M in CH3COONa and 0.10 M in CH3COOH? (Ka for CH3COOH is 1.8 $\times$ 10-5)
questions.question_raw: What is [H+] in mol/L of a solution that is 0.20 M in CH3COONa and 0.10 M in CH3COOH? (Ka for CH3COOH is 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
= -log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{0.2}{0.1}{/tex}
= 5.045
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6

questions.question:
For the reversible reaction:
N2(g) + 3H2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NH3(g) + heat
The equilibrium shifts in forward direction:
questions.question_raw:
For the reversible reaction:
N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3(g) + heat
The equilibrium shifts in forward direction:

options.text: by increasing the concentration of NH3(g)
by decreasing the concentrations of N2 (g) and H2(g)
by increasing pressure and decreasing temperature

questions.explanation:
According to Le-Chatelier's principle, the equilibrium shifts in that direction so as to oppose the applied change.
If concentration of NH3 increases then equilibrium shifts in backward direction to oppose the increased concentration of NH3.
If pressure is decreased then equilibrium shifts to the direction in which there are more number of gaseous moles are present. Thus, it also shifts in backward direction as there are 4 gaseous moles in reactant side and 2 moles in products side. If concentration of N2(g) and H2(g) decreases then equilibrium shifts to backward direction to increase the concentration of reactants. Ifpressure isincreased, equilibrium shifts to the side where lower number of gaseous moles are present i.e., forward direction. Also, when temperature is decreased i.e., the heat evolved in the reaction is consumed then reaction proceeds towards forward direction.

questions.question: In a buffer solution containing equal concentration of B- and HB, the Kb for B- is 10-10. The pH of buffer solution is:
questions.question_raw: In a buffer solution containing equal concentration of B- and HB, the Kb for B- is 10-10. The pH of buffer solution is:
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{C} \cdot \mathrm{B} .]}{[\mathrm{Acid}]}{/tex}
= pKw - pKb + log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{C.B.}]}{[\mathrm{Acid}]}{/tex}
pH = 14 - 10 = 4

questions.question: For a given exothermic reaction, Kp and K'p are the equilibrium constants at temperatures T1 and T2 respectively. Assuming that heat of reaction is constant in temperatures range between T1 and T2, it is readily observation that:(T2 > T1)
questions.question_raw: For a given exothermic reaction, Kp and K'p are the equilibrium constants at temperatures T1 and T2 respectively. Assuming that heat of reaction is constant in temperatures range between T1 and T2, it is readily observation that:(T2 > T1)
questions.explanation:
2.303 log {tex}\frac{K_{p}^{\prime}}{K_{p}}=\frac{\Delta H}{R} \frac{\left[T_{2}-T_{1}\right]}{T_{1} T_{2}}{/tex}
Option {tex}K_{p}>K_{p}^{\prime}{/tex} is correct only if T2 > T1.

questions.question: A buffer solution is prepared in which the concentration of NH3 is 0.30 M and the concentration of $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$ is 0.20 M. If the equilibrium constant, Kb for NH3 equals 1.8 $\times$ 10-5 what is the pH of this solution? (log 2.7 = 0.43)
questions.question_raw: A buffer solution is prepared in which the concentration of NH3 is 0.30 M and the concentration of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} is 0.20 M. If the equilibrium constant, Kb for NH3 equals 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 what is the pH of this solution? (log 2.7 = 0.43)
questions.explanation:
pOH = pKb + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate acid] }]}{[\text { base }]}{/tex}
= - log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{0.2}{0.3}{/tex}
= - log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log 0.66
= 4.7447 - 0.176 = 4.5685
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - 4.5686 = 9.43

questions.question:
In qualitative analysis, the metals of group I can be separated from other ions by precipitating them as chloride salts. A solution initially contains Ag+ and Pb2+ at a concentration of 0.10 M. Aqueous HCl is added to this solution until the Cl- concentration is 0.10 M. What will the concentrations of Ag+ and Pb2+ be at equilibrium?
(Ksp for AgCl = 1.8 $\times$ 10-7 M, [Pb2+] = 1.7 $\times$ 10-5)
questions.question_raw:
In qualitative analysis, the metals of group I can be separated from other ions by precipitating them as chloride salts. A solution initially contains Ag+ and Pb2+ at a concentration of 0.10 M. Aqueous HCl is added to this solution until the Cl- concentration is 0.10 M. What will the concentrations of Ag+ and Pb2+ be at equilibrium?
(Ksp for AgCl = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M, [Pb2+] = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)
questions.explanation:
For AgCl : [Ag+] [Cl-] = 1.8  {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
[Ag+] [0.1] = 1.8  {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
[Ag+] = 1.8  {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
For PbCl2 : [Pb2+] [Cl-]2 = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
[Pb2+] [0.1]2 = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
[Pb2+] = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question: If the equilibrium constant for N2(g) + O2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO(g) is K, the equilibrium constant for $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}(g)$ will be:
questions.question_raw: If the equilibrium constant for N2(g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO(g) is K, the equilibrium constant for {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}(g){/tex} will be:
questions.explanation:
For N2(g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO; {tex}K=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
For {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO} ; K^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{NO}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K^{\prime}=\sqrt{K}{/tex}

questions.question:
A 20 litre container at 400 K contains CO2 (g) at pressure 0.4 atm and an excess of SrO neglect the volume of solid (SrO). The volume of the container is now decreased by moving the movable piston fitted in the container. The maximum volume of the container, when the pressure of CO2 attains its maximum value,
(Given that: SrCO3 (s) $ \rightleftharpoons $ SrO(s) + CO2 (g) Kp = 1.6 atm)
questions.question_raw:
A 20 litre container at 400 K contains CO2 (g) at pressure 0.4 atm and an excess of SrO neglect the volume of solid (SrO). The volume of the container is now decreased by moving the movable piston fitted in the container. The maximum volume of the container, when the pressure of CO2 attains its maximum value,
(Given that: SrCO3 (s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} SrO(s) + CO2 (g) Kp = 1.6 atm)
questions.explanation:
SrCO3(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} SrO(s) + CO2(g); Kp = {tex}P_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}={/tex} 1.6 atm
The reaction should favour forward direction as Q < Kp, but since SrCO3 is not taken initially, the reaction cannot take place in forward direction.
SrO(s) + CO2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} SrCO3; Kp = {tex}\frac{1}{1.6}{/tex} = 0.625 atm
Thus on increasing pressure, volume of container will decrease till {tex}P_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}{/tex} becomes 1.6 atm having the same mole of CO2.
The reaction will attain equilibrium till P = 0.625
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Initial mole of CO2 {tex}=\frac{P V}{R T}=\frac{0.4 \times 20}{R T}=\frac{8}{R T}{/tex}
At eq. V {tex}=\frac{n R T}{P}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{R T} \times \frac{R T}{1.6}{/tex} = 5 litre

questions.question: Buffer solutions have constant acidity and alkalinity because:
questions.question_raw: Buffer solutions have constant acidity and alkalinity because:

options.text: acids and alkalies in these solutions are shielded from attack by other ions
these give unionised acid or base on reaction with added acid or alkali
they have large excess of H+ or OH- ions
they have fixed value of pH

questions.explanation:
Consider a buffer of CH3COOH + CH3COONa
Addition of Acid : {tex}H^++CH_3COO^-\rightarrow\underset{Weak\;acid}{CH_3COOH}{/tex}
Addition of alkali : {tex}OH^-+CH_3COOH\;\rightarrow\underset{Weak\;electrolyte}{H_2O}\;+\;CH_3COO^-{/tex}
Thus, addition of acid or alkali does not cause any change in pH.

questions.question: pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12. The value of the solubility product (Ksp) of Ba(OH)2 is:
questions.question_raw: pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12. The value of the solubility product (Ksp) of Ba(OH)2 is:

[OH-] = 10-2
Now Ba(OH)2 = Ba2+ + 2OH-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = [Ba2+ + [OH-]2
{tex}=\left[\frac{10^{-2}}{2}\right]{/tex} [10-2]2 {tex}\because{/tex} [(Ba2+) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} (OH-)]
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
