{tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} + 2H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2CO3 + 2OH-
Na2CO3 is salt of a strong base and weak acid. Thus pH > 7.

questions.question: Using the Gibbs energy change, $\Delta G^{\circ}$ = + 63.3 kJ, for the following reaction, Ag2CO3(s) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2Ag+(aq.) + $\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}$ (aq.) the Ksp of Ag2CO3(s) in water at 25oC is: (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1).
questions.question_raw: Using the Gibbs energy change, {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = + 63.3 kJ, for the following reaction, Ag2CO3(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2Ag+(aq.) + {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} (aq.) the Ksp of Ag2CO3(s) in water at 25oC is: (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1).
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = - 2.303 RT log Ksp
63300 = -2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 log Ksp
Ksp = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12

questions.question: The acidity of diprotic acids in aqueous solutions increases in the order:
questions.question_raw: The acidity of diprotic acids in aqueous solutions increases in the order:
questions.explanation: Bond length increases from H2S to H2Te. Larger is bond length more easily it breaks up.

questions.question:
2 mole N2 and 3 mole H2 gas are allowed to react in a 20 L flask at 400 K and after complete conversion of H2 into NH3, 10 L H2O was added and temperature reduced to 300 K. Pressure of the gas after reaction is:
N2 + 3H2 $\rightarrow$ 2NH3
questions.question_raw:
2 mole N2 and 3 mole H2 gas are allowed to react in a 20 L flask at 400 K and after complete conversion of H2 into NH3, 10 L H2O was added and temperature reduced to 300 K. Pressure of the gas after reaction is:
N2 + 3H2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NH3
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{N_2}}\limits_2 }\limits_1 + \mathop {\mathop {3{H_2}}\limits_3 }\limits_0 \to \mathop {\mathop {2N{H_3}}\limits_0 }\limits_2 {/tex}
NH3 is dissolved in water; The remaining gas is 1 mole of N2 having pressure {tex}P=\frac{n}{V} R T=\frac{1}{10} \times R \times 300=30 R{/tex}. Note that 10 litre H2O occupies 10 litre volume of container.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Vvessel left available for gas = 20 - 10 = 10 L

questions.question: For the reversible system: X(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ Y(g) + Z(g) a quantity of X was heated at constant pressure P at a certain temperature. The equilibrium partial pressure of X was found to be $\frac P7$. What is the value of Kp at given temperature?
questions.question_raw: For the reversible system: X(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Y(g) + Z(g) a quantity of X was heated at constant pressure P at a certain temperature. The equilibrium partial pressure of X was found to be {tex}\frac P7{/tex}. What is the value of Kp at given temperature?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop X\limits_a }\limits_{a - x} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop Y\limits_0 }\limits_x + \mathop {\mathop Z\limits_0 }\limits_x {/tex}
Total mole at equilibrium = a - x + x + x = a + x
Thus {tex}P_{X}^{\prime}=P \frac{(a-x)}{(a+x)}=\frac{P}{7}{/tex} {tex}\therefore 7 a-7 x=a+x, \therefore x=\frac{6 a}{8}=\frac{3 a}{4}{/tex}
Also {tex}K_{p}=\frac{x^{2}}{(a-x)} \cdot\left[\frac{P}{a+x}\right]=\frac{9 a^{2} \times 4}{16 \times a} \times \frac{P \times 4}{7 a}=\frac{9 P}{7}{/tex}

questions.question: In a reaction at equilibrium 'x' moles of the reactant A decompose to give 1 mole each of C and D. If the fraction of A decomposed at equilibrium is independent of initial concentration of A, then the value of 'x' is:
questions.question_raw: In a reaction at equilibrium 'x' moles of the reactant A decompose to give 1 mole each of C and D. If the fraction of A decomposed at equilibrium is independent of initial concentration of A, then the value of 'x' is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {xA}\limits_a }\limits_{a(1 - \alpha )} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop C\limits_0 }\limits_{\frac{{a\alpha }}{x}} + \mathop {\mathop X\limits_0 }\limits_{\frac{{a\alpha }}{x}} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K=\frac{[C][D]}{[A]^{x}}=\frac{\frac{a^{2} \alpha^{2}}{x^{2} V^{2}}}{\frac{[a(1-\alpha)]^{x}}{V^{x}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\alpha^{2} a^{2-x}}{x^{2}(1-\alpha)^{x} \cdot V^{2-x}}{/tex}
If {tex}\alpha{/tex} is independent of a, then 2 - x = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2

questions.question:
Amount of PCl5 (in moles) need to be added to one litre vessel at 250°C in order to obtain a concentration of 0.1 mole of Cl2 for the given change is:
PCl5 $ \rightleftharpoons $ PCl3 + Cl2; KC = 0.0414
questions.question_raw:
Amount of PCl5 (in moles) need to be added to one litre vessel at 250°C in order to obtain a concentration of 0.1 mole of Cl2 for the given change is:
PCl5 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl3 + Cl2; KC = 0.0414
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Initial\;moles} }\limits_{Moles\;at\;equilibrium} \mathop {\mathop {PC{l_5}}\limits_a }\limits_{(a - 0.1)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {PC{l_3}}\limits_0 }\limits_{0.1} + \mathop {\mathop {C{l_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_{0.1} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{c}=\frac{\frac{0.1}{1} \times \frac{0.1}{1}}{\frac{a-0.1}{1}}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} volume is 1 litre)
or {tex}0.0414=\frac{0.01}{(a-0.1)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 0.3415 moles

questions.question: If 340 g of a mixture of N2 and H2 in the correct ratio gave a 20% yield of NH3. The mass of NH3 produced would be:
questions.question_raw: If 340 g of a mixture of N2 and H2 in the correct ratio gave a 20% yield of NH3. The mass of NH3 produced would be:
questions.explanation: 20% yield of NH3 and thus 20% of 340 {tex}=\frac{20 \times 340}{100}{/tex} = 68 g

questions.question: A weak acid HX (Ka = 1 $\times$ 10-5) on reaction with NaOH gives NaX. For 0.1 M aqueous solution of NaX, the % hydrolysis is:
questions.question_raw: A weak acid HX (Ka = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) on reaction with NaOH gives NaX. For 0.1 M aqueous solution of NaX, the % hydrolysis is:
questions.explanation:
For NaX
{tex}\underset{1-h}{X^-\;}+\;H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons\;H\underset hX+O\underset h{H^-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore h=\sqrt{\frac{K_{H}}{c}}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a} \cdot c}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-5} \times 0.1}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{10^{-8}}{/tex} = 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} %h = 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 10-2 = 0.01

questions.question: The reaction that proceeds in the forward direction is:
questions.question_raw: The reaction that proceeds in the forward direction is:
questions.explanation: NH4Cl + NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O + NH3 + NaCl

questions.question: To neutralise completely 20 mL of 0.1 M aqueous solution of phosphorous acid H3PO3, the volume of 0.1 M KOH solution required is:
questions.question_raw: To neutralise completely 20 mL of 0.1 M aqueous solution of phosphorous acid H3PO3, the volume of 0.1 M KOH solution required is:
questions.explanation:
meq. of KOH = meq. of H3PO3
0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} V = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 (H3PO3 is dibasic)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v = 40 mL

questions.question: Hydrogen ion concentration in mol/L in a solution of pH = 5.4 will be:
questions.question_raw: Hydrogen ion concentration in mol/L in a solution of pH = 5.4 will be:
questions.explanation:
pH = 5.4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -log H+ = 5.4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 3.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 M

questions.question: For the reaction, 2NO2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO(g) + O2(g), $K_{c}=1.8 \times 10^{-6}$ at 185oC. At 185oC, the Kc for $\mathrm{NO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)$ is:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, 2NO2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO(g) + O2(g), {tex}K_{c}=1.8 \times 10^{-6}{/tex} at 185oC. At 185oC, the Kc for {tex}\mathrm{NO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g){/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{c_{1}}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}}=1.8 \times 10^{-6}{/tex}
Also, {tex}K_{c_{2}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{[\mathrm{NO}]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}K_{c_{1}}=\frac{1}{K_{c_{2}}^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}1.8 \times 10^{-6}=\frac{1}{K_{c_{2}}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K_{c_{2}}=7.5 \times 10^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: A quantity of PCl5 was heated in a 10 litre vessel at 250°C to show PCl5(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ PCl3(g) + Cl2(g). At equilibrium the vessel contains 0.1 mole of PCl5, 0.20 mole of PCl3 and 0.20 mole of Cl2. The equilibrium constant of the reaction is:
questions.question_raw: A quantity of PCl5 was heated in a 10 litre vessel at 250°C to show PCl5(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g). At equilibrium the vessel contains 0.1 mole of PCl5, 0.20 mole of PCl3 and 0.20 mole of Cl2. The equilibrium constant of the reaction is:
questions.explanation: {tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}=\frac{\frac{0.2}{10} \times \frac{0.2}{10}}{\frac{0.1}{10}}{/tex} = 0.04

questions.question: Solubility product of MX2 at 298 K is 4 $\times$ 10-12. At this temperature concentration of M2+ ion in mol per litre is:
questions.question_raw: Solubility product of MX2 at 298 K is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12. At this temperature concentration of M2+ ion in mol per litre is:
questions.explanation: Ksp = 4s3 or {tex}s=\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{4 \times 10^{-12}}{4}}{/tex} = 10-4

questions.question: The equilibrium constant for the reaction, N2 (g) + O2 (g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO(g) is 4 $\times$ 10-4 at 2000 K. In presence of a catalyst the equilibrium is attained ten times faster. Therefore, the equilibrium constant, in presence of the catalyst, at 2000 K is:
questions.question_raw: The equilibrium constant for the reaction, N2 (g) + O2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO(g) is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 at 2000 K. In presence of a catalyst the equilibrium is attained ten times faster. Therefore, the equilibrium constant, in presence of the catalyst, at 2000 K is:

options.text: difficult to compute without more data

questions.explanation: Kp and Kc values do not change with catalyst.

questions.question: 0.1 mole of CH3NH2 (Kb = 5 $\times$ 10-4) is mixed with 0.08 mole of HCl and diluted to 1 litre, The [H+] in solution is:
questions.question_raw: 0.1 mole of CH3NH2 (Kb = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) is mixed with 0.08 mole of HCl and diluted to 1 litre, The [H+] in solution is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{\underset{0.02}{0.1}}{CH_3NH_2}\;+\;\underset{\underset0{0.08}}{HCl}\;\rightarrow CH_3\underset{\underset{0.08}0}{NH_3^+Cl^-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log Kb + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate acid }]}{[\text { Base }]}{/tex}
= - log 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 + log {tex}\frac{[0.08]}{0.02}{/tex}
= 3.9031
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}\frac{10^{-14}}{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 M

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements is true?

options.text: HNO3 is a stronger acid than HNO2
HClO4 is a weaker acid than HClO3
In aqueous medium HF is a stronger acid than HCl
H3PO3 is a stronger acid than H2SO3

questions.explanation: Higher is the oxidation number of non-metal in oxoacid, more is acidic nature.

questions.question: At a certain temperature, 2HI $ \rightleftharpoons $ H2 + I2 only 50% HI is dissociated at equilibrium. The equilibrium constant is:
questions.question_raw: At a certain temperature, 2HI {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H2 + I2 only 50% HI is dissociated at equilibrium. The equilibrium constant is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{x^{2}}{4(1-x)^{2}}{/tex}
If, x = 0.5;
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{0.5 \times 0.5}{4 \times(0.5)^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 0.25

questions.question: The pKa of weak acid HA is 4.5. The pOH of an aqueous buffer solution of HA in which 50% of the acid is ionised:
questions.question_raw: The pKa of weak acid HA is 4.5. The pOH of an aqueous buffer solution of HA in which 50% of the acid is ionised:
questions.explanation:
pH = 4.5 + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} [Salt] = [Acid], since [A-] = [HA]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 4.5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 9.5

questions.question: The solubility product Ksp, of a sparingly soluble salt AgIO3 is 1.0 $\times$ 10-8 at a given temperature. What is the mass of AgIO3 (mol. mass = 283) contained in 100 mL solution at this temperature is:
questions.question_raw: The solubility product Ksp, of a sparingly soluble salt AgIO3 is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 at a given temperature. What is the mass of AgIO3 (mol. mass = 283) contained in 100 mL solution at this temperature is:
questions.explanation:
Ksp = s2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m/l = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 283 {tex}\times \frac{100}{1000}{/tex} mL
= 28.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g/100mL

questions.question: The first and second dissociation constant of an acid HA2 are 1 $\times$ 10-5 and 5.0 $\times$ 10-10 respectively. The overall dissociation constant of acid is:
questions.question_raw: The first and second dissociation constant of an acid HA2 are 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 respectively. The overall dissociation constant of acid is:
questions.explanation: K = K1 {tex}\times{/tex} K2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15

questions.question: Eight mole of a gas AB3 attain equilibrium in a closed container of volume 1dm3 as, 2 AB3 $ \rightleftharpoons $ A2 (g) + 3B2(g). If at equilibrium 2 mole of A2 are present then, equilibrium constant is:
questions.question_raw: Eight mole of a gas AB3 attain equilibrium in a closed container of volume 1dm3 as, 2 AB3 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} A2 (g) + 3B2(g). If at equilibrium 2 mole of A2 are present then, equilibrium constant is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{t = 0} }\limits_{At.\;equilibrium} \mathop {\mathop {2A{B_3}(g)}\limits_8 }\limits_{(8 - a)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {{A_2}(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_{at\;2} + \mathop {\mathop {3{B_2}(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_{\frac{{3a}}{2}} {/tex}
Thus, {tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[A_{2}\right]\left[B_{2}\right]^{3}}{\left[A B_{3}\right]^{2}}{/tex}; Also, {tex}\frac{a}{2}=2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a=4{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\left[A B_{3}\right]=\frac{4}{1} ;\left[A_{2}\right]=\frac{2}{1} ;\left[B_{2}\right]=\frac{6}{1}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}K_{c}=\frac{2 \times 6^{3}}{4^{2}}{/tex} = 27 mol2L-2

questions.question: The degree of dissociation of a weak acid is 1.34% at 0.1 M concentration. Its dissociation constant is:
questions.question_raw: The degree of dissociation of a weak acid is 1.34% at 0.1 M concentration. Its dissociation constant is:
questions.explanation: Ka = {tex}C \alpha^{2}{/tex} = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} (1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2)2 = 1.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: For the chemical reaction, 3X (g) + Y (g) $\rightarrow$ X3Y (g); the amount of X3Y at equilibrium is affected by:
questions.question_raw: For the chemical reaction, 3X (g) + Y (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} X3Y (g); the amount of X3Y at equilibrium is affected by:
questions.explanation: The reaction shows a change in mole during the course of reaction and thus, increase in pressure will favour forward reaction. Also Kp changes with temperature. Catalyst has no effect on Kp. Thus, P and T influence the equilibrium concentrations.

questions.question: The pKa of a weak acid HA, is 4.80. The pKb of a weak base, B OH, is 4.78. The pH of an aqueous solution of the corresponding salt, BA, will be:
questions.question_raw: The pKa of a weak acid HA, is 4.80. The pKb of a weak base, B OH, is 4.78. The pH of an aqueous solution of the corresponding salt, BA, will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{1-h}{B^+\;}+\;\underset{1-h}{A^-}\;+\;H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons B\underset HOH\;+\;H\underset hA{/tex}
HA {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + A-
[H+] = {tex}\frac{K_{a} \times[\mathrm{H} A]}{\left[A^{-}\right]}=\frac{K_{a} \times h}{1-h}=K_{a} \times \sqrt{K_{H}}{/tex}
{tex}=K_{a} \times \sqrt{\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a} \times K_{b}}}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w} \times K_{a}}{K_{b}}}{/tex}
or pH = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [pKb + pKa - pKb] = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [14 + 4.8 - 4.78]
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 14.02 = 7.01

questions.question:
For the reversible reaction,
N2(g) + 3H2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NH3(g) at 500°C, the value of Kp is 1.44 $\times$ 10-5 when partial pressure is measured in the atmosphere. The corresponding value of Kc, with concentration in mol litre-1, is:
questions.question_raw:
For the reversible reaction,
N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3(g) at 500°C, the value of Kp is 1.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 when partial pressure is measured in the atmosphere. The corresponding value of Kc, with concentration in mol litre-1, is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{p}=K_{c}(R T)^{\Delta n};{/tex} {tex}\Delta n{/tex} for the given reaction is 2 - 4 = -2
R = 0.082 L atm K-1 mol-1
Thus, {tex}K_{c}=\frac{K_{p}}{(R T)^{\Delta n}}=\frac{1.44 \times 10^{-5}}{(0.082 \times 773)^{-2}}{/tex}

questions.question:
Four species are listed below:
 $\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}$
 H3O+
 $\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$
 HSO3F
Which one of the following is the correct sequence of their acid strength?
questions.question_raw:
Four species are listed below:

  1. {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
  2. H3O+
  3. {tex}\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}
  4. HSO3F

Which one of the following is the correct sequence of their acid strength?
questions.explanation: HSO3F is strong acid. Also, H2O is neutral, its conjugate base is stronger and thus, H3O+ is stronger acid than {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}. Alos {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} is a stronger base of conjugate weak acid H2CO3 whereas {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} is weak base of strong conjugate acid. Thus, {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} is a stronger acid than {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}.

questions.question: Ksp of salt AB, AB2 and A3B are 4.0 $\times$ 10-8, 3.2 $\times$ 10-14 and 2.7 $\times$ 1015 respectively at temperature T. The solubility order of these salts in water at temperature T (in mol litre-1) is:
questions.question_raw: Ksp of salt AB, AB2 and A3B are 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8, 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 and 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 respectively at temperature T. The solubility order of these salts in water at temperature T (in mol litre-1) is:
questions.explanation:
Let solubility be s1, s2, s3 for AB, AB2 and A3B respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s1 = {tex}\sqrt{K_{s p}}=\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
s2 = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-14}}{4}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
s3 = {tex}\sqrt[4]{\frac{K_{s p}}{27}}=\sqrt[4]{\frac{2.7 \times 10^{-15}}{27}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M

questions.question: When two reactants A and B are mixed to give products C and D, the reaction quotient, Q at the initial stages of reaction:
questions.question_raw: When two reactants A and B are mixed to give products C and D, the reaction quotient, Q at the initial stages of reaction:
questions.explanation: At the initial stage if only reactants are taken Q = 0, but it goes increasing with time to become equal to Kc.

questions.question: 2.5 mL of $\frac{2}{5}$ M weak mono acidic base (Kb = 1 $\times$ 10-12 at 25oC) is titrated with $\frac{2}{15}$ M HCl in water at 25oC. The concentration of H+ at equivalent point is: (Kw = 1 $\times$ 10-14 at 25oC)
questions.question_raw: 2.5 mL of {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} M weak mono acidic base (Kb = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 at 25oC) is titrated with {tex}\frac{2}{15}{/tex} M HCl in water at 25oC. The concentration of H+ at equivalent point is: (Kw = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 at 25oC)
questions.explanation:
BOH + HCl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} BCl + H2O
At equivalent point: meq of BOH = meq of HCl
2.5 {tex}\times \frac{2}{5} \times{/tex} 1 = V {tex}\times \frac{2}{15}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 7.5 mL
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total volume = 2.5 + 7.5 = 10 mL
{tex}\therefore{/tex} meq of BCl formed = {tex}\frac{2.5 \times 2}{5}{/tex} = 1
[BCl] = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} = 0.1 M
B+ + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} BOH + H+
[H+] = c {tex}\cdot{/tex} h
Also, {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{1-h}=\frac{K_{H}}{C}=\frac{K_{w}}{K_{b} \times c}=\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-12} \times 0.1}{/tex} = 0.1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} h = 0.27
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 0.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2

questions.question: In which of the following equilibrium, change in volume of the system does not alter the number of moles?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following equilibrium, change in volume of the system does not alter the number of moles?
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta n=0{/tex}

questions.question: Solid Ba(NO3)2 is gradually dissolved in a 1.0 $\times$ 10-4 M Na2CO3 solution. At which concentration of Ba2+ precipitate of begins to form? Ksp of BaCO3 = 5.1 $\times$ 10-9)
questions.question_raw: Solid Ba(NO3)2 is gradually dissolved in a 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M Na2CO3 solution. At which concentration of Ba2+ precipitate of begins to form? Ksp of BaCO3 = 5.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9)
questions.explanation:
Ksp BaCO3 = 5.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
5.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 = [Ba2+] {tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2}\right]{/tex} = [Ba2+] {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ba2+] = [Ba(NO3)2] = 5.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question:
At constant temperature, the equilibrium constant (Kp) for the decomposition reaction.
N2O4 $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO2 is expressed by,
$K_{p}=\frac{\left(4 x^{2} P\right)}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}$
where P = pressure, x = extent of decomposition. Which of the following statement is true?
questions.question_raw:
At constant temperature, the equilibrium constant (Kp) for the decomposition reaction.
N2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2 is expressed by,
{tex}K_{p}=\frac{\left(4 x^{2} P\right)}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
where P = pressure, x = extent of decomposition. Which of the following statement is true?

options.text: Kp increases with increase of P
Kp increase with decrease of x
Kp remains constant with change in P or x
Kp increases with increases of x

questions.explanation: Kp is a characteristic constant for a given reaction and changes only with temperature,

questions.question:
Consider the following equilibrium in a closed container:
N2O4(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NO2(g)
At a fixed temperature, the volume of the reaction container is halved. For this change, which of the given statements holds true regarding the equilibrium constant (Kp) and degree of dissociation ($\alpha$)?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following equilibrium in a closed container:
N2O4(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2(g)
At a fixed temperature, the volume of the reaction container is halved. For this change, which of the given statements holds true regarding the equilibrium constant (Kp) and degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex})?

options.text: Kp does not change, but $\alpha$ changes
Kp changes, but $\alpha$ does not change

questions.explanation:
For the reaction,
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g){/tex}
Kc = {tex}\frac{4 a^{2} \alpha^{2}}{(a-a \alpha) \cdot V}{/tex}
where a is initial mole of N2O4 present in V litre and {tex}\alpha{/tex} is its degree of dissociation,
Also, {tex}K_{p}=K_{c}(R T)^{\Delta n}{/tex}
On reducing the volume of container to {tex}\frac{V}{2} \mathrm{~L}{/tex}, initial concentration of N2O4 in order to have Kc constant, a characteristic constant for a given reaction at a temperature.

questions.question: In which of the following reactions, an increase in pressure at constant temperature does not affect the moles at equilibrium?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following reactions, an increase in pressure at constant temperature does not affect the moles at equilibrium?
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta n=0{/tex}

questions.question: At 25oC, the solubility product of Mg(OH)2 is 1.0 $\times$ 10-11. At which pH, will Mg2+ ions start precipitating in the form of Mg(OH)2 from a solution of 0.001 M Mg2+ ions?
questions.question_raw: At 25oC, the solubility product of Mg(OH)2 is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11. At which pH, will Mg2+ ions start precipitating in the form of Mg(OH)2 from a solution of 0.001 M Mg2+ ions?
questions.explanation:
Ksp Mg(OH)2 = [Mg2+] [OH-]2
1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 = 0.001 {tex}\times{/tex} [OH-]2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 4
and pH = 10

questions.question:
For the reaction:
$\mathrm{CO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}(g)$, Kp/Kc is:
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction:
{tex}\mathrm{CO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}(g){/tex}, Kp/Kc is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta n=1-1-\frac{1}{2}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{p}=K_{c}(R T)^{-1 / 2}{/tex}

questions.question:
Predict the conditions for forward reaction on the basis of Le Chatelier’s principle for:
$2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(g) ; \Delta H=-198 \mathrm{kJ}$
questions.question_raw:
Predict the conditions for forward reaction on the basis of Le Chatelier’s principle for:
{tex}2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(g) ; \Delta H=-198 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex}

options.text: lowering of temperature as well as pressure
lowering the temperature and increasing pressure
any value of temperature and pressure
increasing temperature as well as pressure

questions.explanation: An increase in temperature favours an endothermic reaction whereas an increase in pressure favours the reaction showing a decrease in mole or volume.

questions.question: Which one of the following pairs of solution is not an acidic buffer ?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following pairs of solution is not an acidic buffer ?
questions.explanation: An acidic buffer is a mixture of a weak acid and its salt with a strong base. Among CH3COOH, H2CO3, H3PO4 and HCIO4. The HCIO4 is a strong acid while all other are weak acid thus, HCIO4 and NaClO4 does not constitute to form an acidic buffer.

questions.question:
The equilibrium constants of the following are:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$; K1
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}$; K2
$\mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$; K3
The equilibrium constant (K) of the reaction:
$2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \stackrel{K}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O};$ will be:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constants of the following are:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex}; K1
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}{/tex}; K2
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}; K3
The equilibrium constant (K) of the reaction:
{tex}2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \stackrel{K}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O};{/tex} will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}{/tex}; {tex}\frac{1}{K_{1}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}{/tex}; {tex}K_{2}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\frac{3 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \quad K_{3}^{3}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}}{2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \stackrel{K}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}K=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{5 / 2}}{/tex}
By multiplying (i), (ii), and (iii)
{tex}\frac{1}{K_{1}} \times K_{2} \times K_{3}^{3}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}} \times \frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]} \times \frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2} \times\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{5 / 2}}=K{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K=\frac{K_{2} \times K_{3}^{3}}{K_{1}}{/tex}

questions.question:
Which one of the following conditions will favour maximum formation of the product in the reaction,
$A_{2}(g)+B_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons X_{2}(g);$ $\Delta_{r} H=-X \mathrm{kJ}$?
questions.question_raw:
Which one of the following conditions will favour maximum formation of the product in the reaction,
{tex}A_{2}(g)+B_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons X_{2}(g);{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H=-X \mathrm{kJ}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
{tex}A_{2}(g)+B_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons X_{2}(g);{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H=-X \mathrm{kJ}{/tex}
On increasing pressure equilibrium shifts in a direction where volume or mole decreases i.e., forward direction.
On decreasing temperature equilibrium shifts in exothermic direction i.e., forward direction.
Thus, high pressure and low temperature favours maximum formation of product.

questions.question: What is the pH of the resulting solution when equal volumes of 0.1 M NaOH and 0.01 M HCl are mixed?
questions.question_raw: What is the pH of the resulting solution when equal volumes of 0.1 M NaOH and 0.01 M HCl are mixed?
questions.explanation:
meq. of HCl = 0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} V
meq. of NaOH = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} meq. of NaOH left = 0.1 V - 0.01 V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [NaOH] left = {tex}\frac{0.09 \mathrm{V}}{2 \mathrm{V}}{/tex} = 0.045 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log [OH-] = - log [0.045] = 1.35
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 12.65

questions.question: For the reaction: n1A(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ n2B(g), heat of reaction at constant pressure at 27°C is 2 kcal less than heat of reaction at constant volume, then:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction: n1A(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} n2B(g), heat of reaction at constant pressure at 27°C is 2 kcal less than heat of reaction at constant volume, then:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n R T{/tex}
{tex}\Delta H<\Delta U{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\Delta n=-\mathrm{ve}{/tex}
Now, {tex}K_{p}=K_{c}(R T)^{\Delta n}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{p}<K_{c}{/tex}

questions.question: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4, AgCl, AgBr and AgI are respectively, 1.1 $\times$ 10-12, 1.8 $\times$ 10-10, 5.0 $\times$ 10-13, 8.3 $\times$ 10-17. Which one of the following salts will precipitate last if AgNO3 solution is added to the solution containing equal moles of NaCl, NaBr, NaI and Na2CrO4?
questions.question_raw: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4, AgCl, AgBr and AgI are respectively, 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12, 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10, 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13, 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17. Which one of the following salts will precipitate last if AgNO3 solution is added to the solution containing equal moles of NaCl, NaBr, NaI and Na2CrO4?
questions.explanation:
From the Ksp values of the given salts calculate the solubility values. Salt having highest solubility will precipitate at last.
{tex}\underset{\mathrm s}{{\mathrm{Ag}}_2{\mathrm{CrO}}_4}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset{2\mathrm s}{2\mathrm{Ag}^+}+\underset{\mathrm s}{{\mathrm{CrO}}_4{}^{2-}{}}{/tex}
Ksp = (2s)2(s)
1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 = 2s3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{AgCl}}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Ag}^+}\;+\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Cl}^-}{/tex}
Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = s2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
{tex}\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{AgBr}}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Ag}^+}\;+\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Br}^-}{/tex}
Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 = s2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 0.71 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
{tex}\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{AgI}}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Ag}^+}\;+\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm I^-}{/tex}
Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 = s2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8
Hence, solubility of Ag2CrO4 is maximum thus, it will precipitate at last.

questions.question:
The equilibrium constants of given reactions are:
$\mathrm{HgCl}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HgCl}_{2}$; $K_{1}=3 \times 10^{6}$
$\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HgCl}_{3}^{-}$; K2 = 9.0
The equilibrium constant for disproportionation of HgCl2,
$2 \mathrm{HgCl}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HgCl}^{+}+\mathrm{HgCl}_{3}^{-}$ is:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constants of given reactions are:
{tex}\mathrm{HgCl}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HgCl}_{2}{/tex}; {tex}K_{1}=3 \times 10^{6}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HgCl}_{3}^{-}{/tex}; K2 = 9.0
The equilibrium constant for disproportionation of HgCl2,
{tex}2 \mathrm{HgCl}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HgCl}^{+}+\mathrm{HgCl}_{3}^{-}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{1}=3 \times 10^{-5}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{2}=9.0=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{3}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{3}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{HgCl}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{3}=\frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]} \times \frac{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}K_{3}=\frac{9}{3 \times 10^{6}}=3 \times 10^{-6}{/tex}

questions.question: MY and NY3, two nearly insoluble salts, have the same Ksp values of 6.2 $\times$ 10-13 at room temperature, which statements would be true in regard to MY and NY3?
questions.question_raw: MY and NY3, two nearly insoluble salts, have the same Ksp values of 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 at room temperature, which statements would be true in regard to MY and NY3?

options.text: The salts MY and NY3 are more soluble in 0.5 M KY than in pure water
The molar solubility of MY in water is less than that of NY3
The molar solubilities of MY and NY3 in water are identical
The addition of the salt of KY to solution of MY and NY3 will have no effect on their solubilities

questions.explanation:
Ksp = s . s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 = s2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}s=\sqrt{6.2 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex}
= 7.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 mol L-1

Ksp = s.(3s)3 = 27s4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 = 27s4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 3.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1
Hence, molar solubility of MY in water is less than that of NY3.

questions.question: 2 mole of A2 in one litre vessel are connected through a tube having a valve of negligible volume with 4 mole of B2 in 3 litre vessel at 27°C. The valve is opened to attain the equilibrium A2 + B2 $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2AB. If Kc = 4, what is the concentration in mole litre-1 of AB?
questions.question_raw: 2 mole of A2 in one litre vessel are connected through a tube having a valve of negligible volume with 4 mole of B2 in 3 litre vessel at 27°C. The valve is opened to attain the equilibrium A2 + B2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2AB. If Kc = 4, what is the concentration in mole litre-1 of AB?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{A_2}}\limits_2 }\limits_{2 - x} + \mathop {\mathop {{B_2}}\limits_4 }\limits_{4 - x} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2AB}\limits_0 }\limits_{2x} {/tex}
Total volume = 4 litre
{tex}K_{c}=4=\frac{[A B]^{2}}{\left[A_{2}\right]\left[B_{2}\right]}=\frac{\left(\frac{2 x}{4}\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{2-x}{4}\right) \times\left(\frac{4-x}{4}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}4=\frac{4 x^{2}}{x^{2}-6 x+8}{/tex}
or 24x = 32
{tex}\therefore \ x=\frac{32}{24}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} = 0.33
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2x = 0.66
Thus, {tex}[A B]=\frac{0.66}{4}{/tex} = 0.165 mole litre-1

questions.question: Among the following, the correct order of acidity is:
questions.question_raw: Among the following, the correct order of acidity is:
questions.explanation:
Greater is the oxidation number of a central atom in oxoacids more is the acidic nature of oxoacid.
{tex}\underset{+1}{HClO}<\underset{+3}{HClO_2}<\underset{+5}{HClO_3}<\underset{+7}{HClO_4}{/tex}
Note: In question, acidic strength should have been written in place of acidity.

questions.question:
Equimolar mixture of Cl2 and PCl3 at 250°C is allowed to attain equilibrium as
PCl3 (g) + Cl2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ PCI5(g)
If Kp = 2 atm-1, at what pressure, the conversion of 75% PCl3 takes place inPCl5?
questions.question_raw:
Equimolar mixture of Cl2 and PCl3 at 250°C is allowed to attain equilibrium as
PCl3 (g) + Cl2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCI5(g)
If Kp = 2 atm-1, at what pressure, the conversion of 75% PCl3 takes place inPCl5?
questions.explanation:
Let a mol of each PCl3 and Cl2 be taken to attain equilibrium
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Initial\;mol} }\limits_{Mol\,at\;eq.} \;\;\mathop {\mathop {PC{l_3}}\limits_a }\limits_{0.25a} + \mathop {\mathop {C{l_2}}\limits_a }\limits_{0.25a} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {PC{l_5}}\limits_0 }\limits_{0.75a} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{p}=\frac{0.75 a}{0.25 a \times 0.25 a} \times\left[\frac{P}{1.25 a}\right]^{-1}{/tex}
or {tex}2=\frac{0.75}{0.25 \times 0.25} \times \frac{1.25}{P}{/tex}
or P = 7.5 atm

questions.question: The percentage of pyridine (C5H5N) that forms pyridinium ion (C5H5N+H) in a 0.10 M aqueous pyridine solution (Kb for C5H5N = 1.7 $\times$ 10-9) is:
questions.question_raw: The percentage of pyridine (C5H5N) that forms pyridinium ion (C5H5N+H) in a 0.10 M aqueous pyridine solution (Kb for C5H5N = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9) is:
questions.explanation:
C5H5N + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C5H5N+H + OH-
For pyridine, C = 0.1 M and Kb = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
{tex}\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_{b}}{C}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{1.7 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1.7 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
%{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = 0.013

questions.question: The solubility of AgCl (s) with solubility product 1.6 $\times$ 10-10 in 0.1 M NaCl solution would be:
questions.question_raw: The solubility of AgCl (s) with solubility product 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 in 0.1 M NaCl solution would be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}AgCl\;\rightleftharpoons A\underset s{g^+}+\underset{s+0.1}{Cl^-}{/tex};
{tex}N\underset{\underset0{0.1}}{aCl(aq)}\rightarrow N\underset{\underset{0.1}0}{a^+(a}q)\;+\;C\underset{\underset{0.1}0}{l^-(a}q){/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Ksp = [Ag+] [Cl-]
1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = s {tex}\times{/tex} (s + 0.1)
= s {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 (s << 0.1)
s = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9

questions.question:
For the heterogenous equilibrium at 1 atm pressure:
CaCO3(s) $ \rightleftharpoons $ CaO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 1 atm then temperature is given by:
questions.question_raw:
For the heterogenous equilibrium at 1 atm pressure:
CaCO3(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CaO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 1 atm then temperature is given by:
questions.explanation:
Kp = 1, {tex}\therefore \Delta G^{\circ}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ T=\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{\Delta S^{\circ}}{/tex}

questions.question: Concentration of the Ag+ ions in a saturated solution of Ag2C2O4 is 2.2 $\times$ 10-4 mol L-1. Solubility product of Ag2C2O4 is:
questions.question_raw: Concentration of the Ag+ ions in a saturated solution of Ag2C2O4 is 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1. Solubility product of Ag2C2O4 is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}Ag_2C_2(s)\;\rightleftharpoons2\underset{2s}{Ag^+}C_2\underset s{O_4^{2-}}{/tex}
Given [Ag+] = 2s = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
s = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
Ksp = (2s)2 {tex}\times{/tex} s = 4s3
= 4(1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)3 = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12

questions.question: A mixture of N2 and H2 in the ratio of 1:3 attains equilibrium with NH3 when 50% of the original mixture has reacted. If total pressure at equilibrium is P, then partial pressure of NH3 at equilibrium is:
questions.question_raw: A mixture of N2 and H2 in the ratio of 1:3 attains equilibrium with NH3 when 50% of the original mixture has reacted. If total pressure at equilibrium is P, then partial pressure of NH3 at equilibrium is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{} }\limits_{Mole\,left} \mathop {\mathop {{N_2}}\limits_1 }\limits_{0.5} + \mathop {\mathop {3{H_2}}\limits_3 }\limits_{1.5} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2N{H_3}}\limits_0 }\limits_{1.0} {/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} 50% of 4 mole racts i.e., total 2 mole reacts of 1.3 i.e., {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mole of N2 and H2 reacts.]
Total mole at equilibrium = 0.5 + 1.5 + 1.0 = 3
{tex}\therefore P_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{3} \times P=\frac{P}{3}{/tex}

questions.question:
Following solutions were prepared by mixing different volumes of NaOH and HCl of different concentrations :
 60 ml $\frac{M}{10}$ HCl + 40 mL $\frac{M}{10}$
 55 ml $\frac{M}{10}$ HCl + 45 mL $\frac{M}{10}$
 75 ml $\frac{M}{5}$ HCl + 25 mL $\frac{M}{5}$
 100 ml $\frac{M}{10}$ HCl + 100 mL $\frac{M}{10}$
pH of which one of them will be equal to 1?
questions.question_raw:
Following solutions were prepared by mixing different volumes of NaOH and HCl of different concentrations :

  1. 60 ml {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex} HCl + 40 mL {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex}
  2. 55 ml {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex} HCl + 45 mL {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex}
  3. 75 ml {tex}\frac{M}{5}{/tex} HCl + 25 mL {tex}\frac{M}{5}{/tex}
  4. 100 ml {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex} HCl + 100 mL {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex}

pH of which one of them will be equal to 1?
questions.explanation:
meq. of HCl = 75 {tex}\times \frac{1}{5} \times{/tex} 1 = 15
meq. of NaOH = 25 {tex}\times \frac{1}{5} \times{/tex} 1 = 5
meq. of HCl left in resulting solution = 10
millimole of H+ left = 10
Molarity of [H+] in resulting mixture = {tex}\frac{10}{100}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
pH = -log [H+] = -log {tex}\left[\frac{1}{10}\right]{/tex} = 1.0

questions.question:
2 mole of N2 and 6 mole of H2 reacts to produce NH3. If 40% of the initial mixture reacts as:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
at constant pressure to attain equilibrium, the ratio of initial volume to the final volume of gases is:
questions.question_raw:
2 mole of N2 and 6 mole of H2 reacts to produce NH3. If 40% of the initial mixture reacts as:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex}
at constant pressure to attain equilibrium, the ratio of initial volume to the final volume of gases is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Initial\,mole} }\limits_{Final\;mole} \mathop {\mathop {\mathop {{N_2}}\limits_2 }\limits_{2 - 0.8} }\limits_{1.2} + \mathop {\mathop {\mathop {3{H_2}}\limits_6 }\limits_{6 - 2.4} }\limits_{3.6} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {\mathop {2N{H_3}}\limits_0 }\limits_{1.6} }\limits_{1.6} {/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} 40% of 8 mole reacts i.e., 3.2 mole reacts in the ratio of 1 : 3 i.e., 0.8 mole of N2 and 2.4 mole of H2 reacts
Total mole = 6.4
V {tex}\propto{/tex} mole at constant T
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{V_{i}}{V_{f}}=\frac{8}{6.4}=\frac{10}{8}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The solubility of BaSO4 in water is 2.42 $\times$ 103 gL-1 at 298 K. The value of its solubility product (Ksp) will be: (Given molar mass of BaSO4 = 233 g mol-1)
questions.question_raw: The solubility of BaSO4 in water is 2.42 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 gL-1 at 298 K. The value of its solubility product (Ksp) will be: (Given molar mass of BaSO4 = 233 g mol-1)
questions.explanation:
Solubility of BaSO4, s = {tex}\frac{2.42 \times 10^{-3}}{233}{/tex} (mol L-1)
= 1.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 (mol L-1)
{tex}BaSO_4(s)\rightleftharpoons\underset{(s)}{Ba^{2+}}(aq.)+\underset{(s)}{SO_4^{2-}}(aq.){/tex}
Ksp = [Ba2+] {tex}\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex} = s2
= (1.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)2
= 1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 mol2 L-2

questions.question_raw: Which of the following oxides is most acidic in nature?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{BeO}<\mathrm{MgO}<\mathrm{CaO}<\mathrm{BaO}}{\text { Basic character increases }}{/tex}
BeO is amphoteric oxide while other given oxides are basic.

questions.question:
For the equilibrium:
NH2COONH4 (s) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2NH3 (g) + CO2 (g)
NH3 (g) is added in equilibrium mixture such that partial pressure of NH3 becomes equal to the original total pressure. The ratio of total pressure at second equilibrium to the original equilibrium is:
questions.question_raw:
For the equilibrium:
NH2COONH4 (s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3 (g) + CO2 (g)
NH3 (g) is added in equilibrium mixture such that partial pressure of NH3 becomes equal to the original total pressure. The ratio of total pressure at second equilibrium to the original equilibrium is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}N{H_2}COON{H_4} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {2N{H_3}}\limits_{2p} + \mathop {C{O_2}}\limits_p {/tex} ...(I)
At eq.
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{p}=(2 P)^{2} \times P=4 P^{3}{/tex}
{tex}N{H_2}COON{H_4} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {2N{H_3}}\limits_{3p} + \mathop {C{O_2}}\limits_a {/tex} ...(II)
{tex}K_{p}=(3 P)^{2} \times a{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad a=\frac{4 P^{3}}{9 P^{2}}=\frac{4 P}{9}{/tex}
Thus total pressure II eq. {tex}=3 P+\frac{4 P}{9}=\frac{31 P}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{P_{\mathrm{II}}}{P_{\mathrm{I}}}=\frac{31 P}{9 \times 3 P}=\frac{31}{27}{/tex}

questions.question:
Which is the strongest acid in the following?
 HClO3
 HClO4
 H2SO3
 H2SO4
questions.question_raw:
Which is the strongest acid in the following?

  1. HClO3
  2. HClO4
  3. H2SO3
  4. H2SO4
questions.explanation: Acidic order: {tex}H\overset{+7}{Cl}O_4>H\overset{+5}{Cl}O_3>H\overset{+3}{Cl}O_2>H\overset{+1}{Cl}O{/tex}; Higher is the oxidation number of central non-metal in oxo-acids, more stronger is acid. Also, HClO4 is stronger acid than H2SO4.

questions.question: Zn salt is mixed with (NH4)2S of molarity 0.021 M. The mass of Zn2+ remains unprecipitated in 12 mL of this solution (Ksp ZnS = 4.57 $\times$ 10-24):
questions.question_raw: Zn salt is mixed with (NH4)2S of molarity 0.021 M. The mass of Zn2+ remains unprecipitated in 12 mL of this solution (Ksp ZnS = 4.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24):
questions.explanation:
[(NH4)2S] = 0.021 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [S2-] = 0.021 M
{tex}\because{/tex} At equilibrium [Zn2+] [S2-] = Ksp of ZnS
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Zn2+] = {tex}\frac{4.51 \times 10^{-24}}{0.021}{/tex} = 2.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Zn2+] left in solution = 2.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 {tex}\times{/tex} 65 g/litre
{tex}=\frac{2.15 \times 10^{-22} \times 65 \times 12}{1000}{/tex} g/12 mL
= 1.677 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 g/12 mL

questions.question: A certain ion B- has an Arrhenius constant for basic character (eq. constant 2.8 $\times$ 10-7). The equilibrium constant for Lowry-Bronsted basic character is:
questions.question_raw: A certain ion B- has an Arrhenius constant for basic character (eq. constant 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7). The equilibrium constant for Lowry-Bronsted basic character is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{Bronsted\;base}{B^{-\;}\;}+\;H^+\;\rightleftharpoons\;HB;{/tex} Kb = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HB}]}{\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\underset{Arrhenius\;base}{B^{-\;}\;}+\;H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons\;HB\;+\;OH^-;{/tex} Ka = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HB}]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]}{/tex} = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 ...(ii)
By (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{K_{a}}{K_{b}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HB}]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]} \times \frac{\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{[\mathrm{HB}]}{/tex}
= [H+] [OH-] = Kw ...(iii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kb = {tex}\frac{K_{a}}{K_{w}}=\frac{2.8 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-14}}{/tex} =  2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 107

questions.question: Acetic acid and propionic acid have Ka values 1.75 $\times$ 10-5 and 1.3 $\times$ 10-5 respectively at a certain temperature. An equimolar solution of a mixture of the two acids is partially neutralised by NaOH. How is the ratio of the contents of acetate and propionate ions related to the Ka values?
questions.question_raw: Acetic acid and propionic acid have Ka values 1.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 respectively at a certain temperature. An equimolar solution of a mixture of the two acids is partially neutralised by NaOH. How is the ratio of the contents of acetate and propionate ions related to the Ka values?

options.text: The ratio is unrelated to the Ka values
The ratio is unrelated to the pH of the solution
The ratio is related to the molarity
$\left(\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\right)=\frac{1.75}{1.3} \times\left(\frac{\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are ionised fraction of the acids

questions.explanation:
In a given mixture, the ionisation of two acids can be written as: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} be degree of ionisation at same concentration.
{tex}\underset{1-\alpha}{CH_3COOH}\;\rightleftharpoons C\underset\alpha{H_3C}OO^-+\underset{\alpha+\beta}{H^+}{/tex}
{tex}\underset{1-\beta}{C_2H_5COOH}\;\rightleftharpoons C_2\underset\beta{H_5C}OO^-+\underset{\alpha+\beta}{H^+}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K_{\mathrm{A.A.}}=\frac{[\alpha][\alpha+\beta] \cdot c}{[1-\alpha]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{P.A.}}=\frac{[\beta][\alpha+\beta] \cdot c}{[1-\beta]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{\mathrm{A.A}}}{K_{\mathrm{P.A.}}}=\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \times \frac{(1-\beta)}{\beta}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}=\frac{1.75}{1.3} \times\left[\frac{\beta}{1-\beta}\right]{/tex}

questions.question:
Acetone undergoes dissociation as:
CH3COCH3(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ C2H6(g) + CO(g)
If the initial pressure of acetone is 150 mm at TK and at equilibrium at 27 K. the mole fraction of CO is 1/3, the value of Kp for the reaction is:
questions.question_raw:
Acetone undergoes dissociation as:
CH3COCH3(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} C2H6(g) + CO(g)
If the initial pressure of acetone is 150 mm at TK and at equilibrium at 27 K. the mole fraction of CO is 1/3, the value of Kp for the reaction is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Initial\;mole} }\limits_{Final\;mole} \mathop {\mathop {C{H_2}COC{H_3}(g)}\limits_a }\limits_{a - b} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {{C_2}{H_6}}\limits_0 }\limits_b + \mathop {\mathop {CO(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_b {/tex}
Given {tex}\frac{b}{a+b}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
a = 2ab
PV = nRT
{tex}\therefore P \propto T{/tex}, Thus at 2T initial pressure becomes 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 150 = 300 mm before dissociation.
For acetone before dissociation {tex}300 \times V=a \times R \times 2 T{/tex}
After dissociation: {tex}P \times V=(a+b) \times R \times 2 T{/tex}
{tex}\frac{P}{300}=\frac{(a+b)}{a}=\frac{\left(\frac{3 a}{2}\right)}{a}{/tex}
P = 450 mm
{tex}\therefore \ P_{\mathrm{CO}}^{\prime}=450 \times \frac{b}{a+b}{/tex} = 150 mm
{tex}P_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}^{\prime}=450 \times \frac{b}{a+b}{/tex} = 150 mm
{tex}P_{\mathrm{CH}, \mathrm{COCH}_{3}}^{\prime}=\frac{a-b}{a+b} \times 450{/tex} = 150 mm
{tex}\therefore \ K_{p}=\frac{150 \times 150}{150}{/tex} = 150

questions.question: The ionisation constant of $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$ in water is 5.6 $\times$ 10-10 at 25oC. The rate constant for the reaction of $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$ and OH- to form NH3 and H2O at 25oC is 3.4 $\times$ 10-10 L mol-1 s-1. The rate constant for proton transfer from water to NH3 is:
questions.question_raw: The ionisation constant of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} in water is 5.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 at 25oC. The rate constant for the reaction of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} and OH- to form NH3 and H2O at 25oC is 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 L mol-1 s-1. The rate constant for proton transfer from water to NH3 is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}NH_3+H_2O\;\overset{k_f}{\underset{k_b}\rightleftharpoons}\;NH_4^++OH^-{/tex}; Kb = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1010
{tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NH4OH + H+; Ka = 5.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
{tex}K_{\text {baseNH }_{3}}=\frac{K_{f}}{K_{b}}{/tex}
Also, {tex}K_{\text {baseNH }_{3}}=\frac{K_{w}}{K_{\text {acid } \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}}}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} Kacid {tex}\times{/tex} Kbase = Kw)
or {tex}\frac{K_{f}}{3.4 \times 10^{10}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{5.6 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
or Kf = 6.07 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 sec-1

questions.question:
Indicate the correct answer out of the following for the reaction,
NH4Cl + H2O $ \rightleftharpoons $ NH4OH + HCl
questions.question_raw:

NH4Cl + H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} NH4OH + HCl

options.text: The reaction is favoured by the addition of NH4OH
The reaction is retarded by the addition of KOH
The reaction is retarded by the subtraction of hydrogen ion

questions.question: A solution of Na2CO3 is added drop by drop to 1 litre of a solution containing 10-4 mole of Ba2+ and 10-5 mole of Ag+, if Ksp for BaCO3 is 8.1 $\times$ 10-9 and Ksp for Ag2CO3, is 6.9 $\times$ 10-12, then which is not true:
questions.question_raw: A solution of Na2CO3 is added drop by drop to 1 litre of a solution containing 10-4 mole of Ba2+ and 10-5 mole of Ag+, if Ksp for BaCO3 is 8.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 and Ksp for Ag2CO3, is 6.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12, then which is not true:

options.text: a precipitate of Ag2CO3 will appear when $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]$ reaches 6.9 $\times$ 10-5 mol litre-1
no precipitate of BaCO3 will appear until $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]$ reaches 8.1 $\times$ 10-5 mol per litre
no precipitate of Ag2CO3 will appear until $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]$ reaches 6.9 $\times$ 10-2 mol per litre

questions.explanation:
For precipitation of Ag2CO3;
{tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]=\frac{K_{s p}}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}=\frac{6.9 \times 10^{-12}}{\left(10^{-5}\right)^{2}}{/tex},= 6.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 and for precipitation of BaCO3,
{tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]=\frac{K_{s p}}{\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right]}=\frac{8.1 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-4}}{/tex} = 8.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: The composition of an acidic buffer mixture made up of HA and NaA. of total molarity 0.29 having pH = 4.4 and Ka = 1.8 $\times$ 10-5 in terms of concentration of salt and acid respectively is:
questions.question_raw: The composition of an acidic buffer mixture made up of HA and NaA. of total molarity 0.29 having pH = 4.4 and Ka = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 in terms of concentration of salt and acid respectively is:
questions.explanation:
pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
LEt a mol litre-1 be a concentration of salt, then concentration of acid = (0.29 - a)
4.4 = -log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{a}{(029-a)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 0.09
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Conjugate base] = [Salt] = 0.09 M
[Acid] = 0.29 - 0.09 = 0.20 M

questions.question: To prepare a buffer of pH 8.26, amount of (NH4)2 SO4 to be added into 500 mL of 0.01 M NH4OH solution [pKa $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right)$ = 9.26] is:
questions.question_raw: To prepare a buffer of pH 8.26, amount of (NH4)2 SO4 to be added into 500 mL of 0.01 M NH4OH solution [pKa {tex}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right){/tex} = 9.26] is:
questions.explanation:
Mixing of NH4OH and (NH4)2SO4 gives a basic buffer.
pOH = pKb + log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]}{/tex};
{tex}\mathrm{p} K_{a}{ }_{\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}}{/tex} = 9.26 {tex}\therefore \mathrm{p} K_{b_{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}}}{/tex} = 4.74
5.74 = 4.74 + log {tex}\frac{a}{5}{/tex}; where a is millimole of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} obtained on mixing
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 50
{tex}\therefore{/tex} millimole of (NH4)2 SO4 = 25
or mole of (NH4)2 SO4 = 0.025

questions.question: Percentage ionisation of weak acid can be calculated using the formula:
questions.question_raw: Percentage ionisation of weak acid can be calculated using the formula:
questions.explanation:
For weak acid dissociation equilibria, degree of dissociation a is given as:
{tex}\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_{a}}{C}} \therefore \% \alpha=100 \sqrt{\frac{K_{a}}{C}}{/tex}
Also, Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \cdot C \alpha}{C(1-\alpha)}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \cdot \alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
log Ka = log H+ + log {tex}\frac{\alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
or pKa = pH + log {tex}\frac{(1-\alpha)}{\alpha}{/tex}
or pKa - pH = log {tex}\frac{(1-\alpha)}{\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}=10^{\mathrm{pK}_{a}-\mathrm{pH}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha}=10^{\mathrm{pK}_{a}-\mathrm{pH}}{/tex} + 1
or {tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{\left(1+10^{\mathrm{p} K_{a}-\mathrm{pH}}\right)}{/tex}

questions.question: A student wants to prepare a saturated solution of Ag+ ion. He has got only three samples AgCl (Ksp = 1.8 $\times$ 10-10), AgBr (Ksp = 5 $\times$ 10-13) and Ag2CrO4 (Ksp = 2.4 $\times$ 10-12). Which compound he should use to have maximum [Ag+]?
questions.question_raw: A student wants to prepare a saturated solution of Ag+ ion. He has got only three samples AgCl (Ksp = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10), AgBr (Ksp = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13) and Ag2CrO4 (Ksp = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12). Which compound he should use to have maximum [Ag+]?
questions.explanation:
{tex}s_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}}=s_{\mathrm{AgCl}}=\sqrt{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}=\sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
= 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
{tex}s_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}}=s_{\mathrm{AgBr}}=\sqrt{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}=\sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex}
= 7.07 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M
{tex}s_{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-12}}{4}}{/tex} = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
Also, {tex}s_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}}=2 \times s_{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}{/tex}
= 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
This will give maximum value.

questions.question: The self ionisation constant for pure formic acid, K = [HCOO$\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}$] [HCOO-] has been estimated as 10-6 at room temperature. The density of formic acid is 1.22 g/cm3. The percentage of formic acid molecules in pure formic acid are converted to formate ion:
questions.question_raw: The self ionisation constant for pure formic acid, K = [HCOO{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}{/tex}] [HCOO-] has been estimated as 10-6 at room temperature. The density of formic acid is 1.22 g/cm3. The percentage of formic acid molecules in pure formic acid are converted to formate ion:
questions.explanation:
Given density of formic acid = 1.22 g/cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of formic acid in 1 litre solution = 1.22 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 g
Thus, [HCOOH] {tex}\frac{1.22 \times 10^{3}}{46}{/tex} = 26.5 M
Since in case of auto ionisation
[HCOO{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}{/tex}] [HCOO-] and [HCOO-] [HCOO{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}{/tex}] = 10-6
[HCOO-] = 10-3
Now% dissociation of HCOOH
{tex}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}\right] \times 100}{[\mathrm{HCOOH}]}=\frac{10^{-3}}{26.5} \times{/tex} 100
= 0.004% {tex}\left[\alpha=\frac{C \alpha}{C(1-\alpha)}\right]{/tex}

questions.question:
Liquid ammonia ionises to a slight extent. At -50oC, its self ionisation constant, $K_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]$ = 10-30. How many amide ions, are present per cm3 of pure liquid ammonia?
Assume N = 6.0 $\times$ 1023.
questions.question_raw:
Liquid ammonia ionises to a slight extent. At -50oC, its self ionisation constant, {tex}K_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]{/tex} = 10-30. How many amide ions, are present per cm3 of pure liquid ammonia?
Assume N = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023.
questions.explanation:
{tex}2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}{/tex} (self ionisation) and K = {tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]=\sqrt{K}=\sqrt{10^{-30}}{/tex} = 10-15 M
Number of amide ions in 103 cm3 = 10-15 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
Number of amide ions in one cm3
{tex}=\frac{10^{-15} \times 6 \times 10^{23}}{10^{3}}{/tex}
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 ions

questions.question: What volume of 1.0 M sodium formate solution should be added to 50mL of 0.05 M formic acid to produce a buffer solution of pH = 4.0 (pKa of formic acid = 3.80)?
questions.question_raw: What volume of 1.0 M sodium formate solution should be added to 50mL of 0.05 M formic acid to produce a buffer solution of pH = 4.0 (pKa of formic acid = 3.80)?
questions.explanation:
LEt V mL of 0.1 M HCOONa be mised to 50 mL of 0.05 M HCOOH
{tex}\left[\because[\text { Molarity }]=\frac{\text { Total millimole }}{\text { Total volume }}\right]{/tex}
[HCOOH] = {tex}\frac{50 \times 0.05}{V+50}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In mixture [HCOONa] = {tex}\frac{0.1 \times V}{V+50}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4.0 = 3.80 + log {tex}\frac{(0.1 \times V) /(V+50)}{25 /(V+50)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 39.62 mL

questions.question: The ratio of dissociation constants of two weak acids HA and HB is 4. At what molar concentration ratio, the two acids will have same pH?
questions.question_raw: The ratio of dissociation constants of two weak acids HA and HB is 4. At what molar concentration ratio, the two acids will have same pH?
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{a}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{[\mathrm{H} A]}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} [H+] = [A-])
and {tex}K_{b}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[B^{-}\right]}{[H B]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{[\mathrm{H} B]}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} [H+] [B-])
Also, H+ are same
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{a}}{K_{b}}=\frac{[\mathrm{H} B]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HA}]}{[\mathrm{HB}]}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The volume of water needed to dissolve 1 g of BaSO4 (Ksp = 1.1 $\times$ 10-10) at 25oC is:
questions.question_raw: The volume of water needed to dissolve 1 g of BaSO4 (Ksp = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) at 25oC is:
questions.explanation:
Solubility of BaSO4 = {tex}\sqrt{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1.1 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
= 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} wt. of BaSO4 = 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 233
or {tex}w_{\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}}{/tex} = 244.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 g/litre
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of water needed to dissolve 1 g BaSO4 is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{24437 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 410 litre

questions.question: 50% neutralisation of a solution of formic acid (Ka = 2 $\times$ 10-4) with NaOH would result in a solution having a hydrogen ion concentration of:
questions.question_raw: 50% neutralisation of a solution of formic acid (Ka = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) with NaOH would result in a solution having a hydrogen ion concentration of:
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
pH = pKa + log 1 ({tex}\because{/tex} 50% neutralisation)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -log H+ = -log 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
or H+ = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M

questions.question: pH of a mixture of 1 M benzoic acid (pKa = 4.20) and 1 M sodium benzoate is 4.5. In 300 mL buffer solution, volume of benzoic acid is (log 2 = 0.3):
questions.question_raw: pH of a mixture of 1 M benzoic acid (pKa = 4.20) and 1 M sodium benzoate is 4.5. In 300 mL buffer solution, volume of benzoic acid is (log 2 = 0.3):
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{\left(300-V_{1}\right) \times 1}{1 \times V_{1}}{/tex}; Let V1 be volume of benzoic acid and thus (300 - V1) is volume of benzoate
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 45 = 4.2 + log{tex}\frac{300-V_{1}}{V_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{300-V_{1}}{V_{1}}{/tex} = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V1 = 100 mL

questions.question: If the equilibrium constant for the reaction of weak acid HA with strong base is 109, then pH of 0.1 M NaA is:
questions.question_raw: If the equilibrium constant for the reaction of weak acid HA with strong base is 109, then pH of 0.1 M NaA is:
questions.explanation:
HA + NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} NaA + H2O;
or HA + OH- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A- + H2O
Keq. = 109 = {tex}\frac{\left[A^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
Also, HA {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + A-
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{\mathrm{eq.}}}{K_{a}}=\frac{1}{K_{w}}{/tex} or Ka = 109 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 = 10-5
Thus for A- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HA + OH-
[OH-] = Ch = {tex}\mathcal{C} \sqrt{\frac{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{C}}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w} \cdot C}{K_{a}}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14} \times 0.1}{10^{-5}}}{/tex} = 10-5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-9
and pH = 9

questions.question:
$\Delta G_{f}^{\circ}$ for N2O4 and NO2 are 23.49 and 12.39 kcal at 27°C. The equilibrium constant Kp for the given reaction at 27°C is:
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)$
questions.question_raw:
{tex}\Delta G_{f}^{\circ}{/tex} for N2O4 and NO2 are 23.49 and 12.39 kcal at 27°C. The equilibrium constant Kp for the given reaction at 27°C is:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g){/tex}
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} for the change {tex}=2 \times \Delta G_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}^{\circ},-\Delta G_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=2 \times 12.39-23.49{/tex}
= 1.29 kcal
Now {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -2.303 RT log Kp
{tex}1.29 \times 10^{3}=-2.303 \times 2 \times 300 \log K_{p}{/tex}
log Kp = 0.94
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kp = 0.1148

questions.question: A solution is a mixture of 0.05 M NaCl and 0.05 M Nal. The concentration of iodide ion in the solution when AgCl just starts precipitating is equal to: (Ksp AgCI = 1 $\times$ 10-10 M2 : Ksp AgI = 4 $\times$ 10-16 M2)
questions.question_raw: A solution is a mixture of 0.05 M NaCl and 0.05 M Nal. The concentration of iodide ion in the solution when AgCl just starts precipitating is equal to: (Ksp AgCI = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M2 : Ksp AgI = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 M2)
questions.explanation:
For precipitation
[I-] [Ag+] = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16
{tex}\because{/tex} [I-] = 0.05 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ag+] = {tex}\frac{4}{5} \times{/tex} 10-14 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15
[CI-] [Ag+] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
{tex}\because{/tex} [CI-] = 0.05 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ag+] = {tex}\frac{1}{5} \times{/tex} 10-8 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
Thus [I-] needed for precipitation is equal to 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 and it will continue up to the stage when AgCI begins to precipitate, i.e., 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 becomes the conc. of [Ag+]
Thus [I-] left = {tex}\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-16}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M

questions.question: 10 mL of 10-6 M HCI solution is mixed with 90 mL H2O. pH will change approximately:
questions.question_raw: 10 mL of 10-6 M HCI solution is mixed with 90 mL H2O. pH will change approximately:
questions.explanation:
pH of 10-6 M HCl = 6; on dilution
[HCl] = {tex}\frac{10^{-6} \times 10}{100}{/tex} = 10-7
Thus new pH of HCl is not 7 because it is acid.
For new solution,
pH : [H+] = 10-7 + 10-7 (from H2O) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 6.7

questions.question: In an aqueous solution of volume 500 mL, when the reaction of 2Ag+ + Cu $ \rightleftharpoons $ Cu2+ + 2Ag, reached equilibrium the [Cu2+] was xM. When 500 mL of water is further added, at the equilibrium [Cu2+] will be:
questions.question_raw: In an aqueous solution of volume 500 mL, when the reaction of 2Ag+ + Cu {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Cu2+ + 2Ag, reached equilibrium the [Cu2+] was xM. When 500 mL of water is further added, at the equilibrium [Cu2+] will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}{\mathop {2Ag}\limits_{a.M} ^ + } + Cu \rightleftharpoons \mathop {C{u^{2 + }}}\limits_{xM} + \mathop {2Ag}\limits_{if\;V\;is\;500\;mL} {/tex}
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}=\frac{x}{(a)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
if volume is doubled, then for to have KC constant [Cu2+] will be less than {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex}. i.e., {tex}\frac{x}{4}{/tex}

questions.question:
Kc for the reaction:
[Ag(CN)2]- $ \rightleftharpoons $ Ag+ + 2CN-, the equilibrium constant at 25°C is 4.0 $\times$10-19 then the silver ion concentration in a solution which was originally 0.1 molar in KCN and 0.03 molar in AgNO3 is:
questions.question_raw:
Kc for the reaction:
[Ag(CN)2]- {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Ag+ + 2CN-, the equilibrium constant at 25°C is 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 then the silver ion concentration in a solution which was originally 0.1 molar in KCN and 0.03 molar in AgNO3 is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {2KCN}\limits_{0.1} }\limits_{(0.1 - 0.06)} + \mathop {\mathop {AgN{O_3}}\limits_{0.03} }\limits_0 \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {Ag(CN)_2^ - }\limits_0 }\limits_{0.03} + \mathop {\mathop {KN{O_3}}\limits_0 }\limits_{0.03} + \mathop {\mathop {{K^ + }}\limits_0 }\limits_{0.03} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}^{-}\right]=0.03{/tex}
Now {tex}\begin{array}{ccc} \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}^{-} & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+ & 2 \mathrm{CN}^{-} \\ 0.03 & 0 & 0.04 \\ (0.03-a) & a & (0.04+a) \end{array}{/tex} (left from KCN)
KC is small
{tex}\therefore \ 0.03-a \approx 0.03{/tex} and {tex}0.04+a \approx 0.04{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{c}=4 \times 10^{-19}=\frac{(0.04)^{2} \times a}{0.03}{/tex}; {tex}\therefore a=7.5 \times 10^{-18}{/tex}

questions.question:
For a given reaction:
[Cu(NH3)4]2+ + H2O $ \rightleftharpoons $ [Cu(NH3)3 • H2O]2+ + NH3 net rate of reaction is = 2.0 $\times$10-4s-1[Cu(NH3)4]2+ [H2O] - 3.0 $\times$ 105 L mol-1 s-1 [Cu(NH3)3H2O]2+ [NH3]. What is the value of equilibrium constant for the given reaction?
questions.question_raw:
For a given reaction:
[Cu(NH3)4]2+ + H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} [Cu(NH3)3 • H2O]2+ + NH3 net rate of reaction is = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex}10-4s-1[Cu(NH3)4]2+ [H2O] - 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 L mol-1 s-1 [Cu(NH3)3H2O]2+ [NH3]. What is the value of equilibrium constant for the given reaction?
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{f}=2.0 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}K_{b}=3.0 \times 10^{5}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}K_{\mathrm{eq.}}=\frac{K_{f}}{K_{b}}=\frac{2.0 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{3.0 \times 10^{5} \text { litre } \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{/tex}
{tex}=0.66 \times 10^{-9}{/tex} mol litre-1

questions.question: In an experiment to determine the enthalpy of neutralisation of sodium hydroxide with sulphuric acid, 50 cm3 of 0.4 M sodium hydroxide were titrated thermometrically with 0.25 M sulphuric acid. Which of the following plots gives the correct representation:
questions.question_raw: In an experiment to determine the enthalpy of neutralisation of sodium hydroxide with sulphuric acid, 50 cm3 of 0.4 M sodium hydroxide were titrated thermometrically with 0.25 M sulphuric acid. Which of the following plots gives the correct representation:

meq. of H2SO4 needed = meq. of NaOH = 20. Let volume of H2SO4 needed = VmL

questions.question: In the reaction C(s) + CO2 (g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2CO(g), the equilibrium pressure is 12 atm. If 50% of CO2 reacts, Kp for the change is:
questions.question_raw: In the reaction C(s) + CO2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2CO(g), the equilibrium pressure is 12 atm. If 50% of CO2 reacts, Kp for the change is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total moles = 1.5 and {tex}\Delta n=1{/tex}
Total pressure given equilibrium = 12 atm
{tex}K_{p}=\frac{\left(n_{\mathrm{CO}}\right)^{2}}{\left(n_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}\right)} \times\left[\frac{P}{\Sigma n}\right]^{\Delta n}=\frac{(1)^{2}}{0.5} \times\left(\frac{12}{1.5}\right)^{1}{/tex}
{tex}K_{p}=\frac{12}{1.5 \times 0.5}{/tex} = 16 atm

questions.question: When 20 g of CaCO3 were put into 10 litre flask and heated to 800°C, 35% of CaCO3 remained unreacted at equilibrium. Kp for decomposition of CaCO3 is:
questions.question_raw: When 20 g of CaCO3 were put into 10 litre flask and heated to 800°C, 35% of CaCO3 remained unreacted at equilibrium. Kp for decomposition of CaCO3 is:
questions.explanation:
Moles of CO2 formed {tex}=\frac{20 \times 65}{10^{4}}=1.3 \times 10^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\because \quad P V=n R T{/tex}
{tex}p_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=\frac{1.3 \times 10^{-1}}{10}{/tex}{tex}\times 0.0821 \times 1073{/tex} = 1.145 atm
Now {tex}K_{p}=p_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}^{\prime}{/tex} = 1.145 atm

questions.question: How many mole of HCl are required to prepare one litre of buffer solution (containing NaCN + HCl) of pH 8.5 using 0.01 g formula weight of NaCN (KHCN = 4.1 $\times$ 10-10)?
questions.question_raw: How many mole of HCl are required to prepare one litre of buffer solution (containing NaCN + HCl) of pH 8.5 using 0.01 g formula weight of NaCN (KHCN = 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10)?
questions.explanation:
NaCN + HCl is not a buffer but if HCl is in less amount then, it gives a buffer as it produces HCN.

This is buffer of HCN + NaCN

8.5 = -log 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 + log {tex}\frac{0.010-a}{a}{/tex}

questions.question:
When CO2 dissolves in water the following equilibrium is established.
CO2 + 2H2O $ \rightleftharpoons $ H3O+ + $\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}$ for which the equilibrium constant is 3.8 $\times$ 10-7 and pH = 6.0. The ratio of $\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]$/ [CO2] is:
questions.question_raw:
When CO2 dissolves in water the following equilibrium is established.
CO2 + 2H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex}  H3O+ + {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} for which the equilibrium constant is 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 and pH = 6.0. The ratio of {tex}\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]{/tex}/ [CO2] is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{p}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}{/tex} = 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
and [H3O+] = 10-6
{tex}\therefore \frac{\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}=\frac{3.8 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-6}}{/tex}
= 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 = 0.38

questions.question:
The dissociation equilibrium of a gas AB2 can be represented as:
2AB2(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2AB(g) + B2(g)
The degree of dissociation is x and is small compared to 1. The expression relating the degree of dissociation (x) with equilibrium constant Kp and total pressure P is:
questions.question_raw:
The dissociation equilibrium of a gas AB2 can be represented as:
2AB2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2AB(g) + B2(g)
The degree of dissociation is x and is small compared to 1. The expression relating the degree of dissociation (x) with equilibrium constant Kp and total pressure P is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {2A{B_2}}\limits_1 }\limits_{1 - x} (g) \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2AB(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_x + \mathop {\mathop {{B_2}(g)}\limits_0 }\limits_\frac {x}{2} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore K_{P}=\frac{x^{2} \cdot x}{2(1-x)^{2}} \cdot\left[\frac{P}{1+\frac{x}{2}}\right]^{1}=\frac{x^{3} \cdot P}{2}{/tex} (1 - x {tex}\approx{/tex} 1 and 1 + {tex}\frac{x}{2}={/tex} 1, since c << 1)
or {tex}x=\sqrt[3]{\frac{2 K_{P}}{P}}{/tex}

questions.question:
The % yield of ammonia as a function of time in the reaction
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g), \Delta H<0$
at (P, T1) is given below:
If this reaction is conducted at (P, T2), with T2 > T1, the % yield of ammonia as a function of time is represented by:
questions.question_raw:
The % yield of ammonia as a function of time in the reaction
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g), \Delta H<0{/tex}
at (P, T1) is given below:

If this reaction is conducted at (P, T2), with T2 > T1, the % yield of ammonia as a function of time is represented by:
questions.explanation: The combination of N2 and H2 to give NH3 takes place at slower rate if temperature is reduced to 473 K (200°C) because fewer energetic collisions occurs between molecules, however yield of NH3 is 88%. On the other hand, the combination occurs at faster rate at 723 K (450°C) but the yield of NH3 is 15%. Actually it is the number of energetic collisions which really matters to lead a chemical reaction and the rate of reaction is reduced at low temperature (473 K). The 15% yield of NH3 is better at 723 K because equilibrium is achieved earlier. The NH3 formed is dissolved in water and is drained off as NH3(aq) from the system so that unreacted N2 and H2 recombines again to produce NH3 in order to maintain constant value of Kc. Thus, all the N2 and H2 are converted to NH3 with a faster rate.

questions.question: Which of the following lines correctly show the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant K, for an exothermic reaction?

{tex}\because \ K=A e^{-\frac{\Delta H}{R T}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ \ln K=\ln A-\frac{\Delta H}{R} \times \frac{1}{T}{/tex}
Slope {tex}=-\frac{\Delta H}{R}{/tex}
Since {tex}\Delta H{/tex} -ve and thus slope is positive. Positive slope appears in lines represented by A and B.

 (H+ is catalyst)
