questions.question: Write pressure in terms of concentration and temperature.
questions.question_raw: Write pressure in terms of concentration and temperature.
questions.explanation:
P = CRT
We all know that the ideal gas equation is
PV = nRT;
P = nRT/V;
P = CRT here N/v is concentration, P is the pressure, V is the volume, n is the number of moles, C is the concentration, R is the universal gas constant and T is the temperature.

questions.question: The pH of 0.05 M aqueous solution of weak base, BOH is 12.0. Calculate Kb.
questions.question_raw: The pH of 0.05 M aqueous solution of weak base, BOH is 12.0. Calculate Kb.
questions.explanation:
BOH + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} BH+ + OH-
Initial (M) 1 - 0 0
Change (M) {tex}-\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}
Equilibrium (M) C(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) - C{tex}\alpha{/tex} C{tex}\alpha{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 10-2 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{0.05}{/tex} = 0.2
Now, Kb = {tex}\frac{C \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}=\frac{0.05 \times(0.2)^{2}}{1-0.2}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction:
2HI(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ H2(g) + I2(g) (Kc = 1.00 $\times$ 10-2)
If you start the reaction with 1 mol of HI, 0.371 mol of H2 and 0.750 mol of I2 in a 1L flask, the reaction will ________.
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following reaction:
2HI(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2(g) + I2(g) (Kc = 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2)
If you start the reaction with 1 mol of HI, 0.371 mol of H2 and 0.750 mol of I2 in a 1L flask, the reaction will ________.

options.text: produce more reactants, since Q > Kc
produce more products, since Q < Kc
produce more reactants, since Q < Kc
produce more products, since Q > Kc

questions.explanation:
Reaction: 2HI {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2 + I2
Q = {tex}\frac{\left[{H}_{2}\right]\left[{I}_{2}\right]}{[{HI}]^{2}}=\frac{0.371 \times 0.750}{(1)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.278
Kc = 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Q > Kc
The reaction will proceed in backward direction to form more reactants.

questions.question:
Given the following reactions, find K3.
 N2 + O2 $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NO ... K1 = 4.3 $\times$ 10-25
 2NO + O2 $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NO2 ... K2 = 6.4 $\times$ 109
 2NO + 2NO2 $\rightleftharpoons$ 3O2 + 2N2 K3 = ?
questions.question_raw:
Given the following reactions, find K3.

  1. N2 + O2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO ... K1 = 4.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-25
  2. 2NO + O2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2 ... K2 = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 109
  3. 2NO + 2NO2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 3O2 + 2N2 K3 = ?
questions.explanation:
Reverse (i): 2NO2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} N2 + O2 ... (i)
K1 = 2.32 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024
Reverse (ii): 2NO2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO + O2 ... (ii)
K2 = 1.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
Multiply (i) by 2,
4NO {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2N2 + 2O2 ... (iii)
K1 = 5.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 1048
Add (ii + iii)
2NO2 + 2NO {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 3O2 + 2N2
K3 = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1038

questions.question: In the reversible reaction A + B $\rightleftharpoons$ C + D, the concentration of each C and D at equilibrium is 0.4 mol L-1 and concentration of each A and B is 0.6 mol L-1, then the equilibrium constant K will be ________.
questions.question_raw: In the reversible reaction A + B {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C + D, the concentration of each C and D at equilibrium is 0.4 mol L-1 and concentration of each A and B is 0.6 mol L-1, then the equilibrium constant K will be ________.
questions.explanation:
Balanced equation: A + B {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C + D
Equilibrium constant, K = {tex}\frac{[{C}][{D}]}{[{A}][{B}]}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.4 \times 0.4}{0.6 \times 0.6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}

questions.question:
For the reaction:
N2(g) + 3H2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NH3(g) at 472oC
Kp = 2.79 $\times$ 10-5
Calculate the value of Kc.
questions.question_raw:

N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g) at 472oC
Kp = 2.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

{tex}\triangle{/tex}n = 2 - 4 = -2

questions.question: For the reaction A(g) + B(s) $\rightleftharpoons$ C(g) + D(g), Kp = 1.61 $\times$ 103 and Kc = 49. At what temperature is the reaction being carried out?

questions.explanation:

Kp = Kc(RT){tex}\triangle n{/tex}
1.61 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = 49(0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} T)1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T = 400 K
T = 127oC

questions.question:
At certain temperature, the equilibrium constant (Kc) is 4 for the reaction: SO2(g) + NO2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ SO3(g) + NO(g)
If we take one mole of each of the four gases in one-liter container, what will be the equilibrium concentration of NO?
questions.question_raw:
At certain temperature, the equilibrium constant (Kc) is 4 for the reaction: SO2(g) + NO2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} SO3(g) + NO(g)
If we take one mole of each of the four gases in one-liter container, what will be the equilibrium concentration of NO?
questions.explanation:
Chemical equation is:

  SO2(g) + NO2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} SO3(g ) + NO(g)
Initial (M) 1 1 1 1
Change (M) -x -x +x +x
Equilibrium (M) 1 - x 1 - x 1 + x 1 + x

Kc = {tex}\frac{\left[{SO}_{3}\right][{NO}]}{\left[{SO}_{2}\right]\left[{NO}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4 = {tex}\frac{(1+x)(1+x)}{(1-x)(1-x)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4 = {tex}\frac{(1+x)^{2}}{(1-x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 = {tex}\frac{1+x}{1-x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 + x = 2 - 2x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3x = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 0.33
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [NO] = 1 + x = 1 + 0.33 = 1.33 M

questions.question:
Self ionization of formic acid at room temperature is:
[HCOOH] $\rightleftharpoons$ $\left[{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]$ [HCOO-]
If ionization constant is 10-6 and density of HCOOH is 1.22 g/cm3. What percentage of formic acid molecules are converted to formate ion?
questions.question_raw:
Self ionization of formic acid at room temperature is:
[HCOOH] {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\left[{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex} [HCOO-]
If ionization constant is 10-6 and density of HCOOH is 1.22 g/cm3. What percentage of formic acid molecules are converted to formate ion?
questions.explanation:
Density of HCOOH = 1.22 g/cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Weight of HCOOH in one litre solution = 1.22 {tex}\times{/tex} 103g
Thus [HCOOH] = {tex}\frac{1.22 \times 10^{3}}{46}{/tex} = 26.5 mol = 26.5 M
For auto ionization, {tex}\left[{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex} = [HCOO-] and [HCOO-] {tex}\left[{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex} = 10-6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [HCOO-] = 10-3
Now, % ionization of HCOOH
= {tex}\frac{\left[{HCOO}^{-}\right] \times 100}{[{HCOOH}]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10^{-3}}{26.5} \times 100{/tex} = 0.004%

questions.question: For the equilibrium, N2 + 3H2 $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NH3, Kc at 1000 K is 2.37 $\times$ 10-3. If at equilibrium [N2] = 2M, [H2] = 3M, the concentration of NH3 is ________.
questions.question_raw: For the equilibrium, N2 + 3H2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3, Kc at 1000 K is 2.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3. If at equilibrium [N2] = 2M, [H2] = 3M, the concentration of NH3 is ________.
questions.explanation:
N2 + 3H2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3
Kc = {tex}\frac{\left[{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[{~N}_{2}\right]\left[{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{/tex}
2.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{[2][3]^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 = 0.12798
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\sqrt{0.12798}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0.358 M

questions.question:
For the following reaction, Kc = 1.3 $\times$ 1022, at 25oC.
CH4(g) + H2O(g) CO(g) + 3H2(g)
Find the value of Kp at the same temperature for the reaction,
$\frac{1}{2} {CO}_{({g})}+\frac{3}{2} {H}_{2({g})} \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} {CH}_{4({g})}+\frac{1}{2} {H}_{2} {O}_{({g})}$
questions.question_raw:
For the following reaction, Kc = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022, at 25oC.
CH4(g) + H2O(g)  CO(g) + 3H2(g)
Find the value of Kp at the same temperature for the reaction,
{tex}\frac{1}{2} {CO}_{({g})}+\frac{3}{2} {H}_{2({g})} \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} {CH}_{4({g})}+\frac{1}{2} {H}_{2} {O}_{({g})}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
CH4 + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CO + 3H2 Kc = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022
Reverse the reaction.
CO + 3H2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH4 + H2O Kc = 7.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23
Divide the above reaction by 2.
{tex}\frac{1}{2} {CO}_{({g})}+\frac{3}{2} {H}_{2({g})} \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} {CH}_{4({g})}+\frac{1}{2} {H}_{2} {O}_{({g})}{/tex}
{tex}K_c^"{/tex} = 8.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
Kp = {tex}K_c^"(RT)^{\triangle n}{/tex} = (8.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12) (0.082 {tex}\times{/tex} 298)-1
Kp = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13

questions.question: An acid solution HX (Ka = 1.4 $\times$ 10-5) contains 0.1 mol of acid per 2.0 litre of solution. What is the percent dissociation of the acid?
questions.question_raw: An acid solution HX (Ka = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) contains 0.1 mol of acid per 2.0 litre of solution. What is the percent dissociation of the acid?
questions.explanation:
Ka = {tex}\frac{{C} \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
Ka = {tex}C\alpha^2{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\alpha << 1{/tex}]
Now, C = {tex}\frac{0.1}{2.0}{/tex} = 0.05 mol/L
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.01659
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percentage dissociation = 1.66%

questions.question: Solid ammonium carbamate (NH2COONH4) decomposes to give ammonia gas and carbon dioxide gas. If we start with 2 moles of the compound and the total pressure at equilibrium is 5.81 $\times$ 10-2 atm, find Kp.
questions.question_raw: Solid ammonium carbamate (NH2COONH4) decomposes to give ammonia gas and carbon dioxide gas. If we start with 2 moles of the compound and the total pressure at equilibrium is 5.81 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 atm, find Kp.
questions.explanation:
NH2COONH4(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g) + CO2(g)
Kp = [NH3]2 [CO2]
Kp = (2x)2(x) = 4x3
Total pressure = 2x + x = 3x = 5.81 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0.0193
Kp = 4(0.0193)3
Kp = 2.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: At 25oC, equilibrium is established in the reversible reaction 2A(g) + B(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2C(g). The equilibrium concentrations are [A] = 0.5 M, [B] = 0.3 M, [C] = 0.4 M. What is the value of Kp for this reaction at 25oC?

Arrange the following in the decreasing order of overall dissociation constant value.

Acid {tex}K_{a_1}{/tex} {tex}K_{a_2}{/tex} {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex}
Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13

questions.explanation:

{tex}{K}_{{a}\left({H}_{3} {PO}_{4}\right)}{/tex} = 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13
= 195.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24

= 249 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21

questions.question: For preparing a buffer solution of pH 4.76 by mixing sodium acetate and acetic acid, the ratio of the concentration of salt and acid should be ________. [Given pKa for acetic acid = 4.76]

pH = pKa + log10{tex}\frac{\text { [Salt] }}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
4.76 = 4.76 + log10{tex}\frac{\text { [Salt] }}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log10{tex}\frac{\text { [Salt] }}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\frac{\text { [Salt] }}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex} = 10o
= 1

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction at equilibrium: Fe3O4(S) + CO(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 3FeO(s) + CO2(g)
What effect will reducing the pressure have on the system?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following reaction at equilibrium: Fe3O4(S) + CO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 3FeO(s) + CO2(g)
What effect will reducing the pressure have on the system?

options.text: Equilibrium will shift to the right.
Equilibrium will shift to the left.
Equilibrium will not shift in any direction.

questions.explanation: On reducing the pressure, equilibrium will shift in the direction that produces more gaseous moles. In this case, both reactant and product sides contain one gaseous mole each. Thus, the equilibrium will not shift in any direction.

questions.question: What is the pH of millimolar solution of ammonium hydroxide which is 10% dissociated?
questions.question_raw: What is the pH of millimolar solution of ammonium hydroxide which is 10% dissociated?
questions.explanation:
C = 1 mM = 10-3 M
For a weak base,
[OH-] = C {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}
[OH-] = 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{10}{100}{/tex}
[OH-] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
pOH = - log10[OH-] = -log10(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)
pOH = 4.0
pH + pOH = 14
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 4 = 10.0

questions.question:
Equilibrium constant for the reaction represented below is 50 at 448oC:
H2(g) + I2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2HI(g)
When 1.0 mole of H2 is mixed with 1.0 mole of I2 in a 0.50 L flask and allowed to react, at the end of the reaction, how many moles of I2 are left unreacted? [Given: $\sqrt{50}$ = 7.07]
questions.question_raw:
Equilibrium constant for the reaction represented below is 50 at 448oC:
H2(g) + I2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2HI(g)
When 1.0 mole of H2 is mixed with 1.0 mole of I2 in a 0.50 L flask and allowed to react, at the end of the reaction, how many moles of I2 are left unreacted? [Given: {tex}\sqrt{50}{/tex} = 7.07]
questions.explanation:
H2(g) + I2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2HI(g)

(M) H2 I2 HI
I 2 2 0
C -x -x +2x
E (2 - x) (2 - x) 2x

Kc = {tex}\frac{[{HI}]^{2}}{\left[{H}_{2}\right]\left[{I}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
Kc = {tex}\frac{(2 x)^{2}}{(2-x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt{50}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x}{2-x}{/tex}
x = 1.56
[I2]eq = 2 - 1.56 = 0.44 M = 0.22 moles
Moles left unreacted = Moles at equilibrium = 0.22 moles

questions.question: The elevation of lake A is 4350 m, lake B is 5486 m and lake C is 2150 m. If water temperatures are the same, which lake has a lower concentration of oxygen gas?

A {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2B

(M) A B

16% of A converted to B

questions.question:
Which one of the following conditions will favour maximum formation of the product in the reaction?
A2(g) + B2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ X2(g), $\Delta_rH$ = -x kJ
questions.question_raw:
Which one of the following conditions will favour maximum formation of the product in the reaction?

questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is negative, so the decrease in temperature will favour the formation of the product. The number of gaseous species decreases, so the increase in pressure will favour the formation of the product. Hence, low temperature and high pressure will favour the maximum formation of the product.

questions.question: Determine the pOH of 0.265 M HCIO solution. Ka of HClO is 2.9 $\times$ 10-8.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 4.057 and pOH = 9.943

questions.question: What happens if we drop some radioactive sugar into a saturated sugar solution of nonradioactive sugar?

Only the solid sugar will show traces of radioactive sugar.
The solution will show no traces of radioactive sugar.
Only the solution will show traces of radioactive sugar.

Kh = {tex}\frac{{K}_{{w}}}{{K}_{{a}}}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
h = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_{h}}{C}}=\sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{-12}}{0.05}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 0.001%

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction at equilibrium:
2ICl(aq) $\rightleftharpoons$ I2(aq) + Cl2(aq)
Initially, [ICl] = 0.050 M, [I2] = 0.035 M and [CI2] = 0.030 M
What is the equilibrium concentration of ICl, if Kc = 0.145?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following reaction at equilibrium:
2ICl(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} I2(aq) + Cl2(aq)
Initially, [ICl] = 0.050 M, [I2] = 0.035 M and [CI2] = 0.030 M
What is the equilibrium concentration of ICl, if Kc = 0.145?
questions.explanation:
2ICl(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} I2(aq) + Cl2(aq)
(M) ICl I3 Cl3
I 0.05 0.035 0.030
C +2x -x -x
E (0.05 + 2x) (0.035 - x) 0.03 - x

Q = {tex}\frac{(0.03)(0.035)}{(0.05)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.42
Since Q > Kc, the reaction will proceed towards reactants.
Now, Kc = {tex}\frac{(0.035-x)(0.03-x)}{(0.05+2 x)^{2}}{/tex}
(0.05 + 2x)2 0.145 = 0.00105 - 0.065x + x2
On solving, we get 0.42x2 - 0.094x + 6.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 = 0
The roots of above quadratic equation is determined using formula,
x = {tex} {-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac} \over 2a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0.216 or 0.007
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0.007
[ICl]eq = 0.05 + 2x
[ICl]eq = 0.064 M

questions.question: For a reaction of the type, AB $\rightleftharpoons$ A + B, the total pressure at which AB gets 50% dissociated will be numerically equal to:
questions.question_raw: For a reaction of the type, AB {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A + B, the total pressure at which AB gets 50% dissociated will be numerically equal to:
questions.explanation:
AB {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A + B
Initial (mol) 1 0 0
Equilibrium (mol) {tex}\frac{50}{100}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{50}{100}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{50}{100}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Total no. of moles = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Let P be the total pressure of the gaseous mixture.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Partial pressure of a component in a mixture = {tex}\frac{\text { no. of mol of component }}{\text { Total no. of mol at equilibrium }} \times \mathrm{P}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pA = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{3}{2}} \times {P}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times {P}=\frac{1}{3} {P}{/tex}
Since pA = pB, so pB = {tex}\frac{1}{3}P{/tex};
pAB = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{3}{2}} \times {P}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times {P}=\frac{1}{3} {P}{/tex}
But KP = {tex}\frac{[{A}][{B}]}{[{AB}]}=\frac{\frac{{P}}{3} \times \frac{{P}}{3}}{\frac{{P}}{3}}=\frac{{P}}{3}{/tex}
P = 3Kp

Ksp = 4S3 = 4(1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)3
Ksp = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12

questions.question:
Which of the following change favors the forward direction?
 N2(g) + O2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NO(g) $\triangle$H = -180.7 kJ (Pressure increased and temperature increased)
 Dissolution of NaOH in water (Temperature increased)
 Water vapour $\rightleftharpoons$ Liquid water (Pressure increased)
 2C(s) + O2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2CO(g) (volume reduced)
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following change favors the forward direction?

  1. N2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g) {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = -180.7 kJ (Pressure increased and temperature increased)
  2. Dissolution of NaOH in water (Temperature increased)
  3. Water vapour {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Liquid water (Pressure increased)
  4. 2C(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2CO(g) (volume reduced)
questions.explanation:
1. Pressure has no effect. An increase of temperature will shift the equilibrium in the backward direction.
  2. Solubility will decrease because it is an exothermic process.
  3. At higher temperatures, vapour will be converted to liquid as it decreases volume. Equilibrium will shift in the forward direction i.e., more liquid water is formed.
  4. On reducing the volume, equilibrium will shift to the side accompanied by a decrease in the number of gaseous moles, i.e., backward direction.

questions.question: 0.16 g of an unknown strong acid HA is dissolved to give 200 mL solution. If the measured pOH of the solution is 11.8, identify the acid. (Choose the closest answer).
questions.question_raw: 0.16 g of an unknown strong acid HA is dissolved to give 200 mL solution. If the measured pOH of the solution is 11.8, identify the acid. (Choose the closest answer).
questions.explanation:
pOH = 11.8
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 2.2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 6.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M = [Acid]
Now, 6.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\frac{mol}{L}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2L
= 1.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol of acid
Molar mass of acid = {tex}\frac{0.16 {g}}{1.26 \times 10^{-3} {mol}}{/tex}
= 126.98 g/mol
This value is closest to the molar mass of HI.

questions.question:

 Dielectric constant of water.

 Temperature of the solution.

  1. Dielectric constant of water.
  2. Electrostatic forces of attraction between the ions
  3. Temperature of the solution.

The extent to which dissociation occurs depends on the temperature of the solution and the extent of solvation of ions produced.

questions.question: MY and NY3, two nearly insoluble salts, have the same Ksp values of 6.2 $\times$ 10-13 at room temperature. Which statement would be TRUE in regard to MY and NY3?

The molar solubility of MY is water is less than that of NY3.
The addition of the salt of KY to solution of MY and NY3 will have no effect on their solubilities.
The salts MY and NY3 are more soluble in 0.5 M KY than in pure water.

questions.explanation:
For MY, x = 1, and y = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = {tex}x^{x} \cdot y^{y} \cdot {S}^{x+y}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} S2 = S2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = (6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13)1/2 = 7.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 mol dm-3
For NY3, x = 1, and y = 3,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = 11 {tex}\times{/tex}x 33 {tex}\times{/tex} S4 = 27S4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\left(\frac{6.2 \times 10^{-13}}{27}\right)^{1 / 4}{/tex} = 3.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol dm-3
Thus, the molar solubility of MY in water is lesser than that of NY3.

questions.question:
Hydrogen and iodine react to give hydrogen iodide in a closed vessel. Which of the following statement regarding chemical equilibrium for this reaction is FALSE?
 Relative concentrations of all species at equilibrium will be the same regardless of whether the starting mixture contained 1 mol H2 and 1 mol I2 or 2 mol HI.
 The reaction is reversible.
 Equilibrium is static in nature.
 Concentration of reactants and products can be changed by altering the temperature and pressure conditions.
questions.question_raw:
Hydrogen and iodine react to give hydrogen iodide in a closed vessel. Which of the following statement regarding chemical equilibrium for this reaction is FALSE?

  1. Relative concentrations of all species at equilibrium will be the same regardless of whether the starting mixture contained 1 mol H2 and 1 mol I2 or 2 mol HI.
  2. The reaction is reversible.
  3. Equilibrium is static in nature.
  4. Concentration of reactants and products can be changed by altering the temperature and pressure conditions.
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is static in nature.

questions.question: Which of the following is the CORRECT order of increasing pH for equimolar solutions of HNO3, KCl, CH3NH3F, KOH and NaC2H3O2? (Ka for HC2H3O2 is 1.80 $\times$ 10-5, Ka for HF is 3 .5 $\times$ 10-4, Kb for CH3NH2 is 4.4 $\times$ 10-5).
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the CORRECT order of increasing pH for equimolar solutions of HNO3, KCl, CH3NH3F, KOH and NaC2H3O2? (Ka for HC2H3O2 is 1.80 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, Ka for HF is 3 .5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4, Kb for CH3NH2 is 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5).
questions.explanation:
HNO3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} strong acid
KCl {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} salt of strong acid and weak base.
Hence, KCl is a neutral salt.
KOH {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} strong base
NaC2H3O2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Salt of strong base and weak acid.
Hence, the salt is basic.
CH3NH3F is a salt of weak base and weak acid.
Hence, Kb of the base and Ka of the acid is compared.
Since Ka(HF) > Kb (CH3NH3), the solution will be acidic.

questions.question:

 Urea conducts electricity in its molten state.
 Degree of ionization for acetic acid is $\approx$ 1.
 Degree of ionization for CuSO4 is <<<< 1.
 Aqueous silver nitrate solution conducts electricity.
questions.question_raw:

  2. Degree of ionization for acetic acid is {tex}\approx{/tex} 1.
  3. Degree of ionization for CuSO4 is <<<< 1.
  4. Aqueous silver nitrate solution conducts electricity.
questions.explanation:
1. Urea is a non-electrolyte. It cannot conduct electricity in molten state.
  2. Acetic acid is a weak electrolyte so {tex}\alpha{/tex} <<<< 1.
  3. CuSO4 is a strong electrolyte, so {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 1.

questions.question: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4 , AgCl, AgBr and Agl are respectively, 1.1 $\times$ 10-12, 1.8 $\times$ 10-10, 5.0 $\times$ 10-13 and 8.3 $\times$ 10-17. questions.question_raw: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4 , AgCl, AgBr and Agl are respectively, 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12, 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10, 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 and 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17. Ksp [Ag+]
Ag2CrO4 [Ag+]2 {tex}[CrO_4^{2-}]{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{{K}_{s {p}} /\left[{CrO}_{4}^{2-}\right]}{/tex}
AgCl [Ag+][Cl-] Ksp/[Cl-]
AgBr [Ag+][Br-] Ksp/[Br-]

questions.question_raw: In a buffer containing 0.25 M NH3 and 0.45 M NH4CI, by how much will the pH change if 0.050 mol HCl is added to 1.00 L of the buffer?
questions.explanation:
pH1 = pKa + log10{tex}\frac{0.25}{0.45}{/tex} = pKa - 0.25
pH2 = pKa + log10{tex}\frac{0.2}{0.5}{/tex} = pKa - 0.39
pH1 - pH2 = pKa - 0.25 - pKa + 0.39 = 0.14
pH2 = pH1 - 0.14

questions.question: How many moles of NaOH can be added to 1.0 L of solution of 0.1 M NH3 and 0.1 M NH4CI without changing pOH by more than one unit? [Given: pKb of NH3 = 4.75]
questions.question_raw: How many moles of NaOH can be added to 1.0 L of solution of 0.1 M NH3 and 0.1 M NH4CI without changing pOH by more than one unit? [Given: pKb of NH3 = 4.75]
questions.explanation:
It is a basic buffer.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = pKb + log10{tex}\frac{[Salt]}{[Base]}{/tex}
= 4.75 + log10{tex}\frac{0.1}{0.1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 4.75
Let x mol of NaOH is added. pH will increase and pOH will decrease.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} New pOH = 4.75 - 1 = 3.75
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3.75 = 4.75 + log10{tex}[\frac{0.1 - x}{0.1 + x}]{/tex}
[On adding strong base, the concentration of weak base will increase and that of salt will decrease]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -1 = log10{tex}[\frac{0.1 - x}{0.1 + x}]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 10-1 = {tex}\frac{0.1 - x}{0.1 + x}{/tex} ... [Taking antilog]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}=\frac{0.1-x}{0.1+x}{/tex}
On solving, 11x = 0.9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{0.9}{11}{/tex} = 0.082 mol

questions.question: The solubility of AgCl(S) with solubility product 1.6 $\times$ 10-10 in 0.1 M NaCl solution would be ________.

questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{\begin{array}{l}Consentration\\(M)\end{array}}{AgCl}\; \rightleftharpoons \;\underset S{Ag}\;+\;\underset{S\;+\;0.1}{Cl_{(aq)}^-}{/tex}
Ksp = [Ag+] [Cl-]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = (S) (S + 0.1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = S {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 ({tex}\because{/tex} S << 0.1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 M

questions.question: Kidney stones are formed by calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and calcium oxalate (CaC2O4). If the total concentration of calcium ions in serum that is in equilibrium with these two solids is 8 $\times$ 10-5 M, and the ratio of the solubility product constant of CaCO3 to that of CaC2O4 is 3, the solubility product of CaCO3 is ________.
questions.question_raw: Kidney stones are formed by calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and calcium oxalate (CaC2O4). If the total concentration of calcium ions in serum that is in equilibrium with these two solids is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M, and the ratio of the solubility product constant of CaCO3 to that of CaC2O4 is 3, the solubility product of CaCO3 is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset X{CaCO_3}\; \rightleftharpoons \;\underset{\underset{(X\;+\;Y)}X}{Ca^{2+}}+\underset{\underset XX}{CO_3^{2-}}{/tex}
{tex}\underset Y{CaC_2O_4}\; \rightleftharpoons \;\underset{\underset{(X\;+\;Y)}Y}{Ca^{2+}}+\underset{\underset YY}{CO_4^{2-}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{K}_{{sp}\left({CaCO}_{3}\right)}}{{K}_{{sp}\left({CaC}_{2} {O}_{4}\right)}}=\frac{{X}}{{Y}}{/tex} = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} X = 3Y
(X + Y) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
= total Ca2+ ion concentration
(3Y + Y) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
Y = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
X = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
{tex}{K}_{{sp}\left({CaCO}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = [Ca2+] {tex}[CO_3^{2-}]{/tex}
= 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
= 48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9

questions.question: Consider the nitration of benzene using mixed cone. H2SO4 and HNO3. If a large amount of KHSO4 is added to the mixture, the rate of nitration will be ________.
questions.question_raw: Consider the nitration of benzene using mixed cone. H2SO4 and HNO3. If a large amount of KHSO4 is added to the mixture, the rate of nitration will be ________.
questions.explanation:
HO - NO2 + 2H2SO4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}2 {HSO}_{4}+{NO}_{2}^{+}+{H}_{3} {O}^{+}{/tex}
Nitric acid
KHSO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} K+ + {tex}HSO_4^-{/tex}
On adding KHSO4, the equilibrium will shift towards the reactant side. This will decrease the {tex}NO_2^+{/tex}concentration. Hence, the rate of nitration will be slower.

questions.question:
Calculate the difference in molar solubility of chromate ion at 298 K from the information given below:
${K}_{\text {sp }\left({A}_{{B} 2} {CrO}_{4}\right)}=1.1 \times 10^{-12}$
${K}_{{sp}\left({BaCro}_{4}\right)}=1.2 \times 10^{-10}$
$\sqrt[3]{0.275}$ = 0.65
$\sqrt{1.2}$ = 1.1
questions.question_raw:
Calculate the difference in molar solubility of chromate ion at 298 K from the information given below:
{tex}{K}_{\text {sp }\left({A}_{{B} 2} {CrO}_{4}\right)}=1.1 \times 10^{-12}{/tex}
{tex}{K}_{{sp}\left({BaCro}_{4}\right)}=1.2 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt[3]{0.275}{/tex} = 0.65
{tex}\sqrt{1.2}{/tex} = 1.1
questions.explanation:
1. {tex}\underset{K_{sp}=4S^3}{Ag_2CrO_4}\;=\;\underset{2S}{2Ag^+}\;+\;\underset S{CrO_4^{2-}}{/tex}
    S = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{1.1 \times 10^{-12}}{4}}{/tex} = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
    {tex}\left[{CrO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex} = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
  2. {tex}{BaCrO_4}\;=\;\underset{S}{Ba^{2+}}\;+\;\underset S{CrO_4^{2-}}{/tex}
    Ksp = S2
    S = {tex}\sqrt{{K}_{{sp}}}=\sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
    Difference = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 - 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
    = 5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M

questions.question: At 25oC, Kb for XOH is 1.0 $\times$ 10-10. 0.01 M solution of XOH has [OH-] = ________.

questions.question: If the equilibrium constant for N2(g) + O2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NO(g) is K, the equilibrium constant for $\frac{1}{2} {N}_{2({g})}+\frac{1}{2} {O}_{2({g})} \rightleftharpoons {NO}_{({g})}$ will be:
questions.question_raw: If the equilibrium constant for N2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g) is K, the equilibrium constant for {tex}\frac{1}{2} {N}_{2({g})}+\frac{1}{2} {O}_{2({g})} \rightleftharpoons {NO}_{({g})}{/tex} will be:
questions.explanation:
For the reaction, N2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g)
K = {tex}\frac{[{NO}]^{2}}{\left[{N}_{2}\right]\left[{O}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
For the reaction, {tex}\frac{1}{2} {N}_{2({g})}+\frac{1}{2} {O}_{2({g})} \rightleftharpoons {NO}_{({g})}{/tex}
K' = {tex}\frac{[{NO}]}{\left[{N}_{2}\right]^\frac {1 }{ 2}\left[{O}_{2}\right]^\frac {1 }{ 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} K' = {tex}K^\frac {1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question:
Consider a 0.1 M Ca2+ ion solution. Identify the CORRECT statement when Ca2+ ions begin to precipitate as Ca(OH)2. Given: Ksp of Ca(OH)2 is 1 $\times$ 10-9.
 Concentration of OH- ion is 10-4 M
 pOH of the solution is 4
 pH of the solution is 10
 pH of the solution is 9
 pOH of the solution is 5
questions.question_raw:
Consider a 0.1 M Ca2+ ion solution. Identify the CORRECT statement when Ca2+ ions begin to precipitate as Ca(OH)2. Given: Ksp of Ca(OH)2 is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9.

  1. Concentration of OH- ion is 10-4 M
  2. pOH of the solution is 4
  3. pH of the solution is 10
  4. pH of the solution is 9
  5. pOH of the solution is 5
questions.explanation:
When Ca(OH)2 starts precipitating,
[Ca2+][OH-]2 = Ksp
[0.1][OH-]2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
[OH-] = 10-4 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log[OH-] = -log(10-4) = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - pOH
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - 4 = 10

questions.question_raw:
For the reaction at 298 K,
A(g) + B(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C(g) + D(g)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}Ho = -29.8 kcal, {tex}\triangle{/tex}So = -0.1 kcal K-1
The value of equilibrium constant is ________.
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\Delta H^{\circ}{/tex} = -29.8 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta S^{\circ}{/tex} = -1.00 kJ K-1
From the equation
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\Delta H^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}T \Delta S^o {/tex} = -29.8 - (298 {tex}\times{/tex} )-0.100))
= -29.8 + 29.8 = 0
Now, {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -2.303 RTlog Keq
0 = -2.303 RT log Keq {tex}\therefore{/tex} Keq = 1.

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction:
A2B4(g) + C2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ A2(g) + 2B2C(l)
Enthalpy of formation of A2B4 is 50.6 kJ/mol and B2C is -285.83 kJ/mol. A2 and C2 are in their elemental forms.
Which of the following changes will shift the equilibrium to the right?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following reaction:
A2B4(g) + C2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A2(g) + 2B2C(l)
Enthalpy of formation of A2B4 is 50.6 kJ/mol and B2C is -285.83 kJ/mol. A2 and C2 are in their elemental forms.
Which of the following changes will shift the equilibrium to the right?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta_{{f}} {H}\left({A}_{2} {B}_{4}\right){/tex} = 50.6 kJ/mol ... {tex}\triangle _f H(A_2){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Delta_{{f}} {H}\left({B}_{2} C\right){/tex} = -285.83 kJ/mol ... {tex}\triangle _f H(C_2){/tex} = 0
{tex}\triangle H_{reaction}{/tex} = 2(-285.83) - (50.6)
{tex}\triangle H_{reaction}{/tex} = -622 kJ/mol
The reaction is exothermic.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} All of the mentioned changes will shift the equilibrium to right.

questions.question:
The binding of oxygen by haemoglobin in blood involves the following equilibrium reaction:
${HbH}_{({aq})}^{+}+{O}_{2({aq})} \rightleftharpoons {HbO}_{2({aq})}+{H}_{({aq})}^{+}$
The pH of blood is normally in the range of 7.35 to 7.45. What will happen to the above equilibrium if pH of the blood is increased?
questions.question_raw:
The binding of oxygen by haemoglobin in blood involves the following equilibrium reaction:
{tex}{HbH}_{({aq})}^{+}+{O}_{2({aq})} \rightleftharpoons {HbO}_{2({aq})}+{H}_{({aq})}^{+}{/tex}
The pH of blood is normally in the range of 7.35 to 7.45. What will happen to the above equilibrium if pH of the blood is increased?

options.text: Fewer oxygen molecules will bind to HbH+.
More oxygenated haemoglobin will be produced.

questions.explanation: As pH of the blood increases, the concentration of H+ decreases. This shifts the equilibrium towards the product side, producing more HbO2.

questions.question: The maximum amount of Zn2+ ions that can be mixed in 16 mL solution of (NH4)2S having molarity = 0.026 M, when no precipitation occurs is ________. [Ksp(ZnS) = 3.54 $\times$ 10-22]
questions.question_raw: The maximum amount of Zn2+ ions that can be mixed in 16 mL solution of (NH4)2S having molarity = 0.026 M, when no precipitation occurs is ________. [Ksp(ZnS) = 3.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22]
questions.explanation:
[(NH4)2S] = 0.026 M
[S2-] = 0.026 M
At equilibrium, [Zn2+] [S2-] = Ksp of ZnS
[Zn2+] = {tex}\frac{3.54 \times 10^{-22}}{0.026}{/tex} = 1.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum amount of Zn2+
= 1.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 mol/L = {tex}\frac{1.36 \times 10^{-20} \times 65 \times 16}{1000}{/tex}
= 1.414 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 g/16mL

questions.question:
Considering the combustion of propane as an equilibrium process, which of the following is TRUE?
 Increasing the temperature produces more water molecules.
 Addition of a catalyst to the system increases the equilibrium constant value.
 Removing heat from the system increases the value of the equilibrium constant.
 Increasing the pressure increases the value of equilibrium constant.
questions.question_raw:
Considering the combustion of propane as an equilibrium process, which of the following is TRUE?

  1. Increasing the temperature produces more water molecules.
  2. Addition of a catalyst to the system increases the equilibrium constant value.
  3. Removing heat from the system increases the value of the equilibrium constant.
  4. Increasing the pressure increases the value of equilibrium constant.
questions.explanation: Combustion of propane is an exothermic process. Assuming that the reaction is at equilibrium, removing heat from this reaction mixture will shift the equilibrium to the right. This will produce more products and thus the value of the equilibrium constant will increase.

questions.question: The dissociation constant (Ka) and the percent of degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) of a weak monobasic acid solution of 0.1 M with a pH = 5, are respectively ________.
questions.question_raw: The dissociation constant (Ka) and the percent of degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) of a weak monobasic acid solution of 0.1 M with a pH = 5, are respectively ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex} {H}^{+}={C}_0 \alpha=\sqrt{{K}_\alpha {C}_0} \Rightarrow 10^{-5}{/tex} {tex}=10^{-1} \times \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha=10^{-4}{/tex}
Also; {tex}{K}_{{a}}={C}_{{o}} \alpha^2=10^{-9}{/tex}
Percentage degree of dissociation {tex}=\alpha \times 100 \%=10^{-2} \%{/tex}

questions.question: The percentage of pyridine (C5H5N) that forms pyridinium ion $(C_5H_5\overset+NH)$ in a 0.10 M aqueous pyridine solution is ________.
questions.question_raw: The percentage of pyridine (C5H5N) that forms pyridinium ion {tex}(C_5H_5\overset+NH){/tex} in a 0.10 M aqueous pyridine solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{Pyridine}{C_5H_5N} + \underset{Water}{H_2O}\rightleftharpoons \underset{\begin{array}{l}Pyridinium\\ion\end{array}}{C_5H_5\overset+NH}\;+\;OH^-{/tex}
Now, Kb = {tex}\frac{C a^{2}}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
= {tex}C\alpha^2{/tex} ... ({tex}\because{/tex} 1 - {tex}\alpha \approx{/tex}1)
The degree of dissociation is given by
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{K}_{{b}}}{{C}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1.7 \times 10^{-9}}{0.10}}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percent dissociation = {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 0.013%
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percentage of pyridine that forms pyridinium ion in the given solution = 0.013%

questions.question: For a triprotic acid (H3A), the acid dissociation constant ($K_{a_2}$) is given by the equation ________.
questions.question_raw: For a triprotic acid (H3A), the acid dissociation constant ({tex}K_{a_2}{/tex}) is given by the equation ________.
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{\left[{H}^{+}\right]\left[{HA}^{2-}\right]}{\left[{H}_{2} {A}^{-}\right]}{/tex}

questions.question: Value of pH of 10-8 M HCl solution is ________.
questions.question_raw: Value of pH of 10-8 M HCl solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
10-8 M indicates a very dilute solution. Thus, H+ of water cannot be ignored. But dissociation of water is suppressed due to the common ion effect.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] {tex}\neq{/tex} 10-7 but less than 10-7.
{tex}H_2O\; \rightleftharpoons \;\underset{10^{-8}\;+\;\alpha}{H^+}+\;\underset\alpha{OH^-}{/tex}
Kw = (10-8 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}){tex}\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-8 + 0.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 = 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 6.9788

questions.question: Two weak acids HA1 and HA2 with identical concentrations have Ka1 = 2.1 $\times$ 10-4 and Ka2 = 1.1 $\times$ 10-5. What is the relative strength of acids?
questions.question_raw: Two weak acids HA1 and HA2 with identical concentrations have Ka1 = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 and Ka2 = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. What is the relative strength of acids?
questions.explanation: Relative strength of weak acids = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{K}_{{al}}}{{K}_{{a} 2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\left(2.1 \times 10^{-4}\right)}{\left(1.1 \times 10^{-5}\right)}} \cong{/tex}4 : 1

questions.question: The dissociation constant for the decompostition of N2O4(g) into NO2(g) at 10 atm and 313 K is ________.
questions.question_raw: The dissociation constant for the decompostition of N2O4(g) into NO2(g) at 10 atm and 313 K is ________.
questions.explanation:
N2O4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g)
Initial (mol) 1 0
Equilibrium (mol) 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}

Total number of moles = 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 2{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}
{tex}{p}_{{N}_{2} {O}_{4}}=\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \times 10{/tex},
{tex}{p}_{{NO}_{2}}=\frac{2 \alpha}{1+\alpha} \times 10{/tex}
Kp = {tex}\frac{\left\{\left[\frac{2 \alpha}{1+\alpha}\right] .10\right\}^{2}}{\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \times 10}=\frac{4 \alpha^{2} \times 10}{(1+\alpha)(1-\alpha)}=\frac{40 \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: 20 litres container at 400 K contain CO2(g) at pressure 0.4 atm and an excess of SrO (neglect the volume of solid SrO). The volume of the container is now decreased by moving the movable piston fitted in the container. The maximum volume of the container, when the pressure of CO2 attains its maximum value, will be ________. (Given that: SrCO3(S) $\rightleftharpoons$ SrO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 1.6 atm)
questions.question_raw: 20 litres container at 400 K contain CO2(g) at pressure 0.4 atm and an excess of SrO (neglect the volume of solid SrO). The volume of the container is now decreased by moving the movable piston fitted in the container. The maximum volume of the container, when the pressure of CO2 attains its maximum value, will be ________. (Given that: SrCO3(S) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} SrO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 1.6 atm)
questions.explanation:
SrCO3(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} SrO(s) + CO2(g)
Kp = {tex}{P}_{{CO}_{2}}{/tex} = 1.6 atm
P1 = 0.4 atm, V1 = 20 L, T1 = 400 K
P2 = Kp = 1.6 atm, V2 = ?, T2 = 400 K
{tex}\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{T_{2}}{/tex}
Since T1 = T2,
V2 = {tex}\frac{{P}_{1} {~V}_{1}}{{P}_{2}}=\frac{0.4 \times 20}{1.6}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V2 = 5L

questions.question: In the process of decomposition of ammonia, what change should be made to the system in order to increase the value of Kp for this reaction?
questions.question_raw: In the process of decomposition of ammonia, what change should be made to the system in order to increase the value of Kp for this reaction?
questions.explanation: Decompostition of ammonia is an endothermic process. For an endothermic reaction, increasing the temperature will cause the equilibrium to move towards the side that consumes heat (i.e. towards products). The value of Kp will thus increase.

questions.question:
Find the INCORRECT statement.
 Higher the electron density on nitrogen, more basic is the amine.
 A compound is basic in nature if its conjugate acid can be stabilized through resonance.
 Basic nature of alkali metal hydroxides increases with the electropositivity of the metal.
 The presence of a negative charge on the atom holding the electron pair decreases the basicity.
questions.question_raw:
Find the INCORRECT statement.

  1. Higher the electron density on nitrogen, more basic is the amine.
  2. A compound is basic in nature if its conjugate acid can be stabilized through resonance.
  3. Basic nature of alkali metal hydroxides increases with the electropositivity of the metal.
  4. The presence of a negative charge on the atom holding the electron pair decreases the basicity.
questions.explanation:
The presence of a negative charge on the atom holding the electron pair increases the basicity, while the presence of a positive charge on the atom holding the electron pair decreases the basicity.
OH- > H2O > H3O+

questions.question:
The equilibrium constants of the following are:
N2 + 3H2 $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NH3; K1
N2 + O2 $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NO; K2
H2 + $\frac{1}{2}$O2 $\rightleftharpoons$ H2O; K3
The equilibrium constant (K) of the reaction:
2NH3 + $\frac{5}{2}$O2 $\overset{k}{\rightleftharpoons}$ 2NO + 3H2O, will be ________.
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constants of the following are:
N2 + 3H2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3; K1
N2 + O2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO; K2
H2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2O; K3
The equilibrium constant (K) of the reaction:
2NH3 + {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\overset{k}{\rightleftharpoons}{/tex} 2NO + 3H2O, will be ________.
questions.explanation:
2NH3 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} N2 + 3H2; {tex}(\frac{1}{K_1}){/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} &3 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ;\left(\mathrm{K}_{3}\right)^{3} \\ &\mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO} \quad ;\left(\mathrm{K}_{2}\right) \\ &\hline 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\ \hline \end{aligned}{/tex}
K = {tex}(\frac{1}{K_1}){/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (K3)3 {tex}\times{/tex} (K2) = {tex}\frac{{K}_{2}\left({~K}_{3}\right)^{3}}{{K}_{{1}}}{/tex}

questions.question: In Haber’s process, if the reaction mixture is enclosed in a cylinder fitted with a piston and the mixture is compressed to half the volume, then Qc = ________.
questions.question_raw: In Haber’s process, if the reaction mixture is enclosed in a cylinder fitted with a piston and the mixture is compressed to half the volume, then Qc = ________.
questions.explanation:
N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g)
When volume is compressed to one-half of the initial volume, the concentration is doubled.
Kc = {tex}\frac{\left[{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[{N}_{2}\right]\left[{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{/tex}
Now, Qc = {tex}\frac{\left[2 {NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[2 {N}_{2}\right]\left[2 \mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}=\frac{4\left[{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{2\left[{N}_{2}\right] \times 8\left[{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Qc = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}Kc

questions.question: Calculate the pH of 0.05 M solution of calcium hydroxide.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the pH of 0.05 M solution of calcium hydroxide.
questions.explanation:
[Ca(OH)2] = 0.05 M
{tex}\underset{0.05}{Ca(OH)_2}\;\; \rightleftharpoons Ca^{2+}\;+\;\underset{2(0.05)\;=\;0.1}{2OH-}{/tex}
pOH = -log[OH-] = -log(0.1) = 1
pH = 14 - 1 = 13

questions.question:
If degree of dissociation is $\alpha$ and total pressure at equilibrium is P for following reaction,
PCI5(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) find the INCORRECT options.
questions.question_raw:
If degree of dissociation is {tex}\alpha{/tex} and total pressure at equilibrium is P for following reaction,
PCI5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) find the INCORRECT options.
questions.explanation:
PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g)
Initial moles 1 0 0
Moles after dissociation 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}

Total number of moles = 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex}
= 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}
The partial pressure (pA) of an individual gas component can be calculated as:
pA = xA {tex}\times{/tex} P
where P = Total pressure of gas mixture and xA is mole fraction of that gas.
{tex}{P}_{{PCl}_{5}}=\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot {P}, {P}_{{Cl}_{3}}=\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot {P}, {P}_{{Cl}_{2}}=\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot {P}{/tex}
Kp = {tex}\frac{P_{{PC} 1} \cdot {P}_{{Cl}_{2}}}{{P}_{{PCl}_{5}}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \cdot {P} \times\left(\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \cdot {P}}{\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \cdot {P}}{/tex}
Kp = {tex}\frac{\alpha^{2} {P}}{1-\alpha^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction:
PCl5(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ PCl3(g) + Cl2(g)
The reaction reaches a point of equilibrium at which [Cl2] = 0.4 M. The concentration of the reactant at equilibrium is 0.1 M more than its initial concentration, and Kc for the reaction is 0.0454, find the initial concentration of PCl5.
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following reaction:
PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g)
The reaction reaches a point of equilibrium at which [Cl2] = 0.4 M. The concentration of the reactant at equilibrium is 0.1 M more than its initial concentration, and Kc for the reaction is 0.0454, find the initial concentration of PCl5.
questions.explanation:
PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g)
(M) PCl5 PCl3 Cl2
I x - 0.1 0.5 0.5
C +0.1 -0.1 -0.1
E x 0.4 0.4

Kc = {tex}\frac{(0.4)(0.4)}{x}{/tex}
0.0454x = 0.16
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3.52
x - 0.1 = 3.42
[PCl5]initial = 3.42 M

questions.question: The reaction between N2 and H2 to form ammonia has Kc = 6 $\times$ 10-2 at the temperature 500oC. The numerical value of Kp for this reaction is ________.
questions.question_raw: The reaction between N2 and H2 to form ammonia has Kc = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 at the temperature 500oC. The numerical value of Kp for this reaction is ________.
questions.explanation:
N2 + 3H2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3
{tex}\triangle n{/tex} = 2 - 4 = -2
500oC = 500 + 273 = 773 K
Kp = Kc(RT){tex}\triangle n{/tex}
Kp = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 773)-2
Kp = {tex}\frac{6 \times 10^{-2}}{(0.0821 \times 773)^{2}}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question:

Br2(g) + Cl2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2BrCl(g)
questions.question_raw:
For the following reaction, calculate the value of Kp, if Kc = 4.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 at 25oC.
Br2(g) + Cl2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2BrCl(g)
questions.explanation:
Kp = Kc(RT){tex}\triangle n{/tex}
= 4.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3(0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 298)o
= 4.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} when {tex}\triangle{/tex}n = 0, Kp = Kc

questions.question: pH of a 100 mL solution is 2.0. When the solution is diluted to 1000 mL, the pH of the resulting solution is ________.
questions.question_raw: pH of a 100 mL solution is 2.0. When the solution is diluted to 1000 mL, the pH of the resulting solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
[H+] in mL = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M ({tex}\because{/tex} pH = 2.0)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] in mL = {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{10}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
pH = -log[H+] = -log(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = 3.0

questions.question: If the value of the equilibrium constant for a particular reaction is 1.6 $\times$ 1012, then at equilibrium the system will contain ________.

questions.explanation: If the equilibrium constant (Kc) is very large (generally if Kc > 103), then the reaction is in favor of products. Hence, for the given reaction, at equilibrium, the system will contain the majority of products.

questions.question: Which of the following lines CORRECTLY show the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant, K, for an exothermic reaction?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following lines CORRECTLY show the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant, K, for an exothermic reaction?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle{/tex}Go = -RT In K
Now, {tex}\triangle{/tex}Go = {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ho - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}So
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ho - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}So = -RT In K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In K = {tex}\frac{\Delta {H}^{\circ}}{{RT}}+\frac{\Delta {S}^{\circ}}{{R}}{/tex}
When the temperature range is small, {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ho and {tex}\triangle{/tex}So can be considered constant, hence the equation gives In K as a linear function of {tex}\frac 1T{/tex}.
In K = {tex}\underbrace{\left(\frac{-\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\right)}_{\text {Slope }} \times \frac{1}{T}+\underbrace{\frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}}_{\text {Intercept }}{/tex}
For an exothermic reaction, the enthalpy change is negative. Therefore, the plot of In K versus 1/T will have a positive slope.
Hence, lines A and B correctly show the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant (K) for an exothermic reaction.

questions.question: For which of the following values of equilibrium constant does the reaction mixture contain mostly reactants?
questions.question_raw: For which of the following values of equilibrium constant does the reaction mixture contain mostly reactants?
questions.explanation: The smaller the value of K, the greater will be the concentration of reactants.

questions.question: The pH of neutral water at 25oC is 7.0. What will be the pH of pure water at 80oC?
questions.question_raw: The pH of neutral water at 25oC is 7.0. What will be the pH of pure water at 80oC?
questions.explanation:
At 25oC the pH of neutral water is 7.0.
The ionic product of water (Kw) increases with increase in temperature.
Hence,
At 25oC, [H+] = [OH-] = 10-7
and Kw = [H+] [OH-] = 10-14
On increasing the temperature, Kw = [H+] [OH-] > 10-14
As [H+] = [OH-]
[H+] > 10-7 M or pH < 7

questions.question: What is the pH of 0.35 M aqueous solution of weak acid (HA) if Ka is 3.5 $\times$ 10-5? [Given, log (3.5) = 0.544]
questions.question_raw: What is the pH of 0.35 M aqueous solution of weak acid (HA) if Ka is 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5? [Given, log (3.5) = 0.544]
questions.explanation:
For weak acid, Ka = {tex}\alpha^2 C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (0.35)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} = 10-4
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 10-2
[H+] = {tex}\alpha C{/tex} = 10-2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 = 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
pH = - log10[H+] = -log10 (3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3)
pH = 2.456

questions.question: The dissociation constant (Ka) and percent of degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) of a weak monobasic acid solution of 0.1 M with a pH = 5, are respectively ________.
questions.question_raw: The dissociation constant (Ka) and percent of degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) of a weak monobasic acid solution of 0.1 M with a pH = 5, are respectively ________.
questions.explanation:
pH = -log10[H+]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = antilog10(-pH)
= antilog10(-5)
[H+] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
A weak monobasic acid (HA) dissociates as,
HA(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}{H}_{({aq})}^{+}+{A}_{({aq})}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}\alpha C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[{H}^{+}\right]}{{C}}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percent of degree of dissociation = {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2%
Ka = {tex}\alpha^2 C{/tex} = (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9

questions.question: Find the CORRECT graph with reference to the value of degree of dissociation ($\alpha$).
questions.question_raw: Find the CORRECT graph with reference to the value of degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}).
questions.explanation:
Strong electrolyte: Degree of ionization for this type of electrolyte is 1, i.e., {tex}\alpha \approx 1{/tex}.
E.g. HCl, H2SO4, NaCl, HNO3, KOH, NaOH, AgNO3, CuSO4.
Weak electrolyte: Degree of ionization for this type of electrolyte is much less than 1, i.e., {tex}\alpha{/tex} <<< 1.
E.g. H2O, HCOOH, CH3COOH, NH4OH, liq.SO2, HCN.

questions.explanation:
= C2H6
 = C5H12
 = CH4
 = C6H14

= 3.59 mol

Formula 
Drug

C17H19NO3

questions.question_raw:

Formula Drug PKa
C17H19NO3 Morphine 9.85
C18H21NO3 Codeine 8.2
C17H21NO4 Codeine 8.85

questions.explanation:
Based on the given structure, the drug used is codeine.
Codeine is a weak base.
pKa = 8.2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pKb = 5.8
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kb = 1.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
[Codeine] = 0.005 M = C
Kb = {tex}\frac{C \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.58 x 10-6 = {tex}\frac{0.005 \alpha^{2}}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.0178
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 8.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
pOH = -log10[OH-] = 4.05
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 9.95

= 5 + {tex}\log \frac{[\text { 0.01}]}{\text { [0.1 }]}{/tex} = 5 + log 10-1 = 5 - 1 = 4

questions.question:
For a reaction at equilibrium
A(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ B(g) + $\frac 12$C(g)
the relation between dissociation constant (K), degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) and equilibrium pressure (p) is given by:
questions.question_raw:
For a reaction at equilibrium
A(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} B(g) + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}C(g)
the relation between dissociation constant (K), degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) and equilibrium pressure (p) is given by:
questions.explanation:
Equilibrium pressure (p) = Pi{tex}\left(1-\alpha+\alpha+\frac{\alpha}{2}\right){/tex}
p = {tex}P_{\mathrm{i}} \times\left(1+\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} PA = {tex}\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\frac{\alpha}{2}}\right) p{/tex}
PB = {tex}\left(\frac{\alpha}{1+\frac{\alpha}{2}}\right){/tex}p; PC = {tex}\left(\frac{\frac{\alpha}{2}}{1+\frac{\alpha}{2}}\right)p{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K=\frac{P_{\mathrm{C}}^{\frac{1}{2}} \times P_{\mathrm{B}}}{P_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K {tex}=\frac{\alpha^{\frac{3}{2}} p^{\frac{1}{2}}}{(2+\alpha)^{\frac{1}{2}}(1-\alpha)}{/tex}

questions.question: The gas phase reaction 2NO2(g) $\longrightarrow$ N2O4(g) is an exothermic reaction. The decomposition of N2O4, in equilibrium mixture of NO2(g) and N2O4(g), can be increased by:

options.text: addition of an inert gas at constant pressure
addition of an inert gas at constant volume

questions.explanation:
Reaction at equilibrium, N2O4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2
According to Le Chatelier's principle-
Addition of an inert gas at constant pressure will increase volume or decrease moles per unit volume. Thus, equilibrium shifts towards more number of molecules.

The equation for the equilibrium constant of the reaction 2NH3(g) + $\frac 52$O2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NO(g) + 3H2O(g), (K4) in terms of K1, K2 and K3 is:

1. N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g), K1
  2. N2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g), K2
  3. H2(g)+ {tex}\frac 12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2O(g), K3,

The equation for the equilibrium constant of the reaction 2NH3(g) + {tex}\frac 52{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g) + 3H2O(g), (K4) in terms of K1, K2 and K3 is:
questions.explanation:
To calculate the value of K4 in the given equation we should apply:
eqn. (ii) + eqn.(iii) {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - eqn. (i)
Hence, K4 = {tex}\frac{K_2 K_3^3}{K_1}{/tex}

questions.question:

 The pH of 1 $\times$ 10-8 M HCl solution is 8.
 $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-}$ is $\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-}$
 Kw increases with increase in temperature.
 When a solution of weak monoprotic acid is titrated against a strong base at half neutralisation point, pH = $\frac 12$pKa
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

  1. The pH of 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 M HCl solution is 8.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-}{/tex} is {tex}\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-}{/tex}

  4. When a solution of weak monoprotic acid is titrated against a strong base at half neutralisation point, pH = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}pKa

  2. Conjugate base of {tex}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-}{/tex} is {tex}\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-}{/tex}
  3. Kw increases with increasing temperature, as the temperature increases, the dissociation of water increases.
  4. At half neutralisation point, half of the acid is present in the form of salt.
    pH = Pka + log{tex}\frac 11{/tex} = Pka

questions.question: Class XII students were asked to prepare one litre of buffer solution of pH 8.26 by their chemistry teacher. The amount of ammonium chloride to be dissolved by the student in 0.2 M ammonia solution to make one litre of the buffer is (Given pKb (NH3) = 4.74; Molar mass of NH3 = 17 g mol-1; Molar mass of NH4Cl = 53.5 g mol-1)
questions.question_raw: Class XII students were asked to prepare one litre of buffer solution of pH 8.26 by their chemistry teacher. The amount of ammonium chloride to be dissolved by the student in 0.2 M ammonia solution to make one litre of the buffer is (Given pKb (NH3) = 4.74; Molar mass of NH3 = 17 g mol-1; Molar mass of NH4Cl = 53.5 g mol-1)
questions.explanation:
pH + pOH = 14
pOH = 14 - 8.26
pOH = pKb + log{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_3\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5.74 = 4.74 + log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]}{0.2} \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]{/tex} = 2M
Molar mass of NH4Cl = 53.5 g mol-1
Amount of NH4Cl = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 53.5 = 107 g

questions.question:
20 mL of 0.1 MNH4OH is mixed with 40 mL of 0.05 MHCl. The pH of the mixture is nearest to:
(Given: Kb (NH4OH) = 1 $\times$ 10-5, log 2 = 0.30, log 3 = 0.48, log 5 = 0.69, log 7 = 0.84, log 11 = 1.04)
questions.question_raw:
20 mL of 0.1 MNH4OH is mixed with 40 mL of 0.05 MHCl. The pH of the mixture is nearest to:
(Given: Kb (NH4OH) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, log 2 = 0.30, log 3 = 0.48, log 5 = 0.69, log 7 = 0.84, log 11 = 1.04)
questions.explanation:
C - concn. of salt, NH4Cl, produced by neutralization of NH4OH and HCl.
NH4Cl, no. of moles = 2m mol = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol
{tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right]=\frac{2 \mathrm{mmol}}{(20+40) \mathrm{ml}}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex}M
pH = {tex}7- \frac 12{/tex} pKb {tex}-\frac 12{/tex}logC
= {tex}7-\frac{1}{2} \times(+5)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{1}{30}\right){/tex} = {tex}7-\frac{5}{2}+\frac{1.48}{2}{/tex} = 5.24

questions.question:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}$ and $K_{a_3}$ are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions (A), (B), and (C).
 H2C2O4 $ \rightleftharpoons $ H+ + $\mathrm{HC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}$
 HC2O4 $ \rightleftharpoons $ H+ + $\mathrm{HC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}$
 H2C2O4 $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2H+ + $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-}$
The relationship between $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}$ and $K_{a_3}$ is given as
questions.question_raw:
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}{/tex} and {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex} are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions (A), (B), and (C).

  1. H2C2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + {tex}\mathrm{HC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}{/tex}
  2. HC2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + {tex}\mathrm{HC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}{/tex}
  3. H2C2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2H+ + {tex}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-}{/tex}

The relationship between {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}{/tex} and {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex} is given as
questions.explanation:
H2C2O4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}{/tex} ...(ii)
H2C2O4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2H+ + {tex}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_3}{/tex} ...(iii)
addition of eq. (i) with eq. (ii) gives eq (iii),
So, {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_3}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}{/tex}

questions.question:
A student needs to prepare a buffer solution of propanoic acid and its sodium salt w ith pH 4. The ratio of $\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}$ required to make buffer is ________
Given: Ka(CH3CH2COOH) =1.3 $\times$ 10-5
questions.question_raw:
A student needs to prepare a buffer solution of propanoic acid and its sodium salt w ith pH 4. The ratio of {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex} required to make buffer is ________
Given: Ka(CH3CH2COOH) =1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
questions.explanation:
According to Henderson-Hasselbalch equation
pH = pKa + log{tex}\frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
4 = 5 - log 1.3 + log{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex} = log 1.3 - 1 = log{tex}\frac{1.3}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log A - log (B) = log{tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{B}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex} = 0.13

questions.question: When the hydrogen ion concentration [H+] changes by a factor of 1000, the value of pH of the solution ________.
questions.question_raw: When the hydrogen ion concentration [H+] changes by a factor of 1000, the value of pH of the solution ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]{/tex} = 1000
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{pH}{/tex} = -log {tex}\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]{/tex} = -log 103
= -3; (-ve) sign indicates decrease in pH.

questions.question: On decreasing the pH from 7 to 2, the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt (MX) of a weak acid (HX) increased from 10-4 mol L-1 to 10-3 mol L-1. The pKa of HX is
questions.question_raw: On decreasing the pH from 7 to 2, the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt (MX) of a weak acid (HX) increased from 10-4 mol L-1 to 10-3 mol L-1. The pKa of HX is
questions.explanation:
Let the solubility of MX at PH 7 = s
{tex}MX(s)\rightleftharpoons\underset{\mathrm S}{\mathrm M^+}+\underset{\mathrm S}{\mathrm X^-}{/tex}
Ksp = s2 ...(i)
Let the solubility of MX at pH = 2 = S1
{tex}MX(s)\rightleftharpoons\underset{{\mathrm S}_1}{\mathrm M^+}+\underset{{\mathrm S}_1}{\mathrm X^-}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HX}]}{\left[\mathrm{X}^{-}\right] ;\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}=\frac{\mathrm{s}_1}{\left[\mathrm{X}^{-}\right] \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{s}_1^2}{10^{-2}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Divide equation (ii) by equation (i)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{s}_1^2}{\mathrm{~s}^2 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{/tex};
{tex}\frac{10^{-3}}{\left(10^{-4}\right)^2 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{/tex}
Ka = 10-4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pKa = 4

questions.question: The Ksp for bismuth sulphide (Bi2S3) is 1.08 $\times$ 10-73. The solubility of Bi2S3 in mol L-1 at 298 K is
questions.question_raw: The Ksp for bismuth sulphide (Bi2S3) is 1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-73. The solubility of Bi2S3 in mol L-1 at 298 K is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{Bi}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3 \rightleftharpoons \underset{2 s}{2 \mathrm{Bi}^{3+}}+\underset{3 s}{3 \mathrm{~S}^{2-}}{/tex}
Ksp = (2s)2 + (3s)3 = 108 (s)5
(s)5 = {tex}\frac{1.08 \times 10^{-73}}{108} \Rightarrow{/tex} s = 10-15

questions.question:
The solubility of AgCN in a buffer solution of pH = 3 is x.
The value of x is:
[Assume: No cyano complex is formed; Ksp(AgCN) = 2.2 $\times$ 10-16 and Ka(HCN) = 6.2 $\times$ 10-10]
questions.question_raw:
The solubility of AgCN in a buffer solution of pH = 3 is x.
The value of x is:
[Assume: No cyano complex is formed; Ksp(AgCN) = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 and Ka(HCN) = 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10]
questions.explanation:
AgCN {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ag+ + CN-
Solubility of AgCN is x.
pH = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]{/tex} = 10-3
HCN {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + CN-
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HCN}]} \Rightarrow \frac{\left[10^{-3}\right]\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HCN}]}{/tex} = 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HCN}]}{/tex} = 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
Each CN- hydrolyses to give one HCN as:
CN- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HCN + OH-
HCN is a weak acid, thus, [HCN] >> [CN-]
x = [Ag+] = [CN-] + [HCN]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = [Ag+] {tex}\approx{/tex} [HCN] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [CN-] = x {tex}\times{/tex} 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
Ksp(AgCN) = [Ag+] [CN-]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 = (x) (x {tex}\times{/tex} 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 3.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question:
The solubility of Ca(OH)2 in water is:
[Given: The solubility product of Ca(OH)2 in water = 5.5 $\times$ 10-6]
questions.question_raw:
The solubility of Ca(OH)2 in water is:
[Given: The solubility product of Ca(OH)2 in water = 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6]
questions.explanation:
Let s be the solubility of Ca(OH)2 in water
Ca(OH)2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\underset{s}{\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}} + \underset{2 s}{2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}}{/tex}
Ksp = [Ca2+][OH-]2 = s {tex}\times{/tex} (2s)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 = 4s3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s3 = {tex}\frac {505}{4}\times10^{-6}{/tex}
s {tex}=\left[\frac{5.5}{4}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}} \times 10^{-2}{/tex} = 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2

questions.question: Which amongst the following is the strongest acid?
questions.question_raw: Which amongst the following is the strongest acid?
questions.explanation:
Due to the resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base, CH(CN)3 is the strongest acid amongst the given compounds.
{tex}\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CN})_3 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\overline{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{CN})_3{/tex}


The conjugate bases of CHBr3 and CHl3 are stabilised by inductive effect of halogens. This is why, they are less stable. Also, the conjugate base of CHCl3 involves back-bonding between 2p and 3p orbitals.

questions.question: Following four solutions are prepared by mixing different volumes of NaOH and HCl of different concentrations, pH of which one of them will be equal to 1?
questions.question_raw: Following four solutions are prepared by mixing different volumes of NaOH and HCl of different concentrations, pH of which one of them will be equal to 1?
questions.explanation:
75 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}HCl + 25 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}NaOH
25 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}NaOH will neutralise 25 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}HCl
75 - 25 = 50 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}HCl will remain.
Total volume will be 75 + 25 = 100 mL
When 50 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex} HCl is diluted to 100 mL, molarity will reduce to half, i.e., {tex}\frac {M}{10}{/tex}.
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=[\mathrm{HCl}]=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
pH = -log10[H+] = -log10{tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} = 1

questions.question:
Which of the following is a Lewis acid?
PH3, NF3, NaH, B(CH3)3
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following is a Lewis acid?
PH3, NF3, NaH, B(CH3)3
questions.explanation: Due to incomplete octet of B, it can act as a Lewis acid.

questions.question: 50 mL of 0.2 M ammonia solution is treated with 25 mL of 0.2 M HCl. If pKb of ammonia solution is 4.75, the pH of the mixture will be:
questions.question_raw: 50 mL of 0.2 M ammonia solution is treated with 25 mL of 0.2 M HCl. If pKb of ammonia solution is 4.75, the pH of the mixture will be:
questions.explanation:
NH3 + HCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NH4Cl
Moles of HCl = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = 0.005
Moles of NH3 = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = 0.01
Excess NH3 = 0.01 - 0.005 = 0.005 moles
1 mole ammonia = 1 mole NH4Cl
0.005 NH3 {tex}\equiv{/tex} 0.005 NH4Cl
Total volume = {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{HCl}}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{NH}_3}{/tex} = 25 + 50 = 75 mL
[NH3] = [NH4Cl] = {tex}\frac{0.005 \text { mole }}{75 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~L}}{/tex} = 0.066 M
pOH = pKb + log{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_3\right]}{/tex}
pOH = 4.75 + log{tex}\frac{[0.066]}{[0.066]}{/tex}
pOH = 4.75
pH = 14 - pOH {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH = 9.25

questions.question:
Consider the following equilibrium
AgCl $\downarrow$ + 2NH3 $\rightleftharpoons$ [Ag (NH3)2]+ + Cl-
White precipitate of AgCl appears on adding which of the following?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following equilibrium
AgCl {tex}\downarrow{/tex} + 2NH3 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} [Ag (NH3)2]+ + Cl-
White precipitate of AgCl appears on adding which of the following?
questions.explanation:
2HNO3(aq) + [Ag(NH3)2] + (aq) + Cl-(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} AgCl(s){tex}\downarrow{/tex} + {tex}2 \mathrm{NH}_4^{+}{/tex}(aq) + {tex}2 \mathrm{NO}_3^{-}{/tex}(aq)
When nitric acid is added to amine solution, solution is made acidic and the complex ion dissociates and liberate silver ion to recombine with chloride ion. This is the conformatory test for silver in group 1.

questions.question: Equimolar solutions of the following compounds are prepared separately in water. Which will have the lowest pH value?
questions.question_raw: Equimolar solutions of the following compounds are prepared separately in water. Which will have the lowest pH value?
questions.explanation:
Metal halide on hydrolysis with water form corresponding hydroxides.
The basic strength of hydroxide increases as we move down in a group.
Hence, Be(OH)2 will have lowest pH.

questions.question: What is the pH of a 10-4 M OH- solution at 330K, if Kw at 330 K is 10-13.6?
questions.question_raw: What is the pH of a 10-4 M OH- solution at 330K, if Kw at 330 K is 10-13.6?
questions.explanation:
Given at 330 K, Kw = 10-13.6
pKw = pH + pOH {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 13.6 = pH + pOH
pOH = -log 10-4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pOH = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 13.6 - 4 = 9.6

questions.question:
Values of dissociation constant, Ka are given as follows:
 Acid
 Ka
 HCN
 6.2 $\times$ 10-10
 HF
 7.2 $\times$ 10-4
 HNO2
 4.0 $\times$ 10-4
Correct order of increasing base strength of the base CN-, F- and $\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$ will be:
questions.question_raw:
Values of dissociation constant, Ka are given as follows:

Acid Ka
HCN 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
HF 7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
HNO2 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4

Correct order of increasing base strength of the base CN-, F- and {tex}\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}{/tex} will be:
questions.explanation:
Higher the value of Ka, higher will be the acidic nature. Further, since CN-, F- and NO2- are conjugate base of the acids HCN, HF and HNO2 respectively, hence the correct order of base strength will be: F- < {tex}\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}{/tex} < CN-
({tex}\because{/tex} stronger the acid, weaker will be its conjugate base)

questions.question: A weak acid HX has the dissociation constant 1 $\times$ 10-5 M. It forms a salt NaX on reaction with alkali. The percentage hydrolysis of 0.1 M solution of NaX is
questions.question_raw: A weak acid HX has the dissociation constant 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M. It forms a salt NaX on reaction with alkali. The percentage hydrolysis of 0.1 M solution of NaX is
questions.explanation:
h = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_w}{K_a \times c}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-5} \times 0.1}}{/tex} = 10-4;
Hence, % hydrolysis = 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 0.01

questions.question: For a concentrated solution of a weak electrolyte (Keq = equilibrium constant) A2B3 of concentration c, the degree of dissociation $\alpha$ is
questions.question_raw: For a concentrated solution of a weak electrolyte (Keq = equilibrium constant) A2B3 of concentration c, the degree of dissociation {tex}\alpha{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{c(1-\alpha)}{{\mathrm A}_2{\;\mathrm B}_3(\mathrm{aq}.)}\rightleftharpoons\underset{2c\alpha}{2{\;\mathrm A}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})}+\underset{3c\alpha}{3{\;\mathrm B}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})}{/tex}
Keq = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{A}^{3+}\right]^2\left[\mathrm{~B}^{2-}\right]^3}{\left[\mathrm{~A}_2 \mathrm{~B}_3\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \mathrm{c}^2 \alpha^2 \times 27 \mathrm{c}^3 \alpha^3}{\mathrm{c}(1-\alpha)}{/tex}, {tex}\alpha{/tex}<< 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Keq = {tex}\frac{108 \mathrm{c}^5 \alpha^5}{\mathrm{c}}{/tex}, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}}{108 \mathrm{c}^4}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}}{/tex}

questions.question: The effect of addition of helium gas to the following reaction in equilibrium state is: PCl5(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ PCl3(g) + Cl2(g)
questions.question_raw: The effect of addition of helium gas to the following reaction in equilibrium state is: PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g)

options.text: addition of helium will not affect the equilibrium.
helium will deactivate PCl5 and reaction will stop.
the equilibrium will shift in the forward direction and more of Cl2 and PCl3 gases will be produced.
the equilibrium will go backward due to suppression of dissociation of PCl5.

questions.explanation: If the volume is kept constant and an inert gas such as He is added which does not take part in the reaction the equilibrium remain undisturbed.

questions.question:
The equilibrium constant for the reversible reaction 2A(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2B(g) + C(g) is K1 and for the reaction $\frac 32$A(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ $\frac 32$B(g) + $\frac 34$C(g) is K2 
K1 and K2 are related as:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constant for the reversible reaction 2A(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2B(g) + C(g) is K1 and for the reaction {tex}\frac 32{/tex}A(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\frac 32{/tex}B(g) + {tex}\frac 34{/tex}C(g) is K2 
K1 and K2 are related as:
questions.explanation:
2A(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2B(g) + C(g) K1 ...(i)
{tex}\frac 32{/tex}A(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\frac 32{/tex}B(g) + {tex}\frac 34{/tex}C(g) K2 ...(ii)
eq. (ii) is {tex}\frac 34{/tex} times of eq. (i), hence, K2 = {tex}\left(K_1\right)^{\frac{3}{4}}{/tex}

questions.question: 200mL of 0.01 MHCl is mixed with 400mL of 0.01 MH2SO4. The pH of the mixture is ________.
questions.question_raw: 200mL of 0.01 MHCl is mixed with 400mL of 0.01 MH2SO4. The pH of the mixture is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}\right)_{\mathrm{HCI}}+\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}\right)_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}}{\text { Total volume }}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left(\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{HCl}} \times \mathrm{n}_{\text {factor }, \mathrm{HCl}} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{HCl}}\right)+\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4} \times \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}}{600}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{(0.01 \times 1 \times 200)+(0.01 \times 2 \times 400)}{600}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2+8}{600}=\frac{10}{600}=\frac{1}{60}{/tex}
pH = -log{tex}\left[\frac{1}{60}\right]{/tex} = 1.78

questions.question:
4.0 moles of argon and 5.0 moles of PCl5 are introduced into an evacuated flask of 100 litre capacity at 610 K. The system is allowed to equilibrate. At equilibrium, the total pressure of mixture was found to be 6.0 atm. The Kp for the reaction is
[Given: R = 0.082 L atm K-1 mol-1]
questions.question_raw:
4.0 moles of argon and 5.0 moles of PCl5 are introduced into an evacuated flask of 100 litre capacity at 610 K. The system is allowed to equilibrate. At equilibrium, the total pressure of mixture was found to be 6.0 atm. The Kp for the reaction is
[Given: R = 0.082 L atm K-1 mol-1]
questions.explanation:
PCl5 = 5 mole; Ar = 4 mole
PTotal = {tex}\frac{9 \times 0.82 \times 610}{100}{/tex} = 4.5 atm

{tex}\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{PCl}_5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}; XAO = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{PCl}_5}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{5 \times 4.5}{9}{/tex} = 2.5 atm; PAr = {tex}\frac{4 \times 4.5}{9}{/tex} = 2 atm
{tex}\underset{2.5-P}{{\mathrm{PCl}}_5}\rightleftharpoons\underset P{{\mathrm{PCl}}_3}+\underset P{{\mathrm{Cl}}_2}{/tex}
Ptotal = 2.5 - P + P + P +PAr = 6 atm
P = 1.5 atm
KP = {tex}\frac{1.5 \times 1.5}{1}{/tex} = 2.25
