questions.question:
A weak acid, HA, has a Ka, of 1.00 $\times$ 10-5. If 0.100 mole of this acid is dissolved in one litre of water, the
percentage of acid dissociated at equilibrium is closest to
questions.question_raw:
A weak acid, HA, has a Ka, of 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. If 0.100 mole of this acid is dissolved in one litre of water, the
percentage of acid dissociated at equilibrium is closest to
questions.explanation:
Ka = 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}=\frac{0.1 x \times 0.1 x}{0.1-x}{/tex}
Assume x < < 0.1 
1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}=\frac{x^2 \times 0.01}{0.1} \Rightarrow x^2{/tex}  = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
Percentage of acid dissociated
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1.0%

questions.question: In which of the following, the solubility of AgCl will be minimum?

{tex} AgCl \rightleftharpoons Ag ^{+}+ Cl ^{-}{/tex}
{tex}NaCl \rightleftharpoons Na ^{+}+ Cl ^{-}{/tex}
Thus, due to common ion, Cl- the equilibrium of dissociation of AgCl shifts in backward direction which decreases its solubility.

{tex}H _2 X \rightleftharpoons HX ^{-}+ H ^{+}, K a_1{/tex}
{tex}HX ^{-} \rightleftharpoons X ^{2-}+ H ^{+}, Ka _2{/tex}
In first reaction, H+ comes from a neutral atom while in second H+ comes from an anion which is difficult as compared to first reaction thus,
Ka1 > Ka2 or Ka2 < Ka1.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about pH and H+ ion concentration is incorrect?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements about pH and H+ ion concentration is incorrect?

options.text: A solution of the maximum of one equivalent of each of CH3COOH and NaOH has a pH of 7.
pH of pure neutral water is not zero.
A cold and concentrated H2SO4 has lower H+ ion concentration than a dilute solution of H2SO4
Addition of one drop of concentrated HCI in NH4OH solution decreases pH of the solution.

questions.explanation: HCI is a strong acid and NH4OH is a weak base thus, on addition of HCI to NH4OH solution, concentration of H+ increases and pH decreases according to the equation, pH = - log[H+]. CH3COOH is a weak acid and NaOH is a strong base thus, in a solution containing 1 equivalent of each there are more OH- ions than H+ ions due to incomplete ionization of CH3COOH hence, pH is more than 7. Thus, the statement is incorrect. pH value of pure neutral water is not zero but it has a value of 7. A cold and concentrated H2SO4 has lower H+ ion concentration because in this condition it exists mostly in unionizable form while when diluted it completely ionizes to give H+ ions.

questions.question: If $\alpha$ is the fraction of HI dissociated at equilibrium in the reactions, 2HI(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ H2(g) + I2(g) starting with the 2 moles of HI, then the total number of moles of reactants and products at equilibrium are:

Total number of moles at equilibrium
= 2 - 2{tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2

questions.question:
Solid ammonium carbamate dissociated according to the given reaction
NH2COONH4 (s) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NH3(g) + CO2(g)
Total pressure of the gases in equilibrium is 5 atm. Hence Kp
questions.question_raw:
Solid ammonium carbamate dissociated according to the given reaction
NH2COONH4 (s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g) + CO2(g)
Total pressure of the gases in equilibrium is 5 atm. Hence Kp
questions.explanation:
NH2COONH4(s) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} {tex}\underset{2\mathrm P}{2{\mathrm{NH}}_3(\mathrm g)}\;+\;\underset{\mathrm P}{{\mathrm{CO}}_2}(\mathrm g){/tex}
Total pressure = 2P = P = 3P = 5 atm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = {tex}\frac 53{/tex} atm
Kp = {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_2} \times \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{NH}_3}^2{/tex}
= {tex}\frac 53{/tex}atm {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{2 \times 5}{3} \mathrm{~atm}\right){/tex} = 18.51

questions.question: In the equilibrium SO2Cl2(g) $\leftrightharpoons$ SO2(g) + Cl2(g) at 2000 K and 10 atm pressure, % Cl2 = % SO2 = 40 (by volume) Then
questions.question_raw: In the equilibrium SO2Cl2(g) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} SO2(g) + Cl2(g) at 2000 K and 10 atm pressure, % Cl2 = % SO2 = 40 (by volume) Then
questions.explanation:
SO2Cl2 {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} SO2 + Cl2
KP = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{SO}_2} \times \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}_2}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_2} \mathrm{Cl}_2}{/tex}
Given: {tex}\frac{\text V_{\mathrm{Cl}_2}}{\text V_{\text {total }}}{/tex} = 0.4
or, {tex}\frac{\frac{\mathbf{n}_{\mathrm{Cl}_2} \times \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{p}}}{\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\text {total }} \times \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{p}}}{/tex} = 0.4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathbf{n}_{\mathrm{Cl}_2}}{\mathbf{n}_{\text {total }}}{/tex} = 0.4
Hence, {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{Cl}_2}{/tex} = 0.4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\text {Cl}_2}{/tex} = {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{Cl}_2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Ptotal = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 atm = 4 atm
{tex}\mathrm{P}_{\text {SO}_2}{/tex} = 4 atm
and {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\text {SO}_2}{_{\text {Cl}}}_2{/tex} = 2 atm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kp = {tex}\frac {4\times 4}{2}{/tex} atm = 8 atm

questions.question:
The value of Kp for the reaction
2SO2 (g) + O2 (g) $\leftrightharpoons$ 2SO3 (g) is 5.

2SO2 (g) + O2 (g) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} 2SO3 (g) is 5.
What will be the partial pressure of O2 at equilibrium when equal moles of SO2 and SO3 are present at equilibrium?
questions.explanation:
2SO2 + O2 {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} 2SO3
Kp = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_3}^2}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_3}^2 \times \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{/tex} = 5
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{n}_{\mathrm{SO}_2}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{SO}_3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{SO}_2}{/tex} = {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{SO}_3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_2}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_2}{/tex} = 0.2

questions.question: The number of hydrogen ions in 10 ml of a solution with pH = 13 is
questions.question_raw: The number of hydrogen ions in 10 ml of a solution with pH = 13 is

questions.question: The pKb of dimethylamine and pKa of acetic acid are 3.27 and 4.77 respectively at T (K). The correct option for the pH of dimethylammonium acetate solution is

questions.question: Consider the nitration of benzene using mixed cone. H2SO4 and HNO3. If a large amount of KHSO4 is added to the mixture, the rate of nitration will be:
questions.question_raw: Consider the nitration of benzene using mixed cone. H2SO4 and HNO3. If a large amount of KHSO4 is added to the mixture, the rate of nitration will be:

questions.question: In which of the following equilibrium KC and KP are not equal?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following equilibrium KC and KP are not equal?
questions.explanation:
As we know, Kp = Kc, {tex}\times{/tex} (RT){tex}\Delta{/tex}ng
So, for reaction having same number of gaseous moles on reactants and products side will have same value of Kc and Kp otherwise their values are different.
For reaction, {tex}2 NO _{(g)} \rightleftharpoons N _{2(g)}+ O _{2(g)}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 2 - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Kp = Kc {tex}\times{/tex}(RT)0
Kp = Kc
For reaction, {tex}SO _{2(g)}+ NO _{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons SO _{3(g)}+ NO _{(g)}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 2 - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Kp = Kc
For reaction, {tex}SO _{2(g)}+ I _{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 HI _{(g)}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta n_g{/tex} = 2 - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Kp = Kc
For reaction, {tex}2C _{(s)}+ O _{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 CO _{2(g)}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta n_g{/tex} = 2 - 3 = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Kp = Kc {tex}\times{/tex}(RT)-1
Thus, in this reaction Kp {tex}\ne{/tex} Kc

questions.question: For a reversible reaction, if the concentrations of the reactants are doubled, the equilibrium constant will be
questions.question_raw: For a reversible reaction, if the concentrations of the reactants are doubled, the equilibrium constant will be
questions.explanation: Equilibrium constant of a reaction is independent of the concentration of species involved in the reaction but dependent only on the temperature if the concentration of any species increases then concentration of other species changes accordingly to keep the value of equilibrium constant same.

questions.question:
The equilibrium constant for the reaction.
$A _2 \rightleftharpoons 2 A$
at 500 K and 700 K are 1 $\times$ 10-10 and 1 $\times$ 10-5. The given reaction is:
questions.question_raw:
The equilibrium constant for the reaction.
{tex}A _2 \rightleftharpoons 2 A{/tex}
at 500 K and 700 K are 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 and 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. The given reaction is:
questions.explanation:
Since the equilibrium constant increases with temperature (from {tex}10^{-10}{/tex} at 500 K to {tex}10^{-5}{/tex} at 700 K), the forward reaction is favored at higher temperature.
By Le Chatelier's Principle, this means the reaction absorbs heat, so it is endothermic.

questions.question: The rate constant for forward and backward reactions of hydrolysis of ester are 1.1 $\times$ 10-2 and 1.5 $\times$ 10-3 per minute. Equilibrium constant for the reaction, CH3COOC2H5 + H+ $ \rightleftharpoons$ CH3COOH + C2H5OH is:
questions.question_raw: The rate constant for forward and backward reactions of hydrolysis of ester are 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 and 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 per minute. Equilibrium constant for the reaction, CH3COOC2H5 + H+ {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COOH + C2H5OH is:
questions.explanation:
Equilibrium constant
{tex}=\frac{\text { Rate constant of forward reaction }}{\text { Rate constant of backward reaction }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.1 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}=7.33{/tex}

questions.question: The rate of reaction depends upon the:
questions.question_raw: The rate of reaction depends upon the:
questions.explanation:
According to law of mass action, the rate of a reaction depends upon the active masses or molar concentration of reactants.
Rate of reaction {tex}\propto{/tex} Concentration of reactants

questions.question: Aqueous solution of which of the following compounds is the best conductor of electric current?
questions.question_raw: Aqueous solution of which of the following compounds is the best conductor of electric current?
questions.explanation:
The strong electrolyte which furnishes ions completely in its aqueous solution will be the best conductor of electricity in aqueous solution. Fructose and ammonia are not an electrolyte while acetic acid is a weak electrolyte and HCl is the strong electrolyte
which completely furnishes H+ and CI- ions in aqueous solution thus, HCl is the best conductor of electricity in aqueous solution.

questions.question: Aqueous solution of which of the following boron compounds will be strongly basic nature?
questions.question_raw: Aqueous solution of which of the following boron compounds will be strongly basic nature?
questions.explanation:
{tex}{Na}_{2} {~B}_{4} {O}_{7}{/tex} gives {tex}{H}_{3} {BO}_{3}{/tex} and NaOH in water.

An aqueous solution of borax {tex}{Na}_{2} {~B}_{4} {O}_{7}{/tex} is basic in nature as it on hydrolysis produces {tex}{H}_{3} {BO}_{3}{/tex} (a weak acid) and NaOH (a strong base).

questions.question:
If equal volumes of ${AB}_{2}$ and XY (both are salts) aqueous solutions are mixed, which of the following combination will give a precipitate of ${AY}_{2}$ at 300 K?
[Given ${K}_{{sp}}$ (at 300 K) for ${AY}_{2}=5.2 \times 10^{-7}$]
questions.question_raw:
If equal volumes of {tex}{AB}_{2}{/tex} and XY (both are salts) aqueous solutions are mixed, which of the following combination will give a precipitate of {tex}{AY}_{2}{/tex} at 300 K?
[Given {tex}{K}_{{sp}}{/tex} (at 300 K) for {tex}{AY}_{2}=5.2 \times 10^{-7}{/tex}]
questions.explanation:
{tex}{AY}_{2} \rightleftharpoons {~A}^{2+}+2 {Y}^{-}{/tex}
Here, if {tex}K_{i p} \gt K_{s p}{/tex}, then the precipitation of {tex}{AY}_{2}{/tex} takes place.

  1. {tex}{A}^{2+}=3.6 \times 10^{-3} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}K_{i p} =\left[3.6 \times 10^{-3}\right]\left[5 \times 10^{-4}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{\left[{Y}^{-}\right] } =5 \times 10^{-4} {M} \text { shift }{/tex}
    {tex}K_{i p} =9 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
    So, when {tex}K_{i p} \lt K_{s p}{/tex}, no precipitate is formed.
  2. {tex}{\left[{A}^{2+}\right] } =2 \times 10^{-4} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =\left[2 \times 10^{-4}\right]\left[0.8 \times 10^{-3}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{\left[{Y}^{-}\right] } =0.8 \times 10^{-3} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =1.28 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
    So, when {tex}k_{i p} \lt k_{s p^{\prime}}{/tex} no precipitate is formed.
  3. {tex}{A}^{2+} =2 \times 10^{-2} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =\left[2 \times 10^{-2}\right]\left[2 \times 10^{-2}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{\left[{Y}^{-}\right] } =2 \times 10^{-2} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =8 \times 10^{-6} {M}{/tex}
    So, when {tex}k_{i p} \lt k_{s p}{/tex}, precipitate is formed.
  4. {tex}{A}^{2+} =1.5 \times 10^{-4} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}{Y}^{-} =1.5 \times 10^{-3} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =\left[1.5 \times 10^{-4}\right]\left[1.5 \times 10^{-3}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=3.375 \times 10^{-10} {M}{/tex}
    So, when {tex}k_{i p} \lt k_{s p}{/tex}, no precipitate is formed.

questions.question: Incorrect statement for the use of indicators in acid - base titration is:
questions.question_raw: Incorrect statement for the use of indicators in acid - base titration is:

options.text: Phenolphthalein is a suitable indicator for a weak acid vs strong base titration.
Methyl orange is a suitable indicator for a strong acid vs weak base titration.
Methyl orange may be used for a weak acid vs weak base titration.
Phenolphthalein may be used for a strong acid vs strong base titration.

questions.explanation: Methyl orange is a suitable indicator for a strong acid vs strong base titration, not for a weak acid vs weak base titration. In a weak acid vs weak base titration, the pH at the equivalence point is typically around 7, which is within the pH range where methyl orange undergoes a color change. Therefore, methyl orange is not suitable for use in weak acid vs weak base titrations.
